name: AP Biology
description: Master every concept tested on the AP Biology exam — from molecular chemistry to ecosystem ecology. Aligned to the College Board Course and Exam Description.

acceptableAlternatives: polar due to oxygen's greater electronegativity
polar molecule with partial negative on O and partial positive on H
facts.quizQuestion: What is water polarity?
facts.explanation: Oxygen's higher electronegativity pulls electron density away from the hydrogen atoms, creating a partial negative charge (δ−) on oxygen and partial positive charges (δ+) on each hydrogen. This uneven charge distribution makes water a polar molecule, which underlies nearly all of its unique biological properties.
facts.visualDescription: Water molecule with color-coded partial charges — red oxygen (δ−) and white hydrogens (δ+)
distractors: A nonpolar molecule because H and O share electrons equally
An ionic compound formed by the transfer of electrons
A neutral molecule with no charge differences between atoms
Polar because hydrogen is more electronegative than oxygen
Nonpolar because the molecule is symmetric
A charged ion with a full negative charge on oxygen
Polar due to nitrogen forming the negative pole
A molecule that dissociates completely in solution

acceptableAlternatives: weak attraction between δ+ hydrogen and an electronegative atom
electrostatic attraction between partial charges on H and N/O/F
facts.quizQuestion: What type of weak bond forms between water molecules due to partial charges on H, N, O, or F atoms?
facts.explanation: Hydrogen bonds form when a hydrogen atom covalently bonded to an electronegative atom (like O or N) is attracted to another electronegative atom. Though individually weak, hydrogen bonds are collectively responsible for water's cohesion, high specific heat, and the structure of DNA and proteins.
facts.visualDescription: Dashed line between two water molecules showing H-bond between O and H

acceptableAlternatives: water's attraction to other water molecules via hydrogen bonds
hydrogen bonding between water molecules that creates surface tension
facts.quizQuestion: What property causes water molecules to be attracted to each other via hydrogen bonds, producing surface tension?
facts.explanation: Cohesion occurs because each water molecule can form up to four hydrogen bonds with neighboring water molecules, creating a strong network. This property allows water to form droplets, supports surface tension (letting insects walk on water), and helps pull water up through plant xylem.
facts.visualDescription: Water droplet beading on a leaf surface, showing cohesive forces

acceptableAlternatives: water's attraction to other polar surfaces via hydrogen bonds
attraction between water and charged surfaces like plant cell walls
facts.quizQuestion: What property describes water molecules being attracted to other polar or charged surfaces, enabling capillary action?
facts.explanation: Adhesion occurs when water molecules form hydrogen bonds with polar or charged surfaces such as glass or plant cell walls. In plant xylem, adhesion to the vessel walls works alongside cohesion to pull water upward against gravity in a continuous column.
facts.visualDescription: Water creeping up a narrow glass tube — meniscus and capillary action

acceptableAlternatives: caused by cohesion among surface water molecules
cohesive forces between water molecules at the air-water interface
facts.quizQuestion: What emergent property of water, arising from cohesive hydrogen bonds at the water-air interface, allows insects to walk on water?
facts.explanation: Surface water molecules cannot form hydrogen bonds above them (only air), so they form stronger bonds sideways and downward, creating an elastic-like skin. This is why small insects like water striders can walk on water and why water forms beads on a waxed surface.
facts.visualDescription: Water strider insect resting on pond surface without breaking it

acceptableAlternatives: upward movement of water in narrow spaces due to adhesion
water rising in a narrow tube because adhesion to walls overcomes gravity
facts.quizQuestion: What is the upward movement of water through narrow tubes due to adhesion to vessel walls, critical for water transport in plant xylem?
facts.explanation: Capillary action results from adhesion (water sticking to tube walls) and cohesion (water pulling itself up). In plants, it helps draw water from roots to leaves through xylem vessels, supplementing the main driving force of transpiration pull.
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section of plant xylem vessel showing water column rising

facts.quizQuestion: Water has a specific heat capacity of {___} J/(g·°C).
facts.explanation: Water's specific heat of 4.184 J/(g·°C) is among the highest of any common substance because hydrogen bonds must be broken before temperature rises. This allows oceans and organisms to resist temperature swings that would be lethal to living cells.
facts.visualDescription: Thermometer in ocean water showing stable temperature vs. sand heating rapidly

acceptableAlternatives: buffers temperature changes in organisms and ecosystems
prevents rapid temperature swings due to energy absorbed by hydrogen bonds
facts.quizQuestion: What is the biological significance of water's high specific heat capacity?
facts.explanation: Because water requires a large amount of heat energy to raise its temperature, it acts as a thermal buffer. Organisms that are largely made of water maintain more stable internal temperatures, and large bodies of water moderate regional climates near coastlines.
facts.visualDescription: Coastal city with mild climate next to the ocean versus arid inland desert
distractors: Allows water to evaporate rapidly, cooling organisms quickly
Enables water to dissolve more substances than any other liquid
Causes ice to float, insulating aquatic organisms in winter
Allows water to resist changes in pH when acids are added
Makes water denser than ice, so lakes freeze from the bottom up
Causes water molecules to repel nonpolar molecules into clusters
Gives water its high surface tension that allows insects to walk on it
Allows water to conduct electricity when ions are dissolved

facts.quizQuestion: Water's heat of vaporization is approximately {___} J/g at 100°C.
facts.explanation: Converting 1 gram of liquid water to steam requires 2,260 J because all remaining hydrogen bonds must be broken. This high value means evaporating even a small amount of sweat or water from a leaf removes substantial heat from the organism.
facts.visualDescription: Sweat evaporating from skin surface carrying heat away

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the process by which water carries heat away from a surface as it evaporates, enabling sweating and transpiration to cool organisms?
facts.explanation: When high-energy water molecules escape from a liquid surface as vapor, they carry that kinetic energy away, lowering the average energy (and temperature) of the remaining liquid. This is why sweating cools mammals and transpiration cools leaves on hot days.
facts.visualDescription: Person sweating during exercise with visible evaporation arrows

acceptableAlternatives: ice floats because its hydrogen bond lattice is less dense than liquid water
hydrogen bonds in ice create a rigid open structure that is less dense than liquid water
facts.quizQuestion: What unusual property causes ice to be less dense than liquid water, because hydrogen bonds form an open lattice spacing molecules apart?
facts.explanation: In liquid water, hydrogen bonds constantly break and reform, allowing molecules to pack closer together. When water freezes, molecules lock into a hexagonal lattice with each molecule hydrogen-bonded to four others, creating more space between molecules than in the liquid state.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side diagrams: disordered liquid water vs. ordered hexagonal ice lattice

facts.correctAnswer: Ice floats; insulates liquid water below
acceptableAlternatives: ice floats and insulates the liquid water below, protecting aquatic organisms
floating ice prevents lakes from freezing solid so fish and other life can survive
facts.quizQuestion: What is the biological significance of ice being less dense than liquid water?
facts.explanation: If ice sank, lakes and ponds would freeze from the bottom up, killing all aquatic life. Instead, floating ice creates an insulating barrier that maintains liquid water underneath, where aquatic organisms can survive even in freezing air temperatures.
facts.visualDescription: Frozen lake with ice layer on top and fish swimming in liquid water below
distractors: Ice floating causes evaporation to increase, humidifying surrounding air for land organisms
Ice sinking would warm deep ocean water by releasing heat, driving ocean circulation
Floating ice reflects sunlight, preventing overheating of aquatic ecosystems
Ice floating allows it to be pushed to shore, exposing open water for organisms
If ice sank, deeper water would be oxygenated, benefiting aerobic organisms
Ice floating creates habitat for polar bears and seals on its surface
Floating ice helps mix nutrients between surface and deep water layers
If ice floated it would not matter because aquatic organisms are cold-tolerant

acceptableAlternatives: polar water surrounds and isolates ions and polar molecules, dissolving them
water's polarity allows it to form hydration shells around solutes, making it the universal solvent
facts.quizQuestion: What property of water allows it to dissolve more substances than any other common solvent by surrounding ions with hydration shells?
facts.explanation: Water's polar nature allows it to surround ions and polar molecules with oriented hydration shells, disrupting the interactions that held them together and pulling them into solution. This property is essential for blood, cytoplasm, and every other aqueous biological fluid.
facts.visualDescription: Water molecules surrounding a sodium ion with partial charges oriented toward the ion
distractors: Provides electrons for dissolving covalent bonds in all organic molecules
Acts as a catalyst that breaks down solutes into smaller ions
Dissolves all substances including nonpolar fats and oils equally well
Uses its high specific heat to absorb solute molecules and stabilize them
Forms covalent bonds with solute molecules to keep them in solution
Dissolves substances by heating them past their melting points
Dissolves ionic compounds only, not polar molecular compounds
Breaks peptide bonds in proteins to keep amino acids dissolved

acceptableAlternatives: nonpolar molecules aggregate in water because they cannot form hydrogen bonds with it
hydrophobic molecules cluster away from water, driven by water's tendency to maximize hydrogen bonding
facts.quizQuestion: What phenomenon causes nonpolar molecules to cluster together to minimize contact with water, driving membrane and protein folding?
facts.explanation: Nonpolar molecules cannot participate in hydrogen bonding, so water molecules around them must form a more ordered cage-like arrangement — an energetically unfavorable state. Clustering nonpolar molecules together minimizes the amount of organized water, which increases entropy.
facts.visualDescription: Oil droplets in water clustering together, showing hydrophobic aggregation
distractors: Ionic molecules repel water and are forced to cluster by electromagnetic forces
Nonpolar molecules form strong covalent bonds with each other, excluding water
Polar molecules aggregate when water molecules push them together by surface tension
Hydrophobic molecules actively repel water molecules through charge-charge interactions
Nonpolar molecules dissolve poorly in water because water prefers to bond with ions only
Hydrophobic molecules are excluded from water because they are denser and sink
Nonpolar molecules cluster because they attract water molecules and must compete for them
Hydrophobic interactions are driven by covalent bond formation between nonpolar molecules

facts.quizQuestion: The pH scale ranges from 0 to {___}.
facts.explanation: The pH scale is a logarithmic scale where pH = −log[H⁺]. A difference of 1 pH unit represents a 10-fold change in hydrogen ion concentration. Pure water at 25°C has a pH of 7 (neutral), values below 7 are acidic, and values above 7 are basic.
facts.visualDescription: pH scale strip from 0 (red/acidic) to 14 (blue/basic) with 7 marked as neutral

facts.quizQuestion: Pure water at 25°C has a neutral pH of {___}.
facts.explanation: At pH 7, the concentration of H⁺ ions equals the concentration of OH⁻ ions (10⁻⁷ mol/L each), making the solution neither acidic nor basic. Blood must be maintained between pH 7.35 and 7.45 — even small deviations can be life-threatening.
facts.visualDescription: Beaker of pure water with pH meter reading exactly 7.0

acceptableAlternatives: resists pH changes by using a weak acid/conjugate base pair
weak acid-conjugate base system that stabilizes pH
facts.quizQuestion: What type of solution resists pH changes when small amounts of acid or base are added, using a weak acid and its conjugate base?
facts.explanation: Buffers work by donating protons (H⁺) when a base is added and accepting protons when an acid is added, keeping [H⁺] approximately constant. The bicarbonate buffer system (HCO₃⁻/CO₂) is the primary pH stabilizer in human blood.
facts.visualDescription: Graph showing blood pH staying flat while unbuffered solution pH drops sharply when acid is added

facts.correctAnswer: C, H, N, O, P, S
acceptableAlternatives: C, H, N, O, P, S — six elements making up ~98% of living matter
the six elements: carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, sulfur
facts.quizQuestion: What does the acronym CHNOPS represent in biology?
facts.explanation: These six elements form the backbone of all biological molecules: carbon and hydrogen in organic frameworks, oxygen and nitrogen in functional groups, phosphorus in ATP and DNA, and sulfur in some amino acids. Together they make up approximately 98% of the mass of living organisms.
facts.visualDescription: Periodic table with the six CHNOPS elements highlighted in different colors
distractors: C, H, O, N, Ca, K
C, H, N, O, Fe, Mg
C, H, O, P, Ca, Na
H, O, N, K, Ca, Cl
C, O, N, S, Fe, Zn
C, H, N, P, K, Na
C, H, O, N, Mg, S
H, O, C, Cl, Ca, P

facts.quizQuestion: The CHNOPS acronym represents {___} elements that make up ~98% of living matter.
facts.explanation: The six CHNOPS elements — Carbon, Hydrogen, Nitrogen, Oxygen, Phosphorus, and Sulfur — form virtually all biological molecules. The remaining ~2% consists of trace elements like iron, calcium, zinc, and iodine that are nonetheless essential.
facts.visualDescription: CHNOPS acronym displayed as a molecular building block diagram

facts.quizQuestion: Carbon can form {___} covalent bonds simultaneously, making it the backbone of all organic molecules.
facts.explanation: Carbon has four valence electrons and needs four more to complete its outer shell, so it forms four covalent bonds. This tetravalence allows carbon to build straight chains, branched chains, rings, and double bonds — creating the enormous structural diversity of organic molecules.
facts.visualDescription: Carbon atom diagram showing four bonds radiating outward in a tetrahedral arrangement

facts.correctAnswer: Oxygen binding in hemoglobin's heme group
acceptableAlternatives: essential for hemoglobin oxygen transport via the heme group
iron in heme group carries oxygen in red blood cells
facts.quizQuestion: What is the biological role of iron (Fe) in the human body?
facts.explanation: Iron is a trace element central to hemoglobin function. The Fe²⁺ ion in each heme group can reversibly bind one oxygen molecule, allowing hemoglobin to carry four O₂ molecules per protein. Iron deficiency causes anemia because less oxygen can be transported.
facts.visualDescription: Hemoglobin protein with four red heme groups highlighted, each containing an iron atom
distractors: Forms the backbone of ATP molecules that store cellular energy
Regulates thyroid hormone synthesis and metabolic rate
Strengthens bone and tooth enamel as calcium phosphate deposits
Acts as a cofactor for enzymes that synthesize DNA
Enables nerve impulse transmission across synapses
Forms disulfide bridges that stabilize protein tertiary structure
Regulates membrane fluidity in animal cell membranes
Carries electrons in the electron transport chain as part of cytochromes

acceptableAlternatives: needed to make thyroid hormones; lack of iodine causes goiter
component of thyroxine (T3/T4); iodine deficiency causes enlarged thyroid
facts.quizQuestion: What is the biological role of iodine in the human body?
facts.explanation: Iodine is incorporated directly into the structure of thyroid hormones (T3 has three iodine atoms, T4 has four). These hormones regulate metabolic rate throughout the body. Without enough dietary iodine, the thyroid gland enlarges trying to capture more iodine from the blood — this swelling is called goiter.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of thyroid gland with iodine atoms incorporated into hormone structure
distractors: Required for oxygen transport in hemoglobin's heme group
Essential for bone mineralization as part of hydroxyapatite crystals
Acts as an electron carrier in the mitochondrial electron transport chain
Required for nerve impulse transmission across the neuromuscular junction
Needed for DNA synthesis and cell division throughout the body
Functions as a cofactor for over 300 enzymes in metabolic reactions
Regulates blood glucose levels as part of the insulin hormone structure
Required for collagen synthesis and connective tissue formation

acceptableAlternatives: forms bones/teeth as calcium phosphate, enables muscle contraction and cell signaling
structural role in skeleton, triggers muscle contraction, acts as second messenger
facts.quizQuestion: What are the biological roles of calcium in the human body?
facts.explanation: Calcium phosphate (hydroxyapatite) gives bones and teeth their hardness. Intracellular calcium ions act as a second messenger triggering muscle fiber contraction when released from the sarcoplasmic reticulum. Calcium also triggers neurotransmitter release at synapses.
facts.visualDescription: Three-panel diagram: bone structure, muscle fiber contracting, and cell signaling cascade
distractors: Required for thyroid hormone synthesis and regulation of metabolic rate
Carries oxygen in red blood cells as part of the heme group
Acts as a cofactor for DNA polymerase during DNA replication
Forms the phosphate backbone of DNA and RNA molecules
Regulates membrane fluidity in animal and plant cell membranes
Required for synthesis of coenzyme A and energy metabolism
Carries electrons between protein complexes in the electron transport chain
Forms disulfide bridges that maintain protein tertiary structure

acceptableAlternatives: same element, same protons, different neutrons and mass number
atoms with identical atomic number but different mass numbers due to different neutron counts
facts.quizQuestion: What is an isotope?
facts.explanation: Because isotopes have the same number of protons (and thus electrons), they have identical chemical behavior but different masses. Some isotopes are radioactive (radioisotopes) and decay over time — this is exploited in radiocarbon dating and medical imaging (PET scans).
facts.visualDescription: Two carbon atoms side by side: C-12 with 6 neutrons and C-14 with 8 neutrons labeled
distractors: Atoms of different elements that have the same mass number
Atoms of the same element with the same number of neutrons but different protons
Charged atoms that have gained or lost electrons to form ions
Atoms with the same mass but different numbers of electrons
Molecules with the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements
Atoms of an element in an excited energy state with elevated electrons
Atoms that share electrons with other atoms to form covalent bonds
Unstable atoms that emit radiation due to excess protons in the nucleus

acceptableAlternatives: Carbon-14 with half-life ~5,730 years used to date organic material
¹⁴C radioisotope for dating; decays with ~5,730 year half-life
facts.quizQuestion: What is Carbon-14 and what is it used for in biology?
facts.explanation: Carbon-14 forms in the atmosphere and is incorporated into organisms during their lifetime. After death, the ¹⁴C decays at a known rate (half-life 5,730 years) without being replaced, so the remaining ratio of ¹⁴C to ¹²C reveals how long ago the organism died.
facts.visualDescription: Archaeological fossil with radiocarbon dating timeline showing decay curve
distractors: Stable isotope of carbon used as a tracer to track metabolic pathways in cells
Radioactive isotope used in cancer radiation therapy targeting tumors
Stable isotope used in NMR spectroscopy to image protein structures
Carbon isotope with a half-life of 5,730 minutes used in PET scans
Radioactive isotope used to measure ocean salinity and temperature
Unstable isotope that decays instantly and is used in particle physics
Carbon-14 is used to date geological rock formations millions of years old
Radioactive tracer used to label DNA during replication experiments only

acceptableAlternatives: cofactor for 300+ enzymes; needed for DNA/protein synthesis and immunity
enzyme cofactor essential for DNA synthesis, protein synthesis, and immune defense
facts.quizQuestion: What is the biological role of zinc as a trace element in the human body?
facts.explanation: Zinc ions (Zn²⁺) stabilize enzyme active sites and are required for catalysis in enzymes spanning digestion, DNA replication, and immune cell development. The zinc-finger motif is also critical for transcription factor proteins that regulate gene expression.
facts.visualDescription: Enzyme protein with zinc ion (green sphere) stabilizing its active site
distractors: Forms the heme group in hemoglobin that binds oxygen for transport
Incorporated into thyroid hormones to regulate metabolic rate
Primary mineral component of bones and teeth as hydroxyapatite
Carries electrons in the mitochondrial electron transport chain
Acts as a hormone that regulates blood glucose levels
Required for muscle contraction by binding to troponin during excitation
Regulates membrane fluidity by intercalating between phospholipid tails
Forms disulfide bonds that stabilize protein tertiary structure

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the process in which two monomers are joined by a covalent bond with the release of one water molecule, building polymers?
facts.explanation: In dehydration synthesis (also called a condensation reaction), the -OH group of one monomer and the -H of another combine to release H₂O while forming a new covalent bond. This process builds every type of biological polymer: polysaccharides, proteins, nucleic acids, and fats.
facts.visualDescription: Two monomers approaching with -OH and -H highlighted, then joining as a water molecule exits

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the process in which polymers are broken into monomers by adding a water molecule across each bond; the reverse of condensation?
facts.explanation: Hydrolysis uses water to cleave the covalent bonds linking monomers. Digestive enzymes carry out hydrolysis to break down food macromolecules into absorbable monomers — amylase hydrolyzes starch, proteases hydrolyze proteins, and lipases hydrolyze fats.
facts.visualDescription: Polymer chain being cut as a water molecule inserts across a bond

facts.quizQuestion: What are the four major classes of biological macromolecules?
facts.explanation: Carbohydrates, proteins, and nucleic acids are true polymers built from repeating monomer units linked by the same bond type. Lipids are grouped with macromolecules by function and size but lack a true repeating monomer — they are united by their hydrophobic character instead.
facts.visualDescription: Four icons: sugar chain, protein helix, DNA double helix, and lipid bilayer
distractors: Carbohydrates, lipids, vitamins, and nucleic acids
Glucose, amino acids, fatty acids, and nucleotides

acceptableAlternatives: bond formed by shared electrons between atoms
strong bond where two atoms share one or more electron pairs
facts.quizQuestion: What is a covalent bond?
facts.explanation: Covalent bonds form when two atoms share electrons rather than transferring them (as in ionic bonds). They are stronger than hydrogen bonds and ionic interactions, which is why covalent bonds form the permanent backbone of biological polymers like proteins and DNA.
facts.visualDescription: Two atoms sharing an electron pair shown as overlapping orbitals
distractors: A weak electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions
An attraction between a partial positive hydrogen and an electronegative atom
A bond formed when one atom donates electrons to another atom
A bond between nonpolar molecules mediated by transient dipoles
A strong attraction between metal cations and delocalized electrons
A temporary bond formed when enzymes bind their substrates
A bond formed by the release of a water molecule between two molecules
An attraction between a positively charged amino group and a phosphate group

acceptableAlternatives: C-N covalent bond linking amino acids, formed with loss of H₂O
bond between -COOH of one amino acid and -NH2 of the next forming a C-N linkage
facts.quizQuestion: What covalent bond forms between the carboxyl group of one amino acid and the amino group of the next, releasing water?
facts.explanation: Peptide bonds are formed by dehydration synthesis during translation at the ribosome. The resulting C-N bond is relatively strong and rigid, which gives the polypeptide backbone its characteristic planarity and constrains the angles at which amino acids can rotate.
facts.visualDescription: Two amino acids joining with the -COOH/-NH2 interface highlighted, water molecule expelled

acceptableAlternatives: covalent bond between monosaccharides formed with loss of water
bond linking sugar monomers into disaccharides and polysaccharides
facts.quizQuestion: What covalent bond forms by dehydration synthesis between two monosaccharides, linking sugars into polysaccharides?
facts.explanation: Glycosidic bonds can be alpha (α) or beta (β) depending on the orientation of the hydroxyl group on the anomeric carbon. Starch uses α-1,4 linkages (digestible), while cellulose uses β-1,4 linkages (indigestible to most animals) — same monomer, very different properties.
facts.visualDescription: Two glucose rings connected by an oxygen bridge with the glycosidic bond labeled

acceptableAlternatives: 3'-to-5' phosphate linkage between nucleotides forming the sugar-phosphate backbone
bond joining the 3' OH of one nucleotide to the 5' phosphate of the next
facts.quizQuestion: What covalent bond links nucleotides in DNA/RNA, connecting a sugar's 3' carbon to the next nucleotide's 5' carbon via phosphate?
facts.explanation: Phosphodiester bonds give nucleic acids their characteristic sugar-phosphate backbone with directionality (5' to 3'). DNA polymerase adds new nucleotides only to the 3' end, and exonucleases cleave these bonds during repair. The phosphate groups give DNA its negative charge.
facts.visualDescription: Nucleotide chain showing alternating sugars and phosphates with 3'-5' directionality arrows

acceptableAlternatives: -OH group that adds polarity and water-solubility to molecules
oxygen-hydrogen functional group found in sugars and alcohols
facts.quizQuestion: What functional group found in alcohols and carbohydrates makes molecules polar and water-soluble?
facts.explanation: The -OH group is one of the most common functional groups in biology. In carbohydrates, multiple hydroxyl groups make sugars highly water-soluble and allow them to form hydrogen bonds. Hydroxyl groups are also reactive sites in dehydration synthesis reactions.
facts.visualDescription: Glucose molecule with multiple -OH groups highlighted in color

acceptableAlternatives: -COOH group that donates H⁺ at physiological pH, found in amino acids and fatty acids
carboxyl group (-COOH) makes molecules acidic; present in amino acids and fatty acids
facts.quizQuestion: What functional group, consisting of C=O and -OH, confers acidic properties on molecules like amino acids and fatty acids?
facts.explanation: The -COOH group ionizes at physiological pH, releasing a proton (H⁺) to become -COO⁻, making the molecule acidic. Every amino acid has a carboxyl group at one end, which participates in peptide bond formation, and fatty acids have carboxyl groups that link to glycerol in triglycerides.
facts.visualDescription: Amino acid structure with the -COOH terminus highlighted in red
distractors: Functional group -NH2 that confers basic properties and is found in amino acids
Functional group -OH that makes molecules polar and water-soluble
Functional group -SH that forms disulfide bonds stabilizing protein structure
Functional group -PO4 that gives nucleic acids their negative charge
Functional group -CH3 that is nonpolar and associated with epigenetic silencing
Functional group C=O (carbonyl) found in the middle of ketone molecules
Functional group -NO that makes molecules reactive as signaling molecules
A six-carbon ring structure found in glucose and other monosaccharides

acceptableAlternatives: -NH2 group that accepts H⁺ at physiological pH, found in all amino acids
amino group (-NH2) makes molecules basic; one end of every amino acid
facts.quizQuestion: What functional group containing nitrogen bonded to two hydrogens confers basic properties and is present in all amino acids?
facts.explanation: The -NH2 group accepts protons (H⁺) from solution, making the molecule basic (acts as a base). In amino acids, it is one of the two ends involved in peptide bond formation. The term 'amino acid' reflects having both an amino group (-NH2) and a carboxyl group (-COOH).
facts.visualDescription: Amino acid structure with the -NH2 terminus highlighted in blue

acceptableAlternatives: -PO4 group that adds negative charge; found in ATP, DNA/RNA, and phospholipids
phosphate group (-PO4) in ATP, nucleic acid backbone, and phospholipid head groups
facts.quizQuestion: What functional group carries a negative charge at physiological pH and is found in ATP, phospholipids, and the nucleotide backbone?
facts.explanation: Phosphate groups are highly polar and negatively charged at physiological pH, which is why DNA and RNA are negatively charged overall. The negative charge also prevents them from freely crossing lipid bilayers. The energy in ATP is stored in the bonds between adjacent phosphate groups.
facts.visualDescription: ATP molecule with three phosphate groups in a chain, middle phosphate highlighted
distractors: Functional group -NH2 found at the amino terminus of every polypeptide chain
Functional group -COOH that makes molecules acidic and links fatty acids to glycerol
Functional group -OH that makes carbohydrates and alcohols water-soluble
Functional group -SH found in cysteine that forms disulfide bridges in proteins
Functional group -CH3 added to DNA cytosines during epigenetic silencing
A nitrogenous ring that base-pairs with complementary bases in DNA and RNA
A carbonyl group C=O found at the reactive center of enzyme active sites
The sugar component of nucleotides that forms the backbone of nucleic acids

acceptableAlternatives: -SH group found in cysteine; pairs form disulfide bridges stabilizing protein 3D shape
thiol group (-SH) in cysteine residues; oxidized to -S-S- disulfide bonds
facts.quizQuestion: What functional group found on cysteine residues can form covalent disulfide bonds critical for protein tertiary structure?
facts.explanation: The amino acid cysteine carries a -SH group. When two cysteines come close together in a folded protein, their sulfhydryl groups can be oxidized to form a covalent disulfide bridge (-S-S-). Disulfide bonds are among the strongest stabilizing forces in protein structure and are abundant in extracellular proteins like keratin.
facts.visualDescription: Two cysteine residues in a protein with -SH groups oxidizing to form -S-S- bridge
distractors: Functional group -OH that makes molecules polar and participates in hydrogen bonding
Functional group -COOH that confers acidic properties to amino acids
Functional group -NH2 that accepts protons and forms peptide bonds
Functional group -PO4 that gives nucleic acids their negative charge
Functional group -CH3 that is nonpolar and involved in epigenetic regulation
A carbonyl group C=O at the end of fatty acid chains
A six-membered ring containing sulfur found in some cofactors
The bond formed between a phosphate group and a hydroxyl group in ATP

acceptableAlternatives: C=O group found as aldehyde (terminal) or ketone (internal) in sugars
carbonyl group — aldehyde at chain end, ketone in chain interior
facts.quizQuestion: What functional group (C=O) is found in sugars and metabolic intermediates, forming an aldehyde when terminal or a ketone when internal?
facts.explanation: Glucose is an aldehyde sugar (carbonyl at carbon 1), while fructose is a ketone sugar (carbonyl at carbon 2) — both are C6H12O6 but this positional difference makes them structural isomers with different properties and metabolic fates.
facts.visualDescription: Glucose (aldehyde) and fructose (ketone) drawn side by side with C=O positions labeled

acceptableAlternatives: -CH3 group that is nonpolar; added to DNA cytosines to silence gene expression
methyl group (-CH3) that is hydrophobic and mediates DNA methylation
facts.quizQuestion: What nonpolar functional group attached to cytosine bases regulates gene expression via epigenetic DNA methylation?
facts.explanation: When methyl groups are added to cytosine bases in CpG regions of DNA by DNA methyltransferase enzymes, gene expression is typically silenced. This epigenetic modification does not change the DNA sequence but can be inherited through cell divisions, affecting development and disease.
facts.visualDescription: DNA strand with methyl group (CH3) attached to a cytosine base, gene expression turned off symbol

acceptableAlternatives: same molecular formula, different bonding arrangement
same formula (e.g. C6H12O6) but different structural connectivity
facts.quizQuestion: What are structural isomers?
facts.explanation: Glucose and galactose are both C₆H₁₂O₆ but differ in the position of the -OH group on carbon 4. This small structural difference means galactose cannot directly enter glycolysis — it must first be converted to glucose by an enzyme (missing in people with galactosemia).
facts.visualDescription: Glucose and galactose drawn side by side, with their differing -OH group circled
distractors: Molecules with identical structures that are mirror images of each other
Molecules with the same molecular formula and bonding but different spatial arrangement around a double bond
Molecules with different molecular formulas that perform the same function
Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Molecules that have the same shape but different charge distributions
Polymers that are built from the same monomer but in different sequences
Molecules where one enantiomer is biologically active and the other is not
Atoms that share the same electron configuration but different nuclear charge

acceptableAlternatives: cis-trans isomers differing in group positions around a double bond
same bonding connectivity but different geometry around a C=C double bond
facts.quizQuestion: What are molecules with the same formula but different spatial arrangements around a double bond, such as cis vs. trans fatty acids?
facts.explanation: In unsaturated fatty acids, cis double bonds create a kink in the chain (e.g., oleic acid), preventing tight packing and keeping fats liquid at room temperature. Trans double bonds (trans fats) produce a straighter chain that packs more like saturated fats, raising LDL cholesterol levels.
facts.visualDescription: Cis fatty acid with a kink versus straight trans fatty acid around a C=C double bond
distractors: Molecules with the same molecular formula but different bonding arrangements of atoms
Mirror-image molecules that rotate polarized light in opposite directions
Molecules with different molecular formulas but similar biological functions
Atoms of the same element with different mass numbers due to neutron differences
Proteins with the same amino acid sequence but different folding patterns
DNA molecules with the same base sequence but different methylation patterns
Molecules that differ only in the orientation of their R-groups around a chiral carbon
Polymers built from the same monomers but with different chain lengths

acceptableAlternatives: mirror-image molecules that cannot be superimposed; life uses L-amino acids and D-sugars
chiral molecules that are mirror images; L and D forms have identical properties except rotation of polarized light
facts.quizQuestion: What are non-superimposable mirror-image molecules called, and which forms does biology preferentially use?
facts.explanation: Enantiomers are chiral molecules that differ only in the spatial arrangement around a carbon bonded to four different groups. Living organisms are asymmetric — ribosomes recognize only L-amino acids, and enzymes that process sugars are specific to D-sugars. Synthetic drugs often require the correct enantiomer to be effective.
facts.visualDescription: Two amino acid mirror images side by side, left hand L and right hand D labeled
distractors: Molecules with the same formula but different bonding arrangements between atoms

Molecules with completely different formulas that perform the same biological role
Polymers built from the same monomers but in different sequences
Molecules that are identical in structure but differ in overall molecular mass
Isomers that differ only in whether a hydroxyl group is on carbon 1 or carbon 2
Molecules where one form contains a ring structure and the other is linear

acceptableAlternatives: proteins are built from L-amino acids, not D-amino acids
organisms use L-amino acids; D-amino acids are rare in living systems
facts.quizQuestion: Which enantiomeric form of amino acids do ribosomes on Earth exclusively incorporate into proteins?
facts.explanation: All ribosomes exclusively incorporate L-amino acids into proteins. This homochirality is thought to have been selected early in life's evolution. Antibiotics like vancomycin exploit this by incorporating D-amino acids into bacterial cell wall precursors that mammalian enzymes cannot process.
facts.visualDescription: Ribosome accepting only the L-amino acid from a pair of enantiomers

amino acid side chains determine how proteins fold and interact based on hydrophobicity and charge
facts.quizQuestion: What are the four categories of amino acid R-group properties that determine protein folding and function?
facts.explanation: During protein folding, nonpolar R-groups cluster in the hydrophobic interior, charged R-groups form ionic bonds or point toward water, and -SH groups can form disulfide bridges. The unique combination of 20 possible R-groups at each position creates proteins with specific shapes and functions.

R-groups are all polar to ensure the protein remains soluble in cytoplasm

acceptableAlternatives: functional groups are the chemically active regions that determine molecular properties
reactivity, polarity, and biological interactions of a molecule depend on its functional groups
facts.quizQuestion: How do functional groups determine the chemical properties of biological molecules?
facts.explanation: Two molecules with the same carbon skeleton but different functional groups will have completely different chemical properties — one might be acidic, another basic, another polar. This is why structural isomers like glucose and fructose, or saturated and unsaturated fatty acids, behave so differently in cells.
facts.visualDescription: Carbon skeleton diagram with different functional groups swapped in, showing property changes

acceptableAlternatives: not true polymers — no repeating monomer unit or uniform bond type
diverse hydrophobic molecules grouped by solubility, not by monomer-polymer structure
facts.quizQuestion: Why are lipids not considered true polymers like carbohydrates or proteins?
facts.explanation: Unlike carbohydrates (linked by glycosidic bonds) and proteins (linked by peptide bonds), lipids are a chemically diverse group united only by their insolubility in water, not by a shared monomer-polymer relationship — which is why the AP exam distinguishes them as 'not true polymers.'
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side comparison: polypeptide chain (repeating units) vs. lipid assortment (triglyceride, phospholipid, steroid) with no repeating pattern

facts.correctAnswer: Glycerol + 3 fatty acids via ester bonds
acceptableAlternatives: glycerol + three fatty acids joined by ester linkages; main long-term energy storage lipid
one glycerol and three fatty acids linked by ester bonds — the main storage fat
facts.quizQuestion: What is the molecular structure of a triglyceride, the primary form of long-term energy storage in animals?
facts.explanation: Triglycerides are the most energy-dense biological molecules at ~9 kcal/g, making them ideal for long-term energy storage. They are synthesized by three dehydration synthesis reactions, each joining a fatty acid's carboxyl group to one of glycerol's three hydroxyl groups.
facts.visualDescription: Glycerol backbone with three fatty acid chains attached, labeled with ester linkages at each junction
distractors: A lipid made of two phospholipid molecules linked by a disulfide bond

A protein with three polypeptide chains bonded by peptide bonds
A lipid made of one fatty acid bonded to three glycerol molecules
A steroid with three fused carbon rings attached to a fatty acid tail
A nucleic acid with three nucleotides joined by phosphodiester bonds
A lipid consisting of a phosphate head group and three fatty acid tails
A molecule of glycerol bonded to three phosphate groups for energy transfer

acceptableAlternatives: bond between a fatty acid's carboxyl group and glycerol's hydroxyl group, releasing water
covalent bond linking fatty acids to glycerol in lipid synthesis
facts.quizQuestion: What covalent bond forms between the carboxyl group of a fatty acid and a hydroxyl group of glycerol, joining fatty acids to glycerol?
facts.explanation: Ester linkages are formed by dehydration synthesis — the same mechanism that builds all biological polymers — but here between a fatty acid and glycerol rather than between identical monomers. Three ester linkages join three fatty acids to glycerol to create a triglyceride.
facts.visualDescription: Close-up of glycerol-fatty acid junction showing -COO- ester bond with water molecule byproduct

facts.quizQuestion: Fats yield approximately {___} kcal/g, compared to 4 kcal/g for carbohydrates and proteins.
facts.explanation: Fats store more than twice the energy per gram as carbohydrates because fatty acid chains are highly reduced — they contain more C-H bonds, which release more energy when oxidized. This makes triglycerides the most efficient long-term energy storage molecule in animals.
facts.visualDescription: Bar graph comparing energy density: fat bar at 9 kcal/g vs. carbohydrate and protein bars at 4 kcal/g

acceptableAlternatives: fatty acid with no C=C double bonds; straight chain; solid at room temperature
no double bonds between carbons; tightly packed; solid fats like butter
facts.quizQuestion: What is a fatty acid with no carbon-carbon double bonds that packs tightly into a solid at room temperature, such as butter?
facts.explanation: The absence of double bonds means all carbons are fully 'saturated' with hydrogen atoms. The resulting straight-chain geometry lets saturated fatty acids pack tightly together, increasing intermolecular van der Waals forces and raising the melting point — which is why animal fats (butter, lard) are solid at room temperature.
facts.visualDescription: Straight-chain fatty acid molecule vs. kinked unsaturated chain; butter block labeled as saturated fat example
distractors: A fatty acid with multiple carbon-carbon double bonds that is liquid at room temperature

A fatty acid bonded to glycerol through a peptide rather than an ester bond
A fatty acid with a kinked carbon chain due to a cis double bond
A fatty acid with no hydrogen atoms, consisting only of carbon and oxygen
A fatty acid with an even number of double bonds that is solid at room temperature
A fatty acid found only in plant oils and liquid at all temperatures

acceptableAlternatives: has one or more C=C double bonds; kinked chain; liquid oils like olive oil
double bonds create kinks; unsaturated fats are liquid at room temperature
facts.quizQuestion: What is a fatty acid with one or more C=C double bonds that creates chain kinks, making it liquid at room temperature (e.g., olive oil)?
facts.explanation: Each cis double bond introduces a ~30° kink in the fatty acid chain. These kinks prevent the tight packing that occurs in saturated fats, reducing intermolecular attractions and lowering the melting point — so plant oils (which are highly unsaturated) remain liquid at room temperature.
facts.visualDescription: Kinked fatty acid chain with cis double bond labeled; olive oil bottle as biological example
distractors: A fatty acid with no double bonds that is solid at room temperature

A fatty acid in which all carbons are fully saturated with hydrogen atoms
A fatty acid found only in animal membranes that regulates membrane fluidity
A fatty acid with triple bonds in the carbon chain making it very reactive
A fatty acid with a straight chain that packs tightly and forms butter
A fatty acid lacking a carboxyl group but containing a hydroxyl group instead
A fatty acid bonded to a sterol ring that forms the core of steroids

acceptableAlternatives: hydrogenated fat with hydrogen atoms on opposite sides of double bond; raises heart disease risk
trans configuration fat — hydrogen atoms opposite across double bond; artificial and unhealthy
facts.quizQuestion: What type of artificially hydrogenated fat has hydrogen atoms on opposite sides of its double bond and is linked to cardiovascular disease?
facts.explanation: Trans fats are produced by partial hydrogenation of vegetable oils (e.g., making margarine). Unlike cis unsaturated fats, the trans configuration produces a nearly straight chain that packs more tightly, raising LDL cholesterol and increasing plaque formation in arteries — a cardiovascular health risk.
facts.visualDescription: Comparison of cis vs. trans double bond configuration, with hydrogen positions labeled on each side
distractors: A naturally occurring saturated fat found in butter and animal products
A fatty acid with all double bonds removed by enzymatic action in cells

A type of steroid derived from cholesterol that carries fat in the bloodstream

A fatty acid with a phosphate head group that is solid at room temperature
A wax produced by bees that is solid at room temperature but melts at body temperature
A fat formed when fatty acids polymerize and become solid through cooling

acceptableAlternatives: glycerol + two fatty acid tails + polar phosphate head; amphipathic molecule
two fatty acids and a phosphate head on glycerol; simultaneously hydrophilic and hydrophobic
facts.quizQuestion: What are the structural components of a phospholipid, and why is it described as amphipathic?
facts.explanation: A phospholipid differs from a triglyceride in that one of the three fatty acid positions is replaced by a charged phosphate group (often attached to a small polar molecule like choline). This creates a molecule with a polar 'head' that interacts with water and two nonpolar 'tails' that avoid it — the amphipathic property that drives spontaneous bilayer formation.
facts.visualDescription: Phospholipid diagram with labeled hydrophilic head (phosphate + glycerol) and two hydrophobic fatty acid tails
distractors: A lipid with three fatty acid tails and no phosphate group, used for energy storage
A protein-lipid complex with a phosphate group that transports fats in the blood
A lipid with one fatty acid tail and two phosphate head groups making it fully hydrophilic
A steroid modified by adding a phosphate group to its four-ring structure
A lipid made of glycerol bonded to two phosphate groups and one fatty acid
A carbohydrate with a phosphate group attached, found in the cell membrane
A lipid consisting entirely of fatty acids with no glycerol backbone
A molecule with two glycerol backbones linked by a single phosphate bridge

acceptableAlternatives: having both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions in the same molecule
molecule with a polar head and nonpolar tail, like a phospholipid
facts.quizQuestion: What term describes a molecule that has both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions, enabling phospholipids to form bilayers?
facts.explanation: Amphipathic molecules spontaneously organize in water to minimize thermodynamically unfavorable hydrophobic-water interactions. Phospholipids do this by forming bilayers with tails tucked inward and heads facing water — a self-assembling structure that is the foundation of all cellular membranes.
facts.visualDescription: Phospholipid bilayer cross-section with arrows labeling hydrophilic heads (facing water) and hydrophobic tails (facing inward)

acceptableAlternatives: amphipathic phospholipids self-assemble into bilayers; tails inward, heads outward — basis of cell membranes
hydrophobic tails sequestered internally, polar heads face water — creating a selectively permeable membrane
facts.quizQuestion: What structure do amphipathic phospholipids spontaneously form in water, with hydrophobic tails inward and hydrophilic heads outward?
facts.explanation: The hydrophobic effect drives phospholipid bilayer self-assembly — nonpolar tails cluster together to escape water, while polar heads stay in contact with aqueous environments on both sides. This creates a semi-permeable barrier that controls what enters and exits cells, a universal feature of all living cells.
facts.visualDescription: Phospholipid bilayer forming spontaneously in water, with polar heads on both outer surfaces and hydrophobic tails forming inner core

acceptableAlternatives: four fused carbon ring structure; includes cholesterol and steroid hormones
lipids with a four-ring carbon skeleton — cholesterol, sex hormones, and bile acids
facts.quizQuestion: What structural feature defines steroids, and what are three examples of biologically important steroids?
facts.explanation: All steroids share a core of four fused carbon rings (three 6-carbon rings and one 5-carbon ring) — a structure very different from other lipids. Despite this, they are classified as lipids because they are hydrophobic. Small functional group differences on the ring structure produce dramatically different biological effects (e.g., testosterone vs. estrogen differ by only one -OH group).
facts.visualDescription: Steroid ring structure (four fused rings) with cholesterol labeled as the parent compound
distractors: Lipids characterized by three fatty acid chains bonded to a single phosphate backbone

Glycoproteins with four sugar rings attached to a central protein backbone

facts.quizQuestion: Steroids are characterized by a core structure of {___} fused carbon rings.
facts.explanation: The four-ring steroid skeleton (three 6-membered rings + one 5-membered ring) is called the sterane core. All steroids — from cholesterol to testosterone to cortisol — share this scaffold; their different biological activities arise from different functional groups attached to the rings.
facts.visualDescription: Steroid ring skeleton showing four fused rings with the ring count labeled

acceptableAlternatives: membrane fluidity regulator; also precursor to steroid hormones and vitamin D
stabilizes membrane fluidity; serves as starting material for steroid hormones and vitamin D
facts.quizQuestion: What are the two main biological roles of cholesterol in animal cells?
facts.explanation: Cholesterol's rigid ring structure inserts between phospholipid tails in the membrane — at cold temperatures it prevents them from packing too tightly (preventing solidification), and at warm temperatures it restrains excessive fluidity. This 'fluidity buffer' role is critical for membrane function across temperature ranges.
facts.visualDescription: Cell membrane cross-section with cholesterol molecules interspersed between phospholipid tails, labeled 'fluidity regulator'
distractors: Provides the major source of long-term energy storage by forming lipid droplets in adipose tissue

long-chain fatty acid bonded to a long alcohol; waterproofing lipids found in cuticles and feathers
facts.quizQuestion: What type of lipid made of a fatty acid and long-chain alcohol serves as a waterproofing agent in plant cuticles and feathers?
facts.explanation: Waxes have a very high melting point because of their long, straight chains with extensive van der Waals interactions. The plant cuticle (cutin/wax layer) prevents water loss from leaves; feather waxes from the preen gland maintain waterproofing; and the waxy coating of fruits reduces dehydration and pathogen entry.
facts.visualDescription: Leaf cross-section showing waxy cuticle layer on top; also a duck with water beading off feathers

facts.quizQuestion: What are the key structural features that define DNA?
facts.explanation: DNA's double-stranded structure provides redundancy — each strand serves as a template for the other during replication. The deoxyribose sugar (missing the 2'-OH of ribose) makes DNA more chemically stable than RNA, appropriate for long-term storage of genetic information.
facts.visualDescription: DNA double helix diagram with deoxyribose-phosphate backbone labeled and A-T, G-C base pairs shown

facts.quizQuestion: What are the key structural features of RNA, and what are its three main functional types?
facts.explanation: RNA's single-stranded nature allows it to fold back on itself, forming complex secondary structures (e.g., tRNA cloverleaf, ribozymes). The 2'-OH on ribose makes RNA more reactive and less stable than DNA — suitable for temporary messages (mRNA) rather than permanent genetic storage.

DNA: deoxyribose, T base, double helix, gene storage; RNA: ribose, U base, single strand, gene expression
facts.quizQuestion: What are the key structural differences between DNA and RNA?
facts.explanation: These four differences reflect each molecule's specialized role: deoxyribose makes DNA more stable for long-term storage; thymine (with its methyl group) may protect DNA from UV-induced mutations; double-stranding allows faithful replication; and single-stranded RNA can fold into functional 3D shapes needed for catalysis and translation.

facts.quizQuestion: What are the three structural components of a nucleotide, the monomer of nucleic acids?
facts.explanation: Nucleotides are linked by phosphodiester bonds — the phosphate group of one nucleotide bonds to the 3' carbon of the preceding nucleotide's sugar, forming the sugar-phosphate backbone. The sequence of nitrogenous bases sticking off this backbone encodes genetic information.
facts.visualDescription: Single nucleotide diagram with three labeled components: pentose sugar, phosphate group, and nitrogenous base

acceptableAlternatives: A and G — double-ring nitrogenous bases
facts.quizQuestion: Which two nitrogenous bases are purines (double-ring structure)?
facts.explanation: Purines have a double-ring structure (a six-membered ring fused to a five-membered ring). The mnemonic 'PURe As Gold' helps: Purines = Adenine and Guanine. In double-stranded DNA, each purine always pairs with a pyrimidine (A with T; G with C), maintaining a consistent helix width.
facts.visualDescription: Adenine and guanine molecular structures showing two fused rings, labeled 'purines'

acceptableAlternatives: cytosine (C), thymine (T), and uracil (U) — single-ring nitrogenous bases
C, T, and U — the pyrimidines; thymine in DNA, uracil in RNA
facts.quizQuestion: Which three nitrogenous bases are pyrimidines (single-ring structure)?
facts.explanation: Pyrimidines have a single six-membered ring. The mnemonic 'CUT' helps: Cytosine, Uracil, Thymine. Cytosine is found in both DNA and RNA; thymine is DNA-only; uracil is RNA-only. Since purines pair with pyrimidines, DNA base pairs always consist of one double-ring + one single-ring base, keeping helix width uniform.
facts.visualDescription: Cytosine, thymine, and uracil molecular structures showing a single six-membered ring each, labeled 'pyrimidines'

acceptableAlternatives: the principle of base complementarity (A=T, G=C) discovered by Chargaff
facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the rule stating that in double-stranded DNA, the amount of adenine equals thymine (A=T) and guanine equals cytosine (G=C)?
facts.explanation: Erwin Chargaff observed in 1950 that across many organisms, [A]=[T] and [G]=[C] in DNA. This empirical observation later made sense structurally — Watson and Crick showed that A pairs with T via 2 hydrogen bonds and G pairs with C via 3 hydrogen bonds. If the DNA of an organism is 30% A, it must also be 30% T, and the remaining 40% is split equally between G and C (20% each).
facts.visualDescription: DNA base pairing diagram showing A-T and G-C pairs, with percentage labels demonstrating A=T and G=C equality

acceptableAlternatives: DNA strands run antiparallel — one 5'→3', the other 3'→5'

facts.quizQuestion: What does "antiparallel" mean in the context of the DNA double helix?
facts.explanation: DNA polymerase can only synthesize new DNA in the 5'→3' direction, so the antiparallel arrangement of DNA strands creates the leading and lagging strand problem during replication. The antiparallel orientation is also why complementary base sequences appear 'backwards' — the complement of 5'-ATCG-3' is written 3'-TAGC-5' (or 5'-CGAT-3').
facts.visualDescription: DNA double helix with arrows showing 5'→3' direction on each strand going in opposite directions; 5' and 3' ends labeled

Antiparallel refers to the opposite electrical charges on the two DNA strands
The DNA strands are antiparallel meaning they have different base compositions on each strand

facts.quizQuestion: In DNA, adenine (A) pairs with thymine (T) via {___} hydrogen bonds.
facts.explanation: A-T pairs form 2 hydrogen bonds while G-C pairs form 3. This difference means GC-rich DNA regions require more energy (higher temperature) to separate the two strands — a phenomenon called melting temperature (Tm). Regions that need to unwind easily (like origins of replication) tend to be AT-rich.
facts.visualDescription: A-T base pair with two dashed hydrogen bonds drawn between them; G-C pair with three dashed bonds for comparison

facts.quizQuestion: In DNA, guanine (G) pairs with cytosine (C) via {___} hydrogen bonds.
facts.explanation: G-C pairs form 3 hydrogen bonds, making GC-rich DNA more thermally stable than AT-rich DNA. This is why the melting temperature (Tm) of a DNA duplex increases linearly with GC content. Scientists exploit this by using GC content to predict hybridization behavior in PCR primer design and Southern blotting.
facts.visualDescription: G-C base pair with three dashed hydrogen bonds labeled; comparison with A-T pair showing only two bonds

acceptableAlternatives: adenine + ribose + three phosphate groups; energy released by removing the terminal phosphate
nucleotide with adenine, ribose, and 3 phosphates; powers cellular processes via terminal phosphate hydrolysis
facts.quizQuestion: What are the three structural components of ATP, the universal energy currency of cells?
facts.explanation: ATP is essentially an RNA nucleotide (adenosine monophosphate) with two additional phosphate groups. The bonds between phosphate groups are unstable due to electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged phosphates — releasing the terminal phosphate (forming ADP + Pi) releases ~7.3 kcal/mol that drives endergonic cellular reactions.
facts.visualDescription: ATP molecule with adenine, ribose, and three phosphate groups labeled; arrow showing terminal phosphate hydrolysis to ADP + Pi

facts.quizQuestion: ATP (adenosine triphosphate) contains {___} phosphate groups.
facts.explanation: The 'tri' in triphosphate refers to all three phosphate groups. When the terminal (third) phosphate is hydrolyzed, ATP becomes ADP (diphosphate = 2 phosphates). When a second phosphate is released, ADP becomes AMP (monophosphate = 1 phosphate). The progression ATP → ADP → AMP releases energy in two sequential steps.
facts.visualDescription: ATP structure showing three phosphate groups with labels P1, P2, P3; arrow from P3 showing release to form ADP + Pi

acceptableAlternatives: ATP hydrolysis to ADP + Pi releases ~7.3 kcal/mol; relief of phosphate charge repulsion drives the reaction
~7.3 kcal/mol released when the terminal phosphate is hydrolyzed from ATP to ADP
facts.quizQuestion: How does ATP release energy for cellular work, and approximately how much energy is released per hydrolysis?
facts.explanation: ATP hydrolysis is exergonic (ΔG = −7.3 kcal/mol under standard conditions). Cells couple this exergonic reaction to endergonic reactions (biosynthesis, active transport, muscle contraction) — a strategy called energy coupling. The free energy released drives reactions that would otherwise not proceed spontaneously.
facts.visualDescription: Chemical equation: ATP + H2O → ADP + Pi + energy (7.3 kcal/mol); energy coupling diagram showing ATP hydrolysis powering an endergonic reaction
distractors: ATP releases energy by gaining a phosphate group from ADP, releasing 3.5 kcal/mol
ATP releases energy through oxidation of adenine, releasing 15 kcal/mol of thermal energy
ATP releases energy by converting ribose to deoxyribose, releasing 12 kcal/mol
ATP releases energy when adenine is removed from the molecule, releasing 20 kcal/mol
ATP releases energy by transferring electrons to oxygen, releasing 38 kcal/mol per molecule
ATP stores energy in its carbon-hydrogen bonds; hydrolysis of these bonds releases 4 kcal/mol
ATP releases energy when all three phosphate groups are simultaneously removed, releasing 22 kcal/mol
ATP releases energy by breaking the bond between adenine and ribose, releasing 9 kcal/mol

facts.quizQuestion: There are {___} standard amino acids used to build proteins in all living organisms.
facts.explanation: The 20 standard amino acids differ only in their R-groups (side chains). With 20 amino acid types and typical protein lengths of hundreds to thousands of residues, the number of possible protein sequences is astronomically large (20^n for a protein of n residues) — explaining the enormous diversity of protein structure and function in life.
facts.visualDescription: Grid of 20 amino acid abbreviations (Gly, Ala, Val... etc.) arranged by R-group category

acceptableAlternatives: central carbon + amino group + carboxyl group + H + variable R-group
alpha carbon bonded to -NH2, -COOH, -H, and a unique side chain (R-group)
facts.quizQuestion: What is the general structure shared by all 20 amino acids?
facts.explanation: The four groups bonded to the alpha carbon are constant across all amino acids — only the R-group varies. This shared structure allows all amino acids to form peptide bonds with one another. The R-group's properties (size, charge, polarity) determine how the polypeptide will fold and what function it will serve.
facts.visualDescription: Generalized amino acid diagram with central alpha carbon and four labeled groups: -NH2, -COOH, -H, and 'R-group (variable)'
distractors: A central carbon bonded to two amino groups, one carboxyl group, and one phosphate group
A nitrogen-containing ring bonded to a ribose sugar, a phosphate group, and a hydroxyl group
A central carbon bonded to one amino group, two carboxyl groups, and one R-group
A glycerol backbone bonded to one amino group, one fatty acid, and one R-group
A central carbon bonded to an amino group, a hydroxyl group, a hydrogen atom, and a variable base
A central carbon bonded to four identical amino groups with different R-group attachments
A nitrogen atom bonded to two carboxyl groups, one hydrogen, and one variable side chain
A central carbon bonded to a sulfhydryl group, an amino group, a hydrogen, and a base

acceptableAlternatives: linear amino acid sequence held by peptide bonds; determined by DNA; dictates all higher-order folding
the specific order of amino acids in a polypeptide — the fundamental determinant of protein structure and function
facts.quizQuestion: What is the primary structure of a protein, and why is it considered the most fundamental level?
facts.explanation: Primary structure is the most fundamental because it encodes all the information needed for the protein to fold into its 3D shape. Even a single amino acid substitution (as in sickle cell hemoglobin — glutamate → valine at position 6 of beta-globin) can completely alter protein shape and function.
facts.visualDescription: String of amino acid beads (letters: G-A-L-V-E...) connected by peptide bonds, labeled 'primary structure'
distractors: The local folding patterns of the polypeptide backbone stabilized by hydrogen bonds between backbone groups
The overall 3D folding of a single polypeptide chain determined by R-group interactions
The association of two or more polypeptide subunits into a single functional protein complex
The number of disulfide bonds formed between cysteine residues in the protein's core
The spatial arrangement of alpha helices and beta sheets within a single protein domain
The distribution of hydrophobic and hydrophilic R-groups on the protein's outer surface
The ionic bonds formed between positively and negatively charged R-groups in the folded protein
The pattern of glycosylation (sugar attachment) on the surface of a finished protein

facts.correctAnswer: Alpha helix and beta pleated sheet
acceptableAlternatives: backbone hydrogen bonds create local structures — alpha helices and beta pleated sheets
hydrogen bonds between -NH and C=O of the backbone cause alpha helix or beta sheet folding
facts.quizQuestion: What is secondary protein structure, and what type of bond stabilizes it?
facts.explanation: Secondary structure involves only backbone atoms — the R-groups are not involved. Hydrogen bonds form between the carbonyl oxygen (C=O) of one amino acid and the amino hydrogen (N-H) of another amino acid in the backbone. These patterns repeat regularly to form helices or sheets before the overall tertiary fold occurs.
facts.visualDescription: Polypeptide chain forming an alpha helix (coil) and a beta sheet (accordion fold); dashed lines showing backbone hydrogen bonds
distractors: The overall 3D folding of a polypeptide stabilized by R-group interactions including disulfide bonds
The association of multiple polypeptide chains held together by hydrophobic interactions between subunits
Local folding patterns stabilized by covalent peptide bonds between R-groups of adjacent amino acids
Local folding patterns created by ionic bonds between the alpha carbon of one amino acid and the next
The linear sequence of amino acids stabilized by peptide bonds in the polypeptide backbone
Folding patterns created by phosphodiester bonds between backbone carbonyl and amino groups
The arrangement of R-groups on the exterior of the protein stabilized by van der Waals forces
Local folding patterns determined by disulfide bridges between backbone cysteine residues

facts.quizQuestion: What type of secondary protein structure forms when the polypeptide backbone coils into a right-handed spiral stabilized by hydrogen bonds between every fourth amino acid in the backbone?
facts.explanation: In an alpha helix, each amino acid's backbone -NH donates a hydrogen bond to the backbone C=O of the amino acid that is 4 residues earlier in the sequence. This creates a regular right-handed coil with approximately 3.6 amino acids per turn. The alpha helix is found in many structural proteins (keratin in hair, the transmembrane helices of membrane proteins).
facts.visualDescription: Ribbon diagram of an alpha helix showing the right-handed coiled backbone with dashed hydrogen bonds between backbone groups

facts.quizQuestion: What type of secondary protein structure forms when polypeptide backbone strands align side by side and are held together by hydrogen bonds between backbone groups, creating a flat accordion-like structure?
facts.explanation: Beta pleated sheets consist of extended strands of the polypeptide backbone lying side by side, with hydrogen bonds running between strands (perpendicular to the strand direction) rather than within a single strand. Strands can run parallel or antiparallel. Silk fibroin and the core of immunoglobulins are largely beta sheets.
facts.visualDescription: Ribbon diagram of a beta pleated sheet showing flat extended strands with hydrogen bonds running laterally between them

acceptableAlternatives: 3D shape of a single polypeptide determined by R-group interactions including hydrophobic collapse, ionic bonds, H-bonds, and disulfide bridges
overall conformation of one polypeptide; stabilized by R-group-based forces — hydrophobic, ionic, hydrogen bond, and covalent disulfide
facts.quizQuestion: What is tertiary protein structure, and what types of interactions stabilize it?
facts.explanation: Tertiary structure arises from four classes of R-group interactions: (1) hydrophobic collapse — nonpolar R-groups cluster in the protein interior away from water; (2) ionic bonds between oppositely charged R-groups; (3) hydrogen bonds between polar R-groups; and (4) disulfide bonds between cysteine -SH groups. Hydrophobic interactions are the primary driving force for folding.
facts.visualDescription: Ribbon diagram of a single folded protein showing hydrophobic core (shaded), labeled ionic bond, H-bond, and disulfide bridge between cysteines
distractors: The association of multiple polypeptide subunits into a complex held by interactions between subunit surfaces
The local folding of the polypeptide backbone into alpha helices and beta sheets via backbone hydrogen bonds
The linear sequence of amino acids connected by peptide bonds determined by the gene sequence
The 3D folding of a polypeptide determined only by covalent disulfide bonds between backbone atoms
The arrangement of protein subunits around a central metal ion required for enzyme catalysis
The spatial orientation of the polypeptide in the cell membrane, determined by hydrophilic R-groups only
The 3D structure of multiple polypeptides folded together, held by backbone peptide bonds
The overall shape determined by the sequence of nucleotides in the mRNA that encoded the protein

acceptableAlternatives: covalent S-S bond between two cysteine side chains; stabilizes tertiary structure
-SH + -SH from two cysteines → -S-S- disulfide bridge; covalent, so survives mild denaturation
facts.quizQuestion: What covalent bond forms between two cysteine sulfhydryl groups and strongly stabilizes protein tertiary structure?
facts.explanation: Unlike hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds, and hydrophobic interactions — which are all noncovalent — disulfide bonds are covalent, making them much stronger. Mild denaturation (slight pH change, low urea concentration) can disrupt noncovalent interactions, but disulfide bonds require reduction (breaking with reagents like beta-mercaptoethanol) to be disrupted.
facts.visualDescription: Two cysteine side chains (-SH) forming a -S-S- bridge with oxidation arrow; labeled as covalent bond compared to noncovalent interactions nearby
distractors: A hydrogen bond between two cysteine R-groups that stabilizes secondary protein structure
An ionic bond between a positively and negatively charged R-group in the protein interior
A covalent bond between two lysine residues that forms during post-translational modification
A peptide bond between cysteine and the adjacent amino acid in the polypeptide backbone
A noncovalent hydrophobic interaction between two sulfur-containing R-groups in the protein core
A van der Waals force between two adjacent sulfhydryl groups in folded membrane proteins
A phosphodiester bond between cysteine residues that is unique to enzymes with metal cofactors
A covalent bond between two glycine residues that creates rigid turns in the polypeptide chain

acceptableAlternatives: two or more polypeptide chains assembled into one functional protein; hemoglobin (4 subunits) is a classic example
multiple polypeptides held together by hydrophobic, ionic, and hydrogen bond interactions between subunit surfaces
facts.quizQuestion: What is quaternary protein structure, and what is a well-known biological example?
facts.explanation: Not all proteins have quaternary structure — only those that require multiple polypeptide subunits to function. Hemoglobin is the classic example because its four subunits interact cooperatively: binding of O2 to one subunit increases the O2 affinity of the others (cooperativity). Loss of quaternary structure means loss of this cooperative behavior.
facts.visualDescription: Hemoglobin model showing four polypeptide subunits (2 alpha, 2 beta) with heme groups labeled; arrows showing subunit interactions
distractors: The local folding of the polypeptide backbone into repeating alpha helix or beta sheet patterns
The overall 3D folding of a single polypeptide determined by R-group interactions and hydrophobic collapse
The linear sequence of amino acids that determines all downstream structure of a polypeptide chain
The attachment of carbohydrate groups to a single polypeptide chain during post-translational modification
The interaction between a protein and its DNA template during transcription of the gene
The arrangement of protein domains along a single polypeptide with no subunit association required
The folding of a protein around a metal cofactor that holds the single polypeptide in its active conformation
The covalent cross-linking of two polypeptide chains by disulfide bonds between all cysteine residues

facts.quizQuestion: Hemoglobin, the oxygen-carrying protein in red blood cells, consists of {___} polypeptide subunits.
facts.explanation: Hemoglobin's 4 subunits (2 alpha + 2 beta chains) allow cooperative oxygen binding — when one subunit binds O2, it triggers a conformational change that increases affinity in the remaining subunits. This cooperative behavior gives hemoglobin a sigmoidal O2-binding curve, which is functionally superior to the hyperbolic curve of a single-chain oxygen carrier like myoglobin.
facts.visualDescription: Hemoglobin quaternary structure with four colored subunits labeled: 2 alpha chains (blue) and 2 beta chains (red), each with a heme group

facts.quizQuestion: Protein structure is organized into {___} levels: primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary.
facts.explanation: The four levels build on each other hierarchically: primary (amino acid sequence) → secondary (local backbone folding) → tertiary (overall 3D shape of one chain) → quaternary (assembly of multiple chains). Each level arises from and depends on the level below it, with primary structure ultimately determining all higher-order structure.
facts.visualDescription: Four-step diagram showing: 1) amino acid chain, 2) helix/sheet, 3) folded protein, 4) multi-subunit complex

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the process in which a protein loses its native 3D shape due to disruption of noncovalent interactions by heat, extreme pH, or chemical agents?
facts.explanation: Denaturation disrupts all noncovalent interactions (hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds, hydrophobic interactions) that maintain secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure, causing the protein to unfold and lose function. The covalent peptide bonds holding the amino acid chain together are NOT broken — the primary structure remains intact.
facts.visualDescription: Protein unfolding diagram: folded enzyme → denatured random coil under heat/acid, with active site destroyed and substrate unable to bind

facts.correctAnswer: Primary sequence intact; only 3D shape lost
acceptableAlternatives: denaturation preserves primary structure (peptide bonds intact); destroys secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure only
peptide bonds are covalent and survive denaturation; the amino acid sequence is unchanged even when the protein is unfolded
facts.quizQuestion: After a protein is denatured by heat, what structural level remains intact and why?
facts.explanation: This is a classic AP Biology trap question. Denaturation disrupts noncovalent interactions (secondary, tertiary, quaternary structure) but the covalent peptide bonds linking amino acids together require enzymatic cleavage (hydrolysis by proteases) to break — not just heat or pH change. So a denatured protein still has its full amino acid sequence, just arranged randomly rather than in its functional shape.
facts.visualDescription: Before/after denaturation: folded protein showing H-bonds and hydrophobic core (disrupted) vs. unfolded strand still showing intact peptide bonds in backbone
distractors: Denaturation breaks all bonds including peptide bonds, releasing free amino acids into solution
Denaturation preserves quaternary structure but destroys primary, secondary, and tertiary structure
Denaturation preserves secondary structure (alpha helices) but destroys tertiary and quaternary structure
Denaturation breaks disulfide bonds and peptide bonds but leaves hydrogen bonds and ionic bonds intact
Denaturation destroys all four levels of structure simultaneously, including the amino acid sequence
Denaturation preserves tertiary structure but disrupts primary and secondary structure only
Denaturation preserves the active site shape while disrupting the rest of the protein structure
Denaturation breaks hydrogen bonds in the backbone only, leaving R-group interactions intact

acceptableAlternatives: biological catalyst that lowers activation energy; reusable; usually a protein
speeds reactions by lowering Ea; not consumed; always a protein (or rarely RNA ribozyme)
facts.quizQuestion: What is an enzyme, and how does it affect the energy requirement of a chemical reaction?
facts.explanation: Enzymes lower activation energy (Ea) — the energy needed to start a reaction — by stabilizing the transition state of the reaction. Critically, enzymes do NOT change the overall free energy change (ΔG) of the reaction; they only change the rate. A thermodynamically unfavorable reaction remains unfavorable even with an enzyme.
facts.visualDescription: Energy diagram showing reaction pathway with high activation energy hump (no enzyme) vs. lower hump (with enzyme), same ΔG for both
distractors: A biological molecule that supplies energy to drive endergonic reactions by bonding directly to reactants
A protein that changes the overall free energy change (ΔG) of a reaction to make it thermodynamically favorable
A molecule consumed in the reaction it catalyzes, regenerated separately by ATP hydrolysis
A lipid-based molecule that raises the activation energy to prevent undesired reactions from occurring
A nucleic acid that always acts as an RNA ribozyme, never as a protein
A protein that changes the temperature of a reaction, effectively lowering the activation energy by heating
A molecule that increases the concentration of substrates to push the reaction toward products
A protein catalyst that is permanently altered after each catalytic cycle and must be replaced
