facts.explanation: In competitive inhibition, the inhibitor molecule is structurally similar to the substrate and binds to the same active site, directly blocking substrate access. Because the inhibitor and substrate compete for the same binding site, adding more substrate dilutes the effect of the inhibitor — high substrate concentrations can restore enzyme activity.
facts.visualDescription: An enzyme active site with two molecules competing to enter — one labeled 'substrate' and one labeled 'inhibitor', similar in shape

facts.quizQuestion: What type of inhibition has the inhibitor bind to a regulatory site (not the active site), changing the enzyme's shape so it can no longer function — and cannot be overcome by adding more substrate?
facts.explanation: In noncompetitive (allosteric) inhibition, the inhibitor binds to the allosteric site — a regulatory region separate from the active site. This causes a conformational change that distorts the active site, preventing substrate binding or catalysis. Unlike competitive inhibition, adding more substrate cannot overcome this effect.
facts.visualDescription: An enzyme with an inhibitor molecule attached to a separate site causing the active site to change shape so the substrate no longer fits

facts.quizQuestion: What occurs when a regulatory molecule binds to a site other than the active site and stabilizes the enzyme in its active conformation, increasing catalytic activity?
facts.explanation: Allosteric activation is the positive regulation of enzyme activity through a regulatory molecule binding to the allosteric site. The binding event induces a conformational change that shifts the enzyme into a more active form, increasing its affinity for the substrate or its catalytic rate. This is the opposite of allosteric (noncompetitive) inhibition.
facts.visualDescription: An enzyme with an activator molecule binding to a secondary site, causing the active site to open up and become more accessible

facts.quizQuestion: What regulatory mechanism has the end product of a metabolic pathway inhibit an earlier enzyme in the same pathway, preventing overproduction?
facts.explanation: Feedback inhibition (also called end-product inhibition) is an elegant regulatory strategy where the final product of a metabolic pathway inhibits an allosteric enzyme earlier in the same pathway. For example, isoleucine inhibits threonine deaminase, the first enzyme in its own synthesis pathway. This prevents wasteful overproduction.
facts.visualDescription: A metabolic pathway diagram with arrows from the final product looping back with an inhibition symbol to an early enzyme

facts.correctAnswer: Competitive: active site; Noncompetitive: allosteric site
acceptableAlternatives: competitive inhibition can be overcome by more substrate; noncompetitive cannot
facts.quizQuestion: How do competitive and noncompetitive inhibition differ in binding location and reversibility by substrate concentration?
facts.explanation: Competitive inhibitors bind the active site and mimic the substrate — adding more substrate displaces the inhibitor. Noncompetitive inhibitors bind the allosteric site and change the enzyme's shape — adding more substrate has no effect because the active site geometry is already altered. This distinction is a key concept on the AP Biology exam.
facts.visualDescription: Two enzyme diagrams side by side: one with an inhibitor at the active site (competitive) and one with an inhibitor at a side site causing shape change (noncompetitive)
distractors: Both types bind the active site
Both can be overcome by more substrate
Competitive binds allosteric site; noncompetitive binds active site
Neither type can be overcome by more substrate
Noncompetitive blocks active site; competitive changes shape
Both types permanently inactivate the enzyme
Competitive inhibition cannot be overcome; noncompetitive can

facts.correctAnswer: Rate increases until saturation at Vmax
acceptableAlternatives: increases proportionally then plateaus at saturation
facts.quizQuestion: How does increasing substrate concentration affect enzyme reaction rate — and what happens when all active sites are occupied?
facts.explanation: As substrate concentration increases, reaction rate increases proportionally because more enzyme active sites are occupied. However, once all active sites are saturated (occupied simultaneously), the rate levels off at Vmax — the maximum rate for that enzyme concentration. This Michaelis-Menten saturation kinetics curve is a classic AP Biology graph.
facts.visualDescription: A Michaelis-Menten hyperbolic curve showing reaction rate rising steeply then leveling off at a horizontal plateau (Vmax)
distractors: Rate immediately plateaus regardless of substrate
Rate follows a sigmoidal curve always
Rate is independent of substrate concentration
Rate decreases due to product inhibition at high substrate
Rate depends only on enzyme concentration

facts.quizQuestion: What is the primary energy currency of cells, whose hydrolysis to ADP + Pi releases ~30.5 kJ/mol and powers virtually all cellular work?
facts.explanation: ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is the universal energy currency of living cells. The energy released when ATP is hydrolyzed to ADP and inorganic phosphate (~30.5 kJ/mol under standard conditions) is harnessed to drive endergonic reactions, active transport, muscle contraction, and other cellular work.
facts.visualDescription: A structural diagram of ATP showing the adenosine base, ribose sugar, and three phosphate groups with a lightning bolt between the last two phosphates

facts.quizQuestion: How much free energy (kJ/mol) is released when one ATP is hydrolyzed to ADP + Pi under standard conditions?
facts.explanation: ATP hydrolysis releases ~30.5 kJ/mol (7.3 kcal/mol) under standard conditions. This energy is harnessed to power endergonic cellular processes through energy coupling. Under actual cellular conditions, the value may be higher (~50–60 kJ/mol) due to low product concentrations.
facts.visualDescription: A chemical equation showing ATP → ADP + Pi with an energy release arrow of 30.5 kJ/mol

facts.quizQuestion: What is a chemical reaction that releases free energy (ΔG < 0) and is thermodynamically spontaneous — able to proceed without additional energy input?
facts.explanation: An exergonic reaction releases free energy (ΔG < 0) as products have less free energy than reactants. These reactions are thermodynamically spontaneous, though they may still require activation energy to begin. Cellular respiration is an exergonic process; ATP hydrolysis is exergonic (ΔG ≈ −30.5 kJ/mol).
facts.visualDescription: An energy diagram showing reactants at a higher energy level than products, with the difference labeled as energy released (ΔG < 0)

facts.quizQuestion: What is a chemical reaction that requires free energy input (ΔG > 0) and is non-spontaneous — requiring energy coupling to proceed?
facts.explanation: An endergonic reaction requires an input of free energy (ΔG > 0) because products have more free energy than reactants. These reactions are thermodynamically non-spontaneous and must be driven by coupling to exergonic reactions (like ATP hydrolysis). Photosynthesis and protein synthesis are endergonic processes.
facts.visualDescription: An energy diagram showing reactants at a lower energy level than products, with an upward arrow labeled 'energy input required (ΔG > 0)'

facts.quizQuestion: What is the linking of an exergonic reaction (like ATP hydrolysis) to an endergonic reaction, driving cellular work that would not otherwise occur?
facts.explanation: Energy coupling is how cells use the free energy released by exergonic reactions (typically ATP hydrolysis) to power endergonic reactions that build molecules, move substances against gradients, or do mechanical work. The cell acts as an energy broker — capturing energy from catabolism and redirecting it to drive anabolism.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing ATP hydrolysis on the left (energy releasing) with an arrow connecting to a protein synthesis reaction on the right (energy requiring)

facts.quizQuestion: What thermodynamic quantity measures usable energy in a system (ΔG = ΔH − TΔS), where a negative value indicates a spontaneous (exergonic) reaction?
facts.explanation: Gibbs free energy (ΔG) predicts whether a reaction will occur spontaneously. The equation ΔG = ΔH − TΔS combines enthalpy (heat content) and entropy (disorder) at a given temperature. Negative ΔG = exergonic (spontaneous); positive ΔG = endergonic (non-spontaneous); ΔG = 0 at equilibrium.
facts.visualDescription: A formula diagram showing ΔG = ΔH − TΔS with each component labeled and two energy diagrams showing negative vs positive ΔG

facts.quizQuestion: Which law of thermodynamics states that energy cannot be created or destroyed — only converted from one form to another?
facts.explanation: The first law of thermodynamics (law of conservation of energy) states that the total energy of a closed system is constant. Energy can be transformed from chemical to thermal to mechanical, but the total amount never changes. This explains why cells must continuously acquire energy from external sources (food or light) to do work.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing energy converting between forms (chemical → kinetic → heat) with 'total energy constant' written across all

facts.quizQuestion: Which law states that every energy transformation increases the entropy of the universe, and no energy conversion is 100% efficient?
facts.explanation: The second law of thermodynamics states that any energy transformation increases the total entropy (disorder) of the universe. Some energy is always lost as heat, meaning no process is perfectly efficient. This is why cells must constantly take in energy from food or sunlight — they are constantly 'losing' usable energy to entropy.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing energy flowing through a process with some energy arrows converting to scattered heat arrows (entropy) and the universe disorder increasing

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes metabolic processes that build complex molecules from simpler ones, requiring an energy input — e.g., protein synthesis and DNA replication?
facts.explanation: Anabolism refers to biosynthetic metabolic reactions that build large, complex molecules from smaller subunits. These reactions require energy input (are endergonic) and are powered by ATP. Protein synthesis (amino acids → proteins), DNA replication, and gluconeogenesis (glucose synthesis) are all anabolic processes.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing small molecules (amino acids) being assembled into a large protein with ATP providing energy for each bond

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes metabolic processes that break complex molecules into simpler ones, releasing energy — e.g., glycolysis and cellular respiration?
facts.explanation: Catabolism refers to metabolic reactions that break down complex molecules into simpler components, releasing free energy in the process (exergonic). This energy is captured in ATP. Glycolysis (glucose → pyruvate), the citric acid cycle, and cellular respiration are all catabolic pathways.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing a large starch molecule being broken down into glucose units with energy arrows being released and captured as ATP

facts.quizQuestion: What is the addition of a phosphate group to a molecule — used to store chemical potential energy when ADP is converted to ATP?
facts.explanation: Phosphorylation is the attachment of a phosphate group (−PO₄) to a molecule. When ADP is phosphorylated to ATP, energy is stored in the high-energy phosphate bond. Phosphorylation of proteins by kinases also regulates enzyme activity. Substrate-level and oxidative phosphorylation are the two main ATP-producing phosphorylation types.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing ADP + Pi combining to form ATP with an arrow indicating energy storage in the new phosphate bond

facts.correctAnswer: Potential energy is stored energy; kinetic energy is energy of motion
acceptableAlternatives: potential: stored chemical bonds; kinetic: motion of molecules
facts.quizQuestion: How do potential energy and kinetic energy differ — and how do cells interconvert these forms in processes like ATP use?
facts.explanation: Potential energy is stored energy with the capacity to do work (chemical bonds in ATP, glucose). Kinetic energy is energy actively doing work (heat, molecular movement). Cells constantly interconvert these forms — breaking chemical bonds (potential) releases kinetic energy that drives cellular work, and anabolism captures kinetic energy back into chemical bonds.
facts.visualDescription: Two-panel diagram: left shows a coiled spring (potential energy) and right shows it released and moving an object (kinetic energy), with ATP → ADP as the biological analog
distractors: Potential is kinetic energy in motion; kinetic is stored energy
Both are the same type of energy at the molecular level
Potential energy is heat; kinetic energy is chemical bonds
Only kinetic energy is relevant to cell biology
Potential energy is always greater than kinetic energy in cells
Kinetic energy drives anabolism; potential drives catabolism
ATP stores kinetic energy in its motion
Potential energy is entropy; kinetic energy is enthalpy

facts.quizQuestion: What is the overall equation of photosynthesis, showing the conversion of carbon dioxide and water to glucose and oxygen using light energy?
facts.explanation: The overall photosynthesis equation is: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Carbon dioxide from the air and water from the soil are combined using light energy to produce glucose (stored chemical energy) and oxygen as a byproduct. This equation summarizes two distinct stages: the light reactions and the Calvin cycle.
facts.visualDescription: A clean diagram showing CO₂ and H₂O entering a chloroplast with light, and glucose and O₂ exiting

facts.quizQuestion: Where in the chloroplast do the light reactions occur — the stage where light energy is converted to ATP and NADPH?
facts.explanation: The light reactions take place in the thylakoid membranes within the chloroplast. The photosystems, electron transport chain, and ATP synthase are all embedded in these membranes. Light energy absorbed by photosystems is converted to chemical energy (ATP and NADPH) and oxygen is released.
facts.visualDescription: A cross-section of a chloroplast with the thylakoid membranes highlighted in green and labeled 'light reactions occur here'

facts.quizQuestion: Where in the chloroplast does the Calvin cycle occur — the light-independent stage that uses ATP and NADPH to fix CO₂?
facts.explanation: The Calvin cycle (light-independent reactions) takes place in the stroma of the chloroplast — the fluid-filled space surrounding the thylakoids. The Calvin cycle enzymes (including RuBisCO) are dissolved in the stroma, where they use the ATP and NADPH produced by the light reactions to fix CO₂ into organic molecules.
facts.visualDescription: A chloroplast cross-section with the stroma labeled as the fluid region surrounding the grana, with Calvin cycle arrows shown there

facts.quizQuestion: Which photosystem absorbs light at 680nm, acts FIRST in the light reactions, and catalyzes photolysis — the splitting of water to release O₂?
facts.explanation: Photosystem II (P680) is the first photosystem in the linear electron flow of the light reactions (despite the naming paradox). It absorbs light at 680nm and uses that energy to split water molecules (photolysis), releasing oxygen as a byproduct, hydrogen ions (H⁺), and high-energy electrons to replace those energized by light.
facts.visualDescription: A thylakoid membrane diagram with PSII labeled as the first stop, showing water being split into O₂, H⁺, and electrons entering the system

facts.quizQuestion: Which photosystem absorbs light at 700nm, acts SECOND in the light reactions, and uses energized electrons to reduce NADP⁺ to NADPH?
facts.explanation: Photosystem I (P700) acts after Photosystem II in the linear electron flow. It absorbs light at 700nm and re-energizes electrons arriving from the electron transport chain. These high-energy electrons are then used by NADP⁺ reductase to reduce NADP⁺ to NADPH, which carries the energy to the Calvin cycle.
facts.visualDescription: A thylakoid membrane diagram with PSI labeled as the second photosystem, showing electrons arriving from ETC and NADPH being produced

facts.quizQuestion: What is the splitting of water molecules by light energy at Photosystem II, which releases O₂, H⁺ (for the proton gradient), and electrons to replace those energized by light?
facts.explanation: Photolysis (water splitting) occurs at Photosystem II. Light energy powers the oxidation of water (2H₂O → O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻). The released oxygen becomes the O₂ we breathe; the H⁺ ions contribute to the proton gradient driving ATP synthesis; and the electrons replace those ejected from P680 by light.
facts.visualDescription: A close-up of Photosystem II showing water molecules being split, with O₂ bubbles rising and electrons entering the reaction center

facts.quizQuestion: What is the light-driven synthesis of ATP from ADP + Pi in the thylakoid membrane, powered by the proton gradient generated by the electron transport chain?
facts.explanation: Photophosphorylation is the production of ATP using light energy — specifically through the proton gradient created when electrons flow through the photosynthetic electron transport chain. H⁺ ions pumped into the thylakoid lumen flow back through ATP synthase, powering ATP synthesis. It is the photosynthetic equivalent of oxidative phosphorylation in respiration.
facts.visualDescription: A thylakoid membrane cross-section showing H⁺ ions flowing from the lumen through ATP synthase into the stroma and ATP being produced

facts.quizQuestion: In chloroplasts, what process describes H⁺ ions moving down their concentration gradient from the thylakoid lumen into the stroma through ATP synthase, driving ATP production?
facts.explanation: Chemiosmosis in chloroplasts is the movement of hydrogen ions (H⁺) from the high-concentration thylakoid lumen to the lower-concentration stroma through the ATP synthase enzyme. The flow of H⁺ rotates the enzyme, which powers ATP synthesis. This is the same mechanism used in mitochondria but with the gradient created by the photosynthetic ETC.
facts.visualDescription: A thylakoid membrane diagram with H⁺ ions concentrated inside the lumen (labeled high concentration) flowing out through ATP synthase to produce ATP in the stroma
distractors: Photophosphorylation (the term for the whole process)

facts.quizQuestion: What series of protein complexes in the thylakoid membrane between PSII and PSI passes electrons and uses the released energy to pump H⁺ into the thylakoid lumen?
facts.explanation: The photosynthetic electron transport chain (ETC) in the thylakoid membrane connects PSII and PSI. As high-energy electrons pass through the chain (via plastoquinone, cytochrome b6f complex, and plastocyanin), they release energy that is used to pump H⁺ ions into the thylakoid lumen, building the proton gradient that drives ATP synthesis.
facts.visualDescription: A thylakoid membrane diagram showing electrons jumping from PSII through PQ, cytochrome b6f, and plastocyanin to PSI, with H⁺ being pumped into the lumen at each step

facts.quizQuestion: Which primary photosynthetic pigment absorbs red (~680nm) and blue-violet (~430nm) light and is found in both photosystems, directly driving the light reactions?
facts.explanation: Chlorophyll a is the primary photosynthetic pigment and the only pigment that directly participates in the light reactions (all other pigments are accessory). It is found in the reaction centers of both photosystems. It absorbs red (~680nm) and blue-violet (~430nm) light, which is why leaves appear green — green light is reflected, not absorbed.
facts.visualDescription: Absorption spectrum graph showing chlorophyll a with two peaks: one in the blue-violet region (~430nm) and one in the red region (~680nm)

facts.quizQuestion: Which accessory photosynthetic pigment absorbs blue (~450nm) and orange-red (~640nm) light, then transfers that energy to chlorophyll a?
facts.explanation: Chlorophyll b is an accessory pigment that extends the range of wavelengths a plant can use for photosynthesis. It absorbs blue (~450nm) and orange-red (~640nm) light and transfers that captured energy to chlorophyll a via resonance energy transfer. Chlorophyll b is found only in higher plants and green algae.
facts.visualDescription: An absorption spectrum graph showing chlorophyll b's two absorption peaks at 450nm and 640nm, slightly shifted from chlorophyll a's peaks

facts.quizQuestion: What class of accessory photosynthetic pigments (e.g., beta-carotene, xanthophylls) absorbs blue-green light, transfers energy to chlorophyll a, and protects against photo-oxidative damage?
facts.explanation: Carotenoids are yellow, orange, and red pigments that serve as accessory light-harvesting molecules and photoprotectors in chloroplasts. They absorb blue-green light wavelengths (400–500nm) not efficiently absorbed by chlorophylls and transfer the energy to chlorophyll a. They also quench harmful singlet oxygen species, protecting the photosystems from oxidative damage.
facts.visualDescription: Autumn leaves illustration showing orange and yellow carotenoid pigments becoming visible as chlorophyll breaks down, plus a diagram showing energy transfer to chlorophyll a

facts.quizQuestion: What three products are produced by the light reactions of photosynthesis — two of which power the Calvin cycle and one of which is released as a byproduct?
facts.explanation: The light reactions produce three key outputs: (1) ATP — made by photophosphorylation, used to power the Calvin cycle; (2) NADPH — an electron carrier that powers carbon reduction in the Calvin cycle; and (3) O₂ — a byproduct of water splitting (photolysis) that is released into the atmosphere.
facts.visualDescription: A light reactions summary box showing inputs (light + H₂O) with arrows to three outputs: ATP, NADPH, and O₂ with arrows indicating their destinations (Calvin cycle or atmosphere)

facts.quizQuestion: What is a stack of thylakoid disks within a chloroplast called — the structure whose membranes contain photosystems and ETC components?
facts.explanation: A granum (plural: grana) is a stack of flattened thylakoid membranes within the chloroplast, resembling a stack of coins. These stacked membranes dramatically increase the surface area available for the light reactions. The photosystems, electron transport chain complexes, and ATP synthase are all embedded in the thylakoid membranes of the grana.
facts.visualDescription: A chloroplast cross-section with coin-stack structures labeled 'grana' and the flat thylakoid disks labeled individually, connected by unstacked 'stroma lamellae'
distractors: Thylakoid lumen (the interior of each thylakoid disk)
Stroma lamellae (flat thylakoids connecting adjacent grana)
Plastid (general term for any chloroplast-related organelle)
Cristae (inner mitochondrial membrane folds — not in chloroplasts)
Mitochondrial matrix (interior of the mitochondrion, not chloroplast)
Intermembrane space (region between inner and outer membranes)

facts.correctAnswer: Absorption: pigment absorbance; Action: photosynthesis rate
acceptableAlternatives: absorption: what pigment absorbs; action: what drives photosynthesis rate
facts.quizQuestion: How do the absorption spectrum and action spectrum of photosynthesis differ, and what does their close match indicate?
facts.explanation: The absorption spectrum shows which wavelengths of light are absorbed by a pigment (measured in the lab). The action spectrum shows which wavelengths drive the highest rate of photosynthesis in intact plants. Their close correlation was key evidence that chlorophyll — not any other molecule — is responsible for driving photosynthesis.
facts.visualDescription: Two overlaid graphs: one showing chlorophyll absorption peaks and one showing photosynthesis rate by wavelength, with the curves closely aligned
distractors: Absorption spectrum shows photosynthesis rate; action spectrum shows pigment color
Action spectrum is always broader than absorption spectrum
The two spectra never match in real plants
Absorption spectrum is for animals; action spectrum for plants
Only carotenoids contribute to the action spectrum
Action spectrum peaks at green wavelengths; absorption spectrum at red
The two are inverse of each other

facts.quizQuestion: At what wavelength (nm) does Photosystem II most efficiently absorb light?
facts.explanation: Photosystem II is called P680 because its reaction center chlorophyll absorbs light most efficiently at 680nm — in the red region of the visible spectrum. This is why red light is highly effective at driving the light reactions of photosynthesis.
facts.visualDescription: The visible light spectrum from 400-700nm with an arrow marking 680nm in the red region, labeled 'P680 / PSII'

facts.quizQuestion: At what wavelength (nm) does Photosystem I most efficiently absorb light?
facts.explanation: Photosystem I is called P700 because its reaction center chlorophyll absorbs light most efficiently at 700nm — in the far-red region of the visible spectrum. After receiving electrons from the ETC, P700 re-energizes them with light energy and passes them to NADP⁺ reductase to produce NADPH.
facts.visualDescription: The visible light spectrum from 400-700nm with an arrow marking 700nm at the far-red end, labeled 'P700 / PSI'

facts.quizQuestion: What reduced electron carrier is produced by Photosystem I during the light reactions and carries high-energy electrons to the Calvin cycle to power carbon fixation?
facts.explanation: NADPH (the reduced form of NADP⁺) is produced when PSI passes high-energy electrons to NADP⁺ reductase. It serves as the reducing power for the Calvin cycle — donating electrons to reduce 3-PGA to G3P. NADPH in photosynthesis is analogous to NADH in cellular respiration, but with an extra phosphate group.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing NADP⁺ + electrons → NADPH at Photosystem I, then NADPH traveling to the stroma for the Calvin cycle

facts.quizQuestion: What rotary enzyme embedded in the thylakoid membrane synthesizes ATP as H⁺ ions flow from the lumen into the stroma, driving rotation of its Fo subunit?
facts.explanation: Chloroplast ATP synthase (also called CF1-CF0 ATPase) is a molecular machine embedded in the thylakoid membrane. H⁺ ions flowing down their electrochemical gradient from the lumen into the stroma rotate the membrane-embedded Fo subunit. This mechanical rotation drives the F1 catalytic subunit to synthesize ATP from ADP + Pi.
facts.visualDescription: A molecular diagram of ATP synthase embedded in the thylakoid membrane, with protons flowing through the Fo rotor and ATP being produced at the F1 head

facts.correctAnswer: PSII acts first; named II because discovered second
acceptableAlternatives: PSII acts first; named second because discovered second
facts.quizQuestion: Although Photosystem II is numbered '2,' when does it actually act in the light reactions — and why is it named 'II' if it acts first?
facts.explanation: Photosystem II acts FIRST in the light reactions despite its name. It was numbered II because it was discovered after Photosystem I. The naming reflects order of discovery, not order of function. This is a classic AP Biology trick question — students often assume PSII acts second simply because of the number.
facts.visualDescription: A flow diagram: PSII → Electron Transport Chain → PSI → NADPH, with a note 'PSII was discovered SECOND but acts FIRST'
distractors: PSI acts before PSII in linear electron flow
PSII and PSI act simultaneously in all plants
PSII acts second in cyclic photophosphorylation only
The numbering reflects reaction order, not discovery
PSII acts second because electrons must gain energy at PSI first
PSI produces O₂ while PSII produces NADPH
They alternate in action every other turn
PSII is active at night; PSI is active during the day

facts.quizQuestion: What light-independent reaction cycle occurs in the chloroplast stroma, using ATP and NADPH from the light reactions to fix CO₂ into G3P?
facts.explanation: The Calvin cycle (also called the light-independent reactions or Calvin-Benson cycle) takes place in the stroma of the chloroplast. It uses the ATP and NADPH produced by the light reactions to fix inorganic CO₂ into organic molecules (ultimately G3P), which can then be used to synthesize glucose and other carbohydrates.
facts.visualDescription: A circular diagram showing the three stages of the Calvin cycle (fixation → reduction → regeneration) with CO₂ entering and G3P exiting

facts.quizQuestion: What is the first stage of the Calvin cycle in which RuBisCO attaches CO₂ to RuBP (5C) to produce two molecules of 3-PGA (3C), converting inorganic CO₂ to organic form?
facts.explanation: Carbon fixation is the first stage of the Calvin cycle. RuBisCO catalyzes the carboxylation of RuBP (a 5-carbon molecule) with CO₂ to produce an unstable 6-carbon intermediate that immediately splits into two molecules of 3-phosphoglycerate (3-PGA, 3C). This is the critical step that converts inorganic carbon from the atmosphere into organic form.
facts.visualDescription: Stage 1 of Calvin cycle diagram: CO₂ + RuBP (5C) → two 3-PGA molecules (3C each) with RuBisCO labeled as the enzyme

facts.quizQuestion: What enzyme catalyzes carbon fixation in the Calvin cycle by combining CO₂ with RuBP — and is considered the most abundant enzyme on Earth?
facts.explanation: RuBisCO (ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase) is the enzyme responsible for carbon fixation in the Calvin cycle. It is the most abundant protein on Earth, comprising about 25% of the protein in chloroplast stroma. Despite its abundance, RuBisCO is a relatively slow enzyme (~3 CO₂ per second) and can mistakenly react with O₂ (causing photorespiration).
facts.visualDescription: A large protein complex labeled 'RuBisCO' surrounded by CO₂ molecules in the chloroplast stroma, with RuBP entering and 3-PGA exiting

facts.quizQuestion: What 5-carbon molecule serves as the CO₂ acceptor in the Calvin cycle, is combined with CO₂ by RuBisCO, and is regenerated in the final stage of the cycle?
facts.explanation: RuBP (ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate) is the 5-carbon molecule that acts as the CO₂ acceptor in the Calvin cycle. RuBisCO attaches one CO₂ to RuBP, forming an unstable 6-carbon intermediate that splits into two 3-PGA molecules. The regeneration phase uses ATP to rebuild RuBP from G3P, allowing the cycle to continue.
facts.visualDescription: Calvin cycle diagram with RuBP (5C) highlighted in the circle, with CO₂ entering to produce 3-PGA and the regeneration arrow showing RuBP being rebuilt

facts.quizQuestion: What 3-carbon compound is the FIRST stable product of carbon fixation in C3 plants, formed when RuBisCO combines CO₂ with RuBP?
facts.explanation: 3-phosphoglycerate (3-PGA) is the first stable product of carbon fixation in the Calvin cycle and gives C3 plants their name. When RuBisCO combines one CO₂ with one RuBP (5C), the unstable 6C intermediate immediately splits into two molecules of 3-PGA (3C each). 3-PGA is then reduced to G3P using ATP and NADPH.
facts.visualDescription: The fixation stage of the Calvin cycle showing CO₂ + RuBP → 3-PGA, with '3-PGA' circled and labeled as the 'first stable product'

facts.quizQuestion: What 3-carbon sugar is the direct carbohydrate output of the Calvin cycle, produced using ATP and NADPH, and used to synthesize glucose, sucrose, and starch?
facts.explanation: G3P (glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate) is the direct product of the Calvin cycle's reduction phase. ATP and NADPH from the light reactions are used to reduce 3-PGA to G3P. Some G3P leaves the cycle to be used for glucose, sucrose, and starch synthesis; the rest is used to regenerate RuBP so the cycle can continue.
facts.visualDescription: Calvin cycle diagram with G3P highlighted exiting the cycle, with arrows showing it going to glucose synthesis and RuBP regeneration

facts.quizQuestion: How many ATP molecules are consumed in 3 turns of the Calvin cycle (fixing 3 CO₂)?
facts.explanation: Per 3 turns of the Calvin cycle (fixing 3 CO₂): 9 ATP are consumed total — 6 ATP in the reduction phase (converting 3-PGA to G3P, 2 ATP per turn) and 3 ATP in the regeneration phase (rebuilding 3 RuBP, 1 ATP per turn). Along with 9 ATP, 6 NADPH are also consumed to yield 1 net G3P.
facts.visualDescription: A Calvin cycle stoichiometry table showing 3 turns, with 9 ATP and 6 NADPH inputs clearly labeled

facts.quizQuestion: How many NADPH molecules are consumed in 3 turns of the Calvin cycle (fixing 3 CO₂)?
facts.explanation: Per 3 turns of the Calvin cycle: 6 NADPH are consumed in the reduction phase. Each NADPH donates 2 electrons to reduce one 3-PGA to G3P. With 6 molecules of 3-PGA (2 per turn × 3 turns), 6 NADPH are needed. Along with 6 NADPH, 9 ATP are also consumed over the same 3 turns.
facts.visualDescription: A Calvin cycle stoichiometry table showing 3 turns, with 6 NADPH consumed in the reduction step highlighted

facts.quizQuestion: What category of plants uses only the Calvin cycle for carbon fixation, producing 3-PGA (3C) as the first stable product — including most plants such as wheat, rice, and soybeans?
facts.explanation: C3 plants are named for the 3-carbon compound (3-PGA) produced when RuBisCO fixes CO₂ directly in the Calvin cycle. This includes most plant species (wheat, rice, soybeans, most trees). C3 plants are less efficient in hot, sunny, dry conditions because RuBisCO can react with O₂ instead of CO₂ (photorespiration), wasting energy.
facts.visualDescription: A world map highlighting temperate regions where C3 plants dominate, with examples: wheat, soybeans, and oak trees labeled

facts.quizQuestion: What category of plants pre-fixes CO₂ as oxaloacetate (4C) in mesophyll cells, then releases CO₂ to bundle-sheath cells for the Calvin cycle — including corn, sugarcane, and sorghum?
facts.explanation: C4 plants first fix CO₂ into oxaloacetate (a 4-carbon compound) in mesophyll cells using PEP carboxylase, then transfer the 4C acid to bundle-sheath cells where CO₂ is released for the Calvin cycle. This concentrates CO₂ around RuBisCO, minimizing photorespiration. C4 photosynthesis is more efficient in hot, sunny, dry conditions than C3.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram of a C4 leaf cross-section showing CO₂ fixation as OAA in mesophyll cells (left) and Calvin cycle in bundle-sheath cells (right)

facts.quizQuestion: Which type of plants opens stomata at NIGHT to fix CO₂ into organic acids, closes stomata during the day, and runs the Calvin cycle during daylight — including cacti, pineapple, and agave?
facts.explanation: CAM (Crassulacean Acid Metabolism) plants are adapted to extremely dry (xeric) environments. They open stomata at night to absorb CO₂ and store it as malate in vacuoles, then close stomata during the day (reducing water loss) and release CO₂ for the Calvin cycle. This temporal separation minimizes water loss while enabling photosynthesis.
facts.visualDescription: A day/night diagram of a CAM plant: stomata closed + Calvin cycle running at day; stomata open + CO₂ fixation at night

facts.quizQuestion: What wasteful process occurs when RuBisCO fixes O₂ instead of CO₂ at high temperatures, producing compounds that must be recycled at an ATP cost — minimized in C4 and CAM plants?
facts.explanation: Photorespiration occurs when O₂ concentration is high relative to CO₂ (e.g., hot, dry days when stomata close). RuBisCO's oxygenase activity fixes O₂ to RuBP instead of CO₂, producing phosphoglycolate (not G3P). Recovering carbons from phosphoglycolate consumes ATP and NADPH, releasing CO₂ without making sugars — a net energy loss. C4 and CAM plants have evolved mechanisms to concentrate CO₂ and minimize this.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing RuBisCO with two arrows: one for CO₂ fixation (labeled 'productive') and one for O₂ fixation (labeled 'photorespiration — wasteful')

facts.correctAnswer: C3: 3-PGA; C4: OAA then bundle sheath; CAM: nighttime CO2
acceptableAlternatives: C3 direct Calvin; C4 spatial separation; CAM temporal separation
facts.quizQuestion: What is the first CO₂ fixation product in C3, C4, and CAM photosynthesis?
facts.explanation: C3 plants: CO₂ directly fixed by RuBisCO in Calvin cycle → 3-PGA (3C); prone to photorespiration. C4 plants: CO₂ first fixed as oxaloacetate (4C) in mesophyll, then concentrated for Calvin cycle in bundle-sheath cells (spatial separation). CAM plants: CO₂ fixed at night into organic acids, released for Calvin cycle during day (temporal separation). Both C4 and CAM concentrate CO₂ around RuBisCO, suppressing photorespiration.
facts.visualDescription: A three-column comparison table for C3, C4, CAM showing: first product, cell type, when stomata open, efficiency in heat
distractors: All three produce G3P directly as the first product
C4 and CAM are identical in their CO₂ concentrating strategy
C3 plants have bundle-sheath cells; C4 do not
CAM plants fix CO₂ during the day in mesophyll only
C4 plants open stomata at night; CAM open during day
C3 plants are more efficient in hot, dry climates than C4
CAM plants use PEP carboxylase as their primary enzyme
C4 plants fix CO₂ in bundle sheath; C3 plants fix in mesophyll

facts.quizQuestion: What is the third stage of the Calvin cycle in which G3P molecules are converted back into RuBP using ATP, allowing the cycle to continue fixing CO₂?
facts.explanation: The regeneration phase is the final stage of the Calvin cycle. Using ATP (but not NADPH), G3P molecules are rearranged and phosphorylated to regenerate RuBP — the CO₂ acceptor molecule. Without this phase, the cycle would stop because there would be no RuBP available to accept new CO₂ molecules. This is why the cycle is called a 'cycle' rather than a linear pathway.
facts.visualDescription: The third stage of the Calvin cycle diagram showing G3P molecules being recycled with ATP input to form RuBP, which then enters the fixation step again

facts.quizQuestion: How many turns of the Calvin cycle are required to produce enough G3P to synthesize one molecule of glucose?
facts.explanation: 6 turns of the Calvin cycle are needed to synthesize one glucose molecule. Each turn fixes 1 CO₂, producing 2 G3P but using 5 of them for RuBP regeneration. So 6 turns fix 6 CO₂, produce 12 G3P total, use 10 for regeneration (6 RuBP), and leave 2 net G3P. These 2 G3P (6 carbons total) combine to form one glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆).
facts.visualDescription: A Calvin cycle stoichiometry chart showing 6 turns: 6 CO₂ in → 12 G3P produced → 10 used for RuBP → 2 G3P net output → 1 glucose

acceptableAlternatives: glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water + ATP
facts.quizQuestion: What is the overall equation for cellular respiration, showing the complete aerobic oxidation of glucose?
facts.explanation: Cellular respiration converts glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) and oxygen into carbon dioxide, water, and approximately 30–32 ATP. The process is the reverse of photosynthesis in terms of reactants and products, but occurs through completely different biochemical pathways — glycolysis, pyruvate oxidation, the citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing glucose and O₂ entering a mitochondrion and CO₂, H₂O, and ATP emerging

facts.quizQuestion: What is the total ATP yield from complete aerobic oxidation of one glucose molecule?
facts.explanation: Modern estimates place the total ATP yield at approximately 30–32 ATP per glucose. Older textbooks cited 36–38, but accounting for the energy cost of transporting NADH from the cytoplasm into the mitochondria (the malate-aspartate shuttle) gives the lower, more accurate figure. The breakdown: ~2 from glycolysis, ~2 from the citric acid cycle, and ~26–28 from oxidative phosphorylation.
facts.visualDescription: A summary bar chart showing ATP contributions from each stage of cellular respiration, totaling 30–32

acceptableAlternatives: glycolysis, pyruvate oxidation, citric acid cycle, ETC
three stages: glycolysis, TCA cycle, oxidative phosphorylation
facts.quizQuestion: What are the three main stages of cellular respiration, in order from cytoplasm to inner mitochondrial membrane?
facts.explanation: Cellular respiration has three main stages. Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm and splits glucose into pyruvate. Pyruvate oxidation and the citric acid cycle (Krebs cycle) occur in the mitochondrial matrix and complete glucose oxidation. Oxidative phosphorylation occurs at the inner mitochondrial membrane and produces the majority of ATP via the electron transport chain and ATP synthase.
facts.visualDescription: A flowchart showing glucose → glycolysis (cytoplasm) → pyruvate oxidation → TCA cycle (both in matrix) → ETC/ATP synthase (inner membrane)

facts.quizQuestion: What type of cellular respiration requires oxygen as the final electron acceptor and produces approximately 30–32 ATP per glucose?
facts.explanation: Aerobic respiration requires oxygen as the terminal electron acceptor in the electron transport chain. Oxygen's high electronegativity makes it ideal for this role — it accepts electrons at Complex IV and is reduced to water. Without oxygen, the ETC stalls and only 2 ATP (from glycolysis alone) are available. Aerobic respiration is approximately 15–16× more efficient than fermentation.
facts.visualDescription: A mitochondrion with O₂ entering at Complex IV on the inner membrane and H₂O exiting, with ATP being made by ATP synthase

facts.quizQuestion: What is the third stage of cellular respiration, in which ATP synthase uses a proton gradient generated by the electron transport chain to synthesize ATP?
facts.explanation: Oxidative phosphorylation is the process by which ATP is made using the energy of the proton gradient established by the ETC. As electrons move through Complexes I, III, and IV, protons are pumped from the matrix into the intermembrane space. These protons then flow back through ATP synthase (Complex V), driving rotation that synthesizes ATP. This stage produces the majority of ATP — approximately 26–28 per glucose.
facts.visualDescription: The inner mitochondrial membrane with four protein complexes, protons flowing from left to right, and ATP synthase at the end producing ATP

facts.quizQuestion: What is the first stage of cellular respiration — occurring in the cytoplasm — that splits one glucose (6C) into two pyruvate (3C) with a net yield of 2 ATP and 2 NADH?
facts.explanation: Glycolysis (from Greek: glykys = sweet, lysis = splitting) is the metabolic pathway that breaks glucose into two pyruvate molecules. It occurs in the cytoplasm of ALL cells — prokaryotes and eukaryotes alike — and does not require oxygen, making it evolutionarily ancient. The investment phase consumes 2 ATP; the payoff phase produces 4 ATP and 2 NADH, for a net gain of 2 ATP.
facts.visualDescription: A cytoplasm diagram showing glucose entering, splitting through 10 enzymatic steps into two pyruvate molecules, with ATP and NADH labeled

facts.quizQuestion: Where does glycolysis occur in the cell?
facts.explanation: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm (cytosol) of the cell — outside the mitochondria. This location means glycolysis works in both prokaryotes (which lack mitochondria) and eukaryotes. Because it does not require oxygen or a membrane, glycolysis is the universal energy-generating pathway available to virtually all living organisms.
facts.visualDescription: A eukaryotic cell with the cytoplasm highlighted and a label showing glycolysis occurring outside the mitochondrion

facts.quizQuestion: What is the net ATP yield from glycolysis per glucose molecule?
facts.explanation: Glycolysis has two phases. The investment phase consumes 2 ATP to phosphorylate glucose and fructose-6-phosphate. The payoff phase generates 4 ATP by substrate-level phosphorylation (from 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate and phosphoenolpyruvate). Net result: 4 produced − 2 consumed = 2 net ATP per glucose molecule.
facts.visualDescription: A glycolysis diagram with 2 ATP arrows pointing down (investment) and 4 ATP arrows pointing up (payoff), net = 2 highlighted

facts.quizQuestion: How many NADH molecules are produced per glucose molecule during glycolysis?
facts.explanation: During the payoff phase of glycolysis, each of the two 3-carbon molecules (glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate) is oxidized by NAD⁺, producing 1 NADH each. Because 1 glucose yields 2 three-carbon molecules, the total is 2 NADH per glucose. These NADH molecules will later donate their electrons to the ETC for additional ATP production.
facts.visualDescription: Glycolysis diagram highlighting the two oxidation steps where PGAL is converted to 1,3-BPG, with NAD⁺ being reduced to NADH at each step

facts.quizQuestion: What is the direct synthesis of ATP by transfer of a phosphate group from a substrate molecule to ADP — the mechanism used in glycolysis and the citric acid cycle?
facts.explanation: Substrate-level phosphorylation is distinct from oxidative phosphorylation. It requires no membrane or proton gradient — a phosphate group is directly transferred from a high-energy phosphorylated substrate (like 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate or phosphoenolpyruvate) to ADP to make ATP. Glycolysis produces 4 ATP this way; the citric acid cycle produces 2 more (one GTP per turn × 2 turns).
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing a phosphate group being transferred directly from a substrate molecule (1,3-BPG) to ADP, forming ATP

facts.quizQuestion: What is the first half of glycolysis called, in which 2 ATP are consumed to phosphorylate glucose and prepare it for cleavage into two 3-carbon molecules?
facts.explanation: The investment phase of glycolysis 'spends' 2 ATP to activate glucose. Hexokinase first phosphorylates glucose to glucose-6-phosphate, then phosphofructokinase (the key regulatory enzyme) phosphorylates fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate. This destabilizes the 6-carbon sugar so aldolase can cleave it into two glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (PGAL) molecules for the payoff phase.
facts.visualDescription: First half of glycolysis showing glucose → glucose-6-P → fructose-6-P → fructose-1,6-bisphosphate → two PGAL, with 2 ATP consumed labeled

facts.quizQuestion: What is the second half of glycolysis called, in which each 3-carbon molecule is oxidized to pyruvate, generating 4 ATP and 2 NADH per glucose?
facts.explanation: In the payoff phase, each of the two PGAL molecules undergoes oxidation (reducing NAD⁺ to NADH) and then two rounds of substrate-level phosphorylation to produce 2 ATP each. Per glucose: 4 ATP are made and 2 NADH are produced. Subtracting the 2 ATP spent in the investment phase gives the net yield of 2 ATP. Pyruvate is the final product.
facts.visualDescription: Second half of glycolysis showing two PGAL molecules each being oxidized (producing NADH) and phosphorylated twice (producing 4 ATP total) to yield two pyruvate

facts.quizQuestion: What is the 3-carbon end product of glycolysis, which enters the mitochondrial matrix under aerobic conditions or is converted to lactate or ethanol under anaerobic conditions?
facts.explanation: Pyruvate is the 3-carbon organic acid that is the final product of glycolysis. Its fate depends on oxygen availability: with oxygen, pyruvate is transported into the mitochondrial matrix and oxidized to acetyl-CoA (pyruvate oxidation). Without oxygen, pyruvate is converted to lactate (in animals and some bacteria) or to ethanol + CO₂ (in yeast) to regenerate NAD⁺ for continued glycolysis.
facts.visualDescription: A fork in the road diagram showing pyruvate going one way to the mitochondrion (aerobic) and another way to lactate or ethanol (anaerobic)

facts.quizQuestion: What process converts each pyruvate (3C) to acetyl-CoA (2C) + CO₂ in the mitochondrial matrix, also producing 1 NADH per pyruvate?
facts.explanation: Pyruvate oxidation is a transition step connecting glycolysis to the citric acid cycle. The pyruvate dehydrogenase complex catalyzes three reactions: decarboxylation (removing CO₂), oxidation (reducing NAD⁺ to NADH), and attachment of the remaining 2-carbon acetyl group to coenzyme A to form acetyl-CoA. Since each glucose produces 2 pyruvates, pyruvate oxidation yields 2 acetyl-CoA, 2 CO₂, and 2 NADH per glucose.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram in the mitochondrial matrix showing pyruvate (3C) entering the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex, CO₂ exiting, NADH being produced, and acetyl-CoA (2C) exiting

facts.quizQuestion: Where does pyruvate oxidation occur in the cell?
facts.explanation: After glycolysis, pyruvate is transported from the cytoplasm across both mitochondrial membranes into the mitochondrial matrix. The matrix is the innermost compartment of the mitochondrion, where the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex is located. The matrix is also where the citric acid cycle enzymes reside, making it the hub of aerobic metabolism.
facts.visualDescription: A mitochondrion cross-section with the matrix compartment highlighted, showing pyruvate entering from the cytoplasm and acetyl-CoA entering the TCA cycle

facts.quizQuestion: What is the 2-carbon molecule attached to coenzyme A — produced from pyruvate oxidation — that enters the citric acid cycle?
facts.explanation: Acetyl-CoA is the key entry molecule for the citric acid cycle. The acetyl group (2 carbons) from pyruvate is attached to coenzyme A (CoA) during pyruvate oxidation. Acetyl-CoA can also be produced from fatty acid oxidation (beta-oxidation) and some amino acid catabolism, making it a central metabolic hub. In the citric acid cycle, the acetyl group combines with oxaloacetate to form citrate.
facts.visualDescription: The structure of acetyl-CoA showing a 2-carbon acetyl group bonded to the sulfur of coenzyme A, with an arrow showing it entering the citric acid cycle

facts.quizQuestion: How many NADH molecules are produced by pyruvate oxidation per glucose molecule (counting both pyruvates)?
facts.explanation: Each pyruvate oxidation produces 1 NADH (from the oxidation step in the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex reaction). Since glycolysis produces 2 pyruvates per glucose, pyruvate oxidation yields a total of 2 NADH per glucose. These 2 NADH, along with the 2 NADH from glycolysis and 6 NADH from the citric acid cycle, contribute to the proton gradient for ATP synthesis.
facts.visualDescription: Two pyruvate molecules each being oxidized, with 1 NADH emerging from each, totaling 2 NADH per glucose

facts.quizQuestion: What large multi-enzyme complex in the mitochondrial matrix catalyzes pyruvate oxidation, converting pyruvate to acetyl-CoA while releasing CO₂ and producing NADH?
facts.explanation: The pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC) is one of the largest known enzyme complexes, composed of three enzymatic subunits (E1, E2, E3) and five coenzymes (TPP, lipoic acid, CoA, FAD, and NAD⁺). It is tightly regulated — inhibited by its products (NADH, acetyl-CoA) and activated by NAD⁺ and CoA. PDC deficiency is a metabolic disease causing neurological problems due to impaired energy production.
facts.visualDescription: A large multi-subunit enzyme complex in the mitochondrial matrix with pyruvate entering, CO₂ exiting one side, NADH produced, and acetyl-CoA exiting to the TCA cycle

facts.quizQuestion: What is the second stage of aerobic cellular respiration — occurring in the mitochondrial matrix — that oxidizes acetyl-CoA to CO₂ and produces NADH, FADH₂, and ATP per turn?
facts.explanation: The citric acid cycle (also called the Krebs cycle after Hans Krebs who described it in 1937, or the TCA cycle for tricarboxylic acid) is an 8-step cyclic pathway. Each turn oxidizes one acetyl group (2C) completely to CO₂ while reducing electron carriers. Per turn: 3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, 1 ATP (or GTP), and 2 CO₂ are produced. Since 1 glucose yields 2 acetyl-CoA, the cycle turns twice per glucose.
facts.visualDescription: A circular diagram of the 8-step Krebs cycle in the mitochondrial matrix, with acetyl-CoA entering, CO₂ exiting, and NADH/FADH₂/ATP produced at various steps

facts.quizQuestion: Where does the citric acid cycle occur inside the mitochondrion?
facts.explanation: The citric acid cycle enzymes are dissolved in the mitochondrial matrix — the innermost aqueous compartment enclosed by the inner mitochondrial membrane. This is the same compartment where pyruvate oxidation occurs, so acetyl-CoA can flow directly into the cycle. The matrix is also where CO₂ is released and electron carriers (NADH, FADH₂) are produced before passing electrons to the ETC.
facts.visualDescription: A mitochondrion cross-section with the matrix compartment labeled, showing the circular TCA cycle and its enzymes within it

facts.quizQuestion: What is the 4-carbon molecule that begins and ends each turn of the citric acid cycle, combining with acetyl-CoA to form citrate and being regenerated at the end?
facts.explanation: Oxaloacetate (OAA) is the 4-carbon regenerating acceptor of the citric acid cycle. Citrate synthase catalyzes the condensation of oxaloacetate (4C) with acetyl-CoA (2C) to form citrate (6C) at the start of each turn. Through eight reactions, the 6-carbon citrate is oxidized, releasing 2 CO₂ and regenerating oxaloacetate. This cyclic regeneration is why the pathway is called a 'cycle.'
facts.visualDescription: The citric acid cycle circle with oxaloacetate highlighted at both the start and end, with acetyl-CoA entering at the top and citrate forming

facts.quizQuestion: What is the 6-carbon molecule formed when acetyl-CoA (2C) combines with oxaloacetate (4C) at the start of each turn of the citric acid cycle, giving the cycle its name?
facts.explanation: Citrate (the ionized form of citric acid) gives the citric acid cycle its name. Citrate synthase catalyzes the condensation reaction combining the 2-carbon acetyl group from acetyl-CoA with the 4-carbon oxaloacetate to form 6-carbon citrate. Citrate is then isomerized to isocitrate, which begins the series of oxidative decarboxylation reactions that release CO₂ and produce NADH.
facts.visualDescription: The first step of the TCA cycle showing acetyl-CoA (2C) + oxaloacetate (4C) → citrate (6C) catalyzed by citrate synthase

facts.quizQuestion: How many NADH molecules are produced per single turn of the citric acid cycle?
facts.explanation: Per single turn of the citric acid cycle, 3 NADH are produced: one at the isocitrate dehydrogenase step, one at the alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase step, and one at the malate dehydrogenase step. Additionally, 1 FADH₂ is produced at the succinate dehydrogenase step. Since the cycle turns twice per glucose, the citric acid cycle yields 6 NADH and 2 FADH₂ per glucose.
facts.visualDescription: The TCA cycle with three 'NAD⁺→NADH' arrows highlighted at isocitrate, alpha-ketoglutarate, and malate steps, labeled 3 NADH total per turn

facts.quizQuestion: How many FADH₂ molecules are produced per single turn of the citric acid cycle?
facts.explanation: FADH₂ is produced at a single step in the citric acid cycle: the succinate dehydrogenase reaction (Complex II of the ETC), where succinate is oxidized to fumarate and FAD is reduced to FADH₂. Only 1 FADH₂ is produced per turn, compared to 3 NADH. FADH₂ yields fewer ATP than NADH because it feeds electrons into Complex II, which does not pump protons.
facts.visualDescription: The TCA cycle with the succinate→fumarate step highlighted, showing FAD being reduced to FADH₂, labeled '1 FADH₂ per turn'

facts.quizQuestion: How many CO₂ molecules are released per single turn of the citric acid cycle?
facts.explanation: Two CO₂ molecules are released per turn of the citric acid cycle through two oxidative decarboxylation reactions. The first releases CO₂ when isocitrate is oxidized to alpha-ketoglutarate (by isocitrate dehydrogenase). The second releases CO₂ when alpha-ketoglutarate is converted to succinyl-CoA (by alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase). These two carbons came from acetyl-CoA, accounting for the complete oxidation of the acetyl group.
facts.visualDescription: The TCA cycle with two CO₂ arrows exiting — one at isocitrate and one at alpha-ketoglutarate — labeled as the 2 carbons from acetyl-CoA being released

facts.quizQuestion: What electron carrier is reduced to its high-energy form at the succinate dehydrogenase step of the citric acid cycle, yielding fewer ATP than NADH when it donates electrons to the ETC?
facts.explanation: FAD (flavin adenine dinucleotide) is a coenzyme that acts as an electron carrier. It is reduced to FADH₂ at Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase). Unlike NADH, which feeds electrons into Complex I (pumping 4 protons), FADH₂ feeds electrons directly into Complex II, which does not pump any protons. As a result, FADH₂ generates only ~1.5 ATP compared to ~2.5 ATP for NADH. FAD is also a derivative of riboflavin (vitamin B₂).
facts.visualDescription: The TCA cycle succinate→fumarate step showing FAD accepting two electrons to become FADH₂, with a note indicating fewer ATP produced compared to NADH

facts.correctAnswer: The citric acid cycle turns twice per glucose: 6 NADH, 2 FADH₂, 2 ATP, 4 CO₂ total
facts.quizQuestion: How many times does the citric acid cycle turn per glucose molecule, and what is the total output from those turns?
facts.explanation: Because glycolysis splits one glucose (6C) into two pyruvates (3C each), and each pyruvate oxidation yields one acetyl-CoA (2C), the citric acid cycle must turn twice per glucose to oxidize both acetyl groups. Total output per glucose from two turns: 6 NADH (3×2), 2 FADH₂ (1×2), 2 ATP (1×2), and 4 CO₂ (2×2). These products feed into oxidative phosphorylation.
facts.visualDescription: A summary box showing 'Per glucose: 2 turns × [3 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 ATP + 2 CO₂] = 6 NADH + 2 FADH₂ + 2 ATP + 4 CO₂'
distractors: Once per glucose: 3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, 1 ATP, 2 CO₂
Three times per glucose: 9 NADH total
Twice per glucose: 4 NADH, 2 FADH₂, 2 ATP
Twice per glucose: 3 NADH, 3 FADH₂, 2 ATP

facts.quizQuestion: What electron carrier is reduced from its oxidized form (NAD⁺) during glycolysis and the citric acid cycle, then donates electrons to Complex I of the ETC to drive ATP synthesis?
facts.explanation: NAD⁺ (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide, oxidized form) acts as an electron acceptor, gaining two electrons and one proton to become NADH (the reduced form). NADH is produced in glycolysis (2 per glucose), pyruvate oxidation (2 per glucose), and the citric acid cycle (6 per glucose) — totaling 10 NADH per glucose. Each NADH donates electrons to Complex I of the ETC, contributing to the proton gradient and yielding approximately 2.5 ATP.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing NAD⁺ accepting electrons (becoming NADH) in glycolysis and the TCA cycle, then NADH donating electrons to Complex I of the ETC

facts.quizQuestion: Where are the protein complexes of the electron transport chain located in the mitochondrion?
facts.explanation: The four protein complexes (I–IV) of the electron transport chain are embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane, specifically in its folded regions called cristae. The cristae dramatically increase the surface area of the inner membrane, allowing many ETC complexes and ATP synthase molecules to be packed in, maximizing ATP production capacity. This is why mitochondria in highly active cells (e.g., cardiac muscle) have very dense cristae.
facts.visualDescription: A mitochondrion with the inner membrane (cristae) highlighted, showing four labeled protein complexes (I, II, III, IV) embedded in the membrane
distractors: Mitochondrial matrix (the aqueous interior of the mitochondrion)
Intermembrane space (between the two mitochondrial membranes)
Cytoplasm (outside the mitochondrion, site of glycolysis)
Thylakoid membrane (analogous location in chloroplasts, not mitochondria)
Stroma of the chloroplast (site of the Calvin cycle, not ETC)
Endoplasmic reticulum (membrane for protein and lipid synthesis)
Nucleus (contains DNA, not involved in electron transport)

facts.quizQuestion: What is the first large protein complex of the ETC that accepts electrons from NADH and transfers them to ubiquinone (CoQ), pumping H⁺ from the matrix into the intermembrane space?
facts.explanation: Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase) is the largest of the ETC complexes and the entry point for electrons from NADH. It oxidizes NADH to NAD⁺, accepts the electrons, and passes them to ubiquinone (CoQ). The energy released is used to pump 4 H⁺ from the matrix into the intermembrane space per NADH oxidized, contributing to the proton gradient that drives ATP synthase. Complex I is the site of action for the antibiotic rotenone (a pesticide) and is implicated in Parkinson's disease.
facts.visualDescription: The inner mitochondrial membrane with Complex I highlighted, showing NADH donating electrons, NAD⁺ being regenerated, CoQ accepting electrons, and 4 H⁺ being pumped across the membrane

facts.quizQuestion: Which ETC protein complex accepts electrons from FADH₂ and transfers them to ubiquinone, but does NOT pump protons — explaining why FADH₂ yields fewer ATP than NADH?
facts.explanation: Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase) is unique among ETC complexes in that it does not pump any protons across the inner mitochondrial membrane. It accepts electrons from FADH₂ (produced in the TCA cycle when succinate is oxidized to fumarate) and passes them to ubiquinone (CoQ). Because Complex II contributes no protons to the gradient, electrons entering via FADH₂ generate fewer ATP (~1.5) than electrons from NADH (~2.5), which enter at Complex I.
facts.visualDescription: The inner mitochondrial membrane showing Complex II with FADH₂ entering and CoQ accepting electrons, but no proton pumping arrows — contrasted with Complex I which does pump protons
distractors: Complex II is the final electron acceptor
Complex II reduces oxygen to water

facts.quizQuestion: What mobile electron carrier in the inner mitochondrial membrane shuttles electrons from Complexes I and II to Complex III?
facts.explanation: Ubiquinone (also called coenzyme Q or CoQ) is a lipid-soluble, mobile electron carrier embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane. It can freely diffuse within the lipid bilayer. Ubiquinone accepts electrons from both Complex I (from NADH) and Complex II (from FADH₂), becoming ubiquinol (fully reduced form), then donates those electrons to Complex III. CoQ is the convergence point for both NADH and FADH₂ pathways.
facts.visualDescription: The inner mitochondrial membrane showing CoQ (Q) as a small mobile molecule floating between Complex I/II and Complex III, carrying electrons from one to the other

facts.quizQuestion: What small mobile electron carrier protein in the intermembrane space shuttles electrons from Complex III to Complex IV?
facts.explanation: Cytochrome c is a small (12 kDa) heme-containing protein loosely associated with the outer surface of the inner mitochondrial membrane, in the intermembrane space. It accepts electrons from Complex III (cytochrome bc1 complex) one at a time and carries them to Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase). Cytochrome c is also a key signaling molecule — when released from the intermembrane space into the cytoplasm, it triggers programmed cell death (apoptosis).
facts.visualDescription: The intermembrane space with cytochrome c (a small blue protein) shown moving between Complex III and Complex IV, carrying electrons

facts.quizQuestion: What is the last protein complex of the ETC that accepts electrons from cytochrome c and passes them to O₂ — the final electron acceptor — reducing it to H₂O?
facts.explanation: Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase) is the terminal complex of the electron transport chain. It accepts 4 electrons from 4 cytochrome c molecules, combines them with 4 H⁺ from the matrix and 1 O₂, and produces 2 H₂O. This is where oxygen is consumed in cellular respiration. Complex IV also pumps 2 H⁺ per electron pair across the membrane. Cyanide and carbon monoxide are fatal because they block Complex IV.
facts.visualDescription: Complex IV shown at the end of the ETC with cytochrome c donating electrons, O₂ accepting them, and H₂O being produced, with protons being pumped across the membrane
distractors: Complex IV is the entry point for NADH
Complex III is the final acceptor

facts.quizQuestion: What molecule is the final electron acceptor in aerobic cellular respiration, receiving electrons at Complex IV and being reduced to water?
facts.explanation: Oxygen serves as the final electron acceptor in aerobic respiration because of its very high electronegativity — it strongly attracts electrons. At Complex IV, 4 electrons from cytochrome c and 4 H⁺ from the matrix reduce 1 O₂ to 2 H₂O. Without O₂, electrons cannot pass through the ETC, the proton gradient collapses, and ATP synthesis stops. This is why oxygen deprivation is rapidly fatal.
facts.visualDescription: The end of the ETC showing O₂ entering Complex IV, accepting electrons and protons, and being reduced to 2 H₂O

facts.quizQuestion: What is the movement of H⁺ ions from the intermembrane space into the matrix through ATP synthase — driven by the electrochemical gradient — that powers synthesis of ~26–28 ATP per glucose?
facts.explanation: Chemiosmosis (proposed by Peter Mitchell in 1961, Nobel Prize 1978) describes how the potential energy of the proton gradient is converted to chemical energy (ATP). H⁺ ions accumulated in the intermembrane space flow down their electrochemical gradient through ATP synthase (Complex V) into the matrix. This flow drives the rotation of the ATP synthase rotor, causing conformational changes that catalyze ATP synthesis from ADP + Pi. Each rotation of the ATP synthase produces approximately 3 ATP.
facts.visualDescription: The inner mitochondrial membrane showing H⁺ ions flowing from the intermembrane space (high concentration) through the channel of ATP synthase into the matrix, with ATP being produced
distractors: H⁺ flows from matrix to intermembrane space
ATP synthase pumps protons using ATP
Chemiosmosis is the same as glycolysis

facts.quizQuestion: What is the difference in H⁺ concentration between the intermembrane space (high) and the matrix (low), generated by the ETC, that drives ATP synthase to produce ATP?
facts.explanation: The proton gradient (or proton motive force) is an electrochemical potential energy store created by the ETC. Complexes I, III, and IV pump H⁺ from the matrix into the intermembrane space, creating a high proton concentration (low pH) there. The resulting concentration gradient and electrical potential (inside more negative) combine to create a powerful driving force that pushes H⁺ through ATP synthase, powering ATP production. The gradient represents stored potential energy, analogous to water behind a dam.
facts.visualDescription: A mitochondrion cross-section showing H⁺ ions densely packed in the intermembrane space and fewer in the matrix, with ATP synthase as the only 'gate' through which they can pass

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many ATP molecules are produced from the electron transport chain (oxidative phosphorylation) per glucose molecule?
facts.explanation: The ETC yields approximately 26–28 ATP per glucose via chemiosmosis. This comes from: 10 NADH × ~2.5 ATP each = ~25 ATP, plus 2 FADH₂ × ~1.5 ATP each = ~3 ATP, totaling ~28 ATP maximum. The actual value is slightly lower (~26–28) due to membrane transport costs. This compares to only 4 ATP from substrate-level phosphorylation (2 glycolysis + 2 TCA), confirming why the ETC is by far the most productive stage.
facts.visualDescription: A bar chart showing ETC producing 26-28 ATP (tall bar) compared to glycolysis 2 ATP and TCA 2 ATP (short bars), illustrating the dominant contribution of oxidative phosphorylation

facts.correctAnswer: NADH yields ~2.5 ATP; FADH₂ yields ~1.5 ATP (fewer because Complex II does not pump protons)
facts.quizQuestion: Why does each NADH fed to the ETC yield more ATP than each FADH₂, and what are the approximate ATP yields for each?
facts.explanation: NADH feeds electrons into Complex I, which pumps 4 H⁺ per pair of electrons, contributing substantially to the proton gradient. FADH₂ feeds electrons into Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase), which pumps NO protons. Because fewer protons are added to the gradient, fewer ATP are made when FADH₂ is used. Modern P/O ratios: NADH ≈ 2.5 ATP, FADH₂ ≈ 1.5 ATP. This distinction is critical for understanding total ATP accounting.
facts.visualDescription: Two parallel pathways: NADH → Complex I (4 H⁺ pumped) → more ATP; FADH₂ → Complex II (0 H⁺ pumped) → less ATP, with ~2.5 and ~1.5 ATP labeled respectively
distractors: NADH and FADH₂ yield equal ATP
FADH₂ yields more ATP than NADH
NADH yields 3 ATP; FADH₂ yields 2 ATP (old values only)
NADH yields 2 ATP; FADH₂ yields 2 ATP
FADH₂ enters at Complex I, yielding the same ATP as NADH
NADH yields ~1.5 ATP; FADH₂ yields ~2.5 ATP
NADH and FADH₂ both bypass Complex I

facts.quizQuestion: What are the inner folds of the inner mitochondrial membrane called, which greatly increase surface area for ETC complexes and ATP synthase to maximize ATP production?
facts.explanation: Cristae are the extensive infoldings of the inner mitochondrial membrane. By dramatically increasing the total surface area of the inner membrane, cristae allow many more ETC complexes and ATP synthase molecules to be embedded, multiplying the capacity for ATP production. Cells with high energy demands (e.g., cardiac muscle, hepatocytes) have mitochondria with very densely packed cristae. The degree of cristae folding is thus an adaptation to energy demand.
facts.visualDescription: A mitochondrion diagram showing the outer membrane (smooth) and inner membrane (highly folded cristae) with ETC complexes and ATP synthase labeled on the cristae

facts.quizQuestion: What anaerobic process regenerates NAD⁺ from NADH, allowing glycolysis to continue producing ATP without oxygen — yielding only 2 ATP per glucose?
facts.explanation: Fermentation is an anaerobic metabolic process that allows cells to continue making ATP (via glycolysis only) when oxygen is unavailable. Glycolysis requires NAD⁺, but when there is no oxygen, the ETC cannot reoxidize NADH to NAD⁺. Fermentation solves this by using pyruvate (or an intermediate) as the electron acceptor, reducing it and regenerating NAD⁺. The fermentation reactions themselves produce no ATP — the 2 ATP per glucose come entirely from glycolysis.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing glycolysis producing NADH and pyruvate, then fermentation recycling NADH back to NAD⁺ by reducing pyruvate, with 2 ATP as the only energy output

facts.quizQuestion: What type of fermentation reduces pyruvate directly to lactate, regenerating NAD⁺ — occurring in muscle cells during intense exercise and in Lactobacillus bacteria used in yogurt production?
facts.explanation: In lactic acid fermentation, pyruvate is directly reduced to lactate (lactic acid) by lactate dehydrogenase, using the electrons from NADH and regenerating NAD⁺. No CO₂ is released. In human muscle cells, this allows ATP production to continue briefly during intense exercise when oxygen delivery is insufficient. The lactate produced is transported to the liver, where it is reconverted to glucose (Cori cycle). Lactobacillus bacteria use this pathway to ferment milk into yogurt.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing pyruvate + NADH → lactate + NAD⁺, with insets showing human muscle and yogurt bacteria as examples

facts.quizQuestion: What type of fermentation converts pyruvate to ethanol + CO₂ while regenerating NAD⁺ — carried out by yeast and underlying bread baking, beer, and wine production?
facts.explanation: Alcoholic fermentation is a two-step process. First, pyruvate is decarboxylated to acetaldehyde by pyruvate decarboxylase, releasing CO₂. Then acetaldehyde is reduced to ethanol by alcohol dehydrogenase, oxidizing NADH back to NAD⁺. The CO₂ produced makes bread dough rise and gives carbonation to beer. The ethanol is the basis of alcoholic beverages. Yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) is the primary organism used industrially.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing pyruvate → acetaldehyde + CO₂ → ethanol + NAD⁺ regenerated, with beer mugs and bread loaves as illustrations

facts.quizQuestion: What is the essential purpose of fermentation — the reason cells carry it out when oxygen is unavailable?
facts.explanation: The fundamental purpose of fermentation is to regenerate NAD⁺. Glycolysis requires NAD⁺ as an electron acceptor and produces NADH. In aerobic conditions, the ETC reoxidizes NADH back to NAD⁺. Without oxygen, the ETC cannot function, NADH accumulates, and NAD⁺ runs out — stopping glycolysis. Fermentation bypasses this by using pyruvate as the electron acceptor, reducing it to lactate or ethanol while reoxidizing NADH to NAD⁺. This allows glycolysis to continue and produce 2 ATP.
facts.visualDescription: A cycle diagram showing glycolysis consuming NAD⁺ and producing NADH, then fermentation converting NADH back to NAD⁺ by reducing pyruvate, enabling glycolysis to continue
distractors: To produce additional ATP beyond glycolysis
To produce acetyl-CoA for the TCA cycle
To increase the pH of the cytoplasm
To produce ethanol as an energy source

facts.quizQuestion: How many ATP molecules does fermentation produce per glucose molecule (in total, including from glycolysis)?
facts.explanation: Fermentation produces exactly 2 ATP per glucose — and these come entirely from glycolysis, NOT from the fermentation reactions themselves. Fermentation (conversion of pyruvate to lactate or ethanol) produces zero additional ATP; it only regenerates NAD⁺ to keep glycolysis running. This makes fermentation approximately 15–16× less efficient than aerobic respiration (~30–32 ATP), but it allows survival without oxygen.
facts.visualDescription: An equation showing: Glucose → 2 Pyruvate (glycolysis: +2 ATP) → Lactate or Ethanol (fermentation: +0 ATP). Total = 2 ATP.

facts.correctAnswer: Aerobic: ~30–32 ATP; Fermentation: 2 ATP (~15–16× less efficient but requires no oxygen)
acceptableAlternatives: aerobic respiration produces far more ATP than fermentation
facts.quizQuestion: How does the ATP yield of aerobic respiration compare to fermentation, and what is the trade-off?
facts.explanation: Aerobic respiration produces ~30–32 ATP per glucose by fully oxidizing glucose through four stages (glycolysis, pyruvate oxidation, citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation), using oxygen as the final electron acceptor. Fermentation produces only 2 ATP per glucose (from glycolysis alone), making it ~15–16× less efficient. The trade-off is that fermentation works without oxygen, allowing anaerobic survival — critical when oxygen is unavailable (e.g., yeast in a sealed container, exercising muscle, anaerobic bacteria).
facts.visualDescription: A comparison bar chart with aerobic respiration at ~32 ATP (tall bar) and fermentation at 2 ATP (very short bar), with labels indicating O₂ required vs. O₂ not required
distractors: Fermentation is more efficient than aerobic respiration
Both produce 4 ATP from glycolysis only
Aerobic produces 10 ATP; fermentation produces 2 ATP
Both use the same ETC to produce ATP

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes an organism that requires oxygen for survival and cannot carry out anaerobic metabolism — dying in the absence of O₂?
facts.explanation: Obligate aerobes cannot survive without oxygen because they rely exclusively on aerobic respiration for ATP production. Their enzymes and metabolic machinery are adapted for aerobic conditions, and they lack the ability to ferment or use alternative electron acceptors. Examples include many fungi, most multicellular animals (including humans under extreme oxygen deprivation), and aerobic bacteria like Mycobacterium tuberculosis. The term 'obligate' means they are 'required' or 'obligated' to use oxygen.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing an obligate aerobe (e.g., a muscle cell) with an 'O₂ required' symbol, contrasted with an anaerobe that can survive without O₂

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes an organism that can use either aerobic respiration when O₂ is available, or anaerobic fermentation when O₂ is absent — exemplified by yeast, E. coli, and many human gut bacteria?
facts.explanation: Facultative anaerobes are metabolically versatile — they prefer aerobic respiration (much higher ATP yield) but can switch to fermentation when oxygen is unavailable. When oxygen is present, they use the full respiratory pathway for ~30–32 ATP per glucose. When oxygen is absent, they shift to fermentation for only 2 ATP per glucose. Yeast are a classic example: in the presence of oxygen they grow and respire aerobically; in its absence they ferment sugars to ethanol (used in wine and beer brewing). E. coli similarly switches pathways.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing a yeast cell or E. coli using aerobic respiration in the presence of O₂ (high ATP) and switching to fermentation in the absence of O₂ (low ATP)

facts.correctAnswer: Ligands bind receptors on target cells
facts.quizQuestion: How do cells communicate via chemical signaling?
facts.explanation: Cells release chemical signals called ligands that travel to target cells and bind to specific receptors. Only cells expressing the appropriate receptor will respond to the signal. This binding triggers a conformational change that initiates the cellular response.
facts.visualDescription: A signaling molecule docking into a receptor on a cell surface like a key in a lock
distractors: Electrical impulses travel through tight junctions
Hormones are produced inside the target cell
Enzymes diffuse freely between all cells
Signals are transmitted via DNA exchange
Cells respond to all chemical molecules equally
Ligands are always proteins produced by the nucleus
Receptors are located exclusively in the nucleus

facts.quizQuestion: What molecule or protein is a signaling molecule that binds to a specific receptor?
facts.explanation: A ligand is any molecule that binds specifically to a receptor. Ligands can be hormones, neurotransmitters, growth factors, or other signaling molecules. The ligand-receptor interaction is highly specific — like a lock and key.
facts.visualDescription: A small molecule fitting precisely into a receptor binding site

facts.quizQuestion: What is specificity in cell signaling?
facts.explanation: Signaling specificity means that a given chemical signal only affects cells that have the correct receptor for that signal. Even if a hormone circulates throughout the bloodstream, only target cells with matching receptors will respond. This ensures precise coordination of cellular responses.
facts.visualDescription: A hormone molecule passing by many cells, only binding to one cell that has the matching receptor
distractors: All cells in the organism respond equally to every signal
Only cells nearest to the signal source respond
Specificity is determined by the size of the signaling molecule
All hydrophobic signals bind all cell types
Cells respond based on their distance from the signaling cell
Signal strength determines which cells respond
Every cell has receptors for all possible ligands
Specificity is encoded in the signal's charge, not structure
