acceptableAlternatives: Meiosis produces 4 haploid cells; mitosis produces 2 diploid cells
facts.quizQuestion: What is the key difference between meiosis and mitosis in terms of number of divisions and type of daughter cells produced?
facts.explanation: Meiosis undergoes two rounds of division (meiosis I and II) producing four genetically unique haploid cells — used for sexual reproduction. Mitosis undergoes one division producing two genetically identical diploid cells — used for growth and repair.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side comparison: meiosis branching into 4 haploid cells vs. mitosis producing 2 diploid cells
distractors: Meiosis: 1 division, 2 haploid cells; mitosis: 2 divisions, 4 diploid cells
Both produce 2 diploid cells but meiosis is slower
Meiosis only occurs in prokaryotes; mitosis only in eukaryotes
Mitosis produces haploid cells; meiosis produces diploid cells
Both produce 4 cells but mitosis keeps them diploid
Meiosis adds chromosomes; mitosis removes chromosomes
Mitosis uses crossing over; meiosis does not
Meiosis produces 2 cells; mitosis produces 4 cells

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes a matched pair of chromosomes — one from each parent — that carry the same genes at the same loci but may carry different alleles?
facts.explanation: Homologous chromosomes (homologs) are matched pairs: one chromosome inherited from the mother and one from the father. They carry the same genes at the same positions (loci) but may have different allele versions of those genes. They pair during meiosis I.
facts.visualDescription: Two chromosomes of the same size and shape, one maternal and one paternal, aligned side by side

facts.quizQuestion: What are the two identical DNA copies of a chromosome, joined at the centromere, that are produced during DNA replication in S phase?
facts.explanation: Sister chromatids are produced when DNA replicates before cell division. The two identical copies remain attached at the centromere by cohesin proteins. In meiosis II (and mitosis), sister chromatids are separated at anaphase — not in meiosis I.
facts.visualDescription: Two identical chromosome copies joined at the centromere, forming an X shape

facts.quizQuestion: What structure, formed during prophase I, consists of two homologous chromosomes (each made of two sister chromatids) paired together — totaling 4 chromatids?
facts.explanation: A tetrad (also called a bivalent) forms when two homologous chromosomes synapse during prophase I. Each homolog is already a pair of sister chromatids, so the tetrad contains 4 chromatids total. Crossing over occurs between non-sister chromatids within the tetrad.
facts.visualDescription: Two homologous chromosomes, each as a pair of joined chromatids, synapsed together forming a 4-chromatid structure

facts.correctAnswer: Meiosis I is reductional; meiosis II is equational
acceptableAlternatives: Reductional division is meiosis I; equational is meiosis II
facts.quizQuestion: How do meiosis I and meiosis II differ in terms of 'reductional' vs. 'equational' division?
facts.explanation: Meiosis I is the reductional division because it halves the chromosome number by separating homologs. Meiosis II is the equational division because it separates sister chromatids without changing the chromosome number — similar to mitosis.
facts.visualDescription: Two-panel diagram: meiosis I arrows show chromosome number halving; meiosis II shows chromatids separating at same number
distractors: Meiosis I is equational; meiosis II is reductional
Meiosis I separates sister chromatids; meiosis II separates homologs
Reductional division occurs in mitosis; equational in meiosis
Meiosis II halves the chromosome number; meiosis I does not
Equational division doubles the chromosome number
Meiosis I is similar to mitosis; meiosis II is unique to meiosis

acceptableAlternatives: crossing over, independent assortment, random fertilization
facts.quizQuestion: What are the three main sources of genetic variation in sexually reproducing organisms?
facts.explanation: Sexual reproduction generates genetic variation through three mechanisms: (1) crossing over during prophase I creates recombinant chromosomes; (2) independent assortment of homologs at metaphase I shuffles whole chromosomes; and (3) random fertilization combines gametes unpredictably.
facts.visualDescription: Three icons: crossing chromosomes, randomly oriented chromosome pairs, and a sperm meeting an egg

facts.quizQuestion: Which process, based on the random orientation of homologous pairs at metaphase I, generates 2^n possible gamete chromosome combinations?
facts.explanation: Independent assortment occurs because each homologous pair lines up randomly at metaphase I — either chromosome of a pair can face either pole. With n chromosome pairs, 2^n different chromosome combinations are possible in the gametes.
facts.visualDescription: Two homologous pairs at the metaphase plate shown in two possible orientations, illustrating independent assortment

facts.quizQuestion: How many genetically different gametes can a human produce from independent assortment alone (humans have n=23)?
facts.explanation: With 23 pairs of homologous chromosomes, independent assortment generates 2^23 = 8,388,608 (approximately 8.4 million) different gamete chromosome combinations. Crossing over multiplies this diversity even further.
facts.visualDescription: 23 pairs of chromosomes branching into millions of possible gamete combinations

facts.quizQuestion: What are chromosomes called that carry new allele combinations created by crossing over — combinations not present in either original parental chromosome?
facts.explanation: Recombinant chromosomes result from crossing over during prophase I. Non-sister chromatids exchange segments, creating chromosomes with allele combinations that didn't exist in either parent. This dramatically increases genetic diversity beyond what independent assortment alone provides.
facts.visualDescription: Two chromosomes after crossing over, each showing a mix of light and dark segments from both parental chromosomes

facts.quizQuestion: Which source of genetic variation in sexual reproduction involves any sperm being able to fertilize any egg, vastly multiplying the possible genetically unique offspring?
facts.explanation: Random fertilization means any one of the ~8.4 million possible sperm can fertilize any one of the ~8.4 million possible eggs. This multiplies the possible combinations to more than 70 trillion — a key reason sexually reproducing populations are so genetically diverse.
facts.visualDescription: Many diverse sperm cells approaching an egg, with arrows showing unpredictable pairing

facts.quizQuestion: What is the failure of chromosomes or chromatids to separate properly during meiosis, resulting in gametes with an abnormal chromosome number?
facts.explanation: Nondisjunction occurs when chromosomes fail to separate correctly during meiosis I (homologs don't separate) or meiosis II (sister chromatids don't separate). This produces aneuploid gametes with either one extra chromosome (n+1) or one fewer (n-1).
facts.visualDescription: Chromosomes clumping together instead of separating during cell division, with unequal distribution to daughter cells

facts.correctAnswer: Meiosis I: all 4 gametes aneuploid; meiosis II: 2 of 4 gametes aneuploid
acceptableAlternatives: Meiosis I nondisjunction affects all gametes; meiosis II affects only 2
facts.quizQuestion: How do the outcomes of nondisjunction in meiosis I differ from nondisjunction in meiosis II in terms of aneuploid gametes produced?
facts.explanation: In meiosis I nondisjunction, both homologs go to the same pole — all four resulting gametes are aneuploid. In meiosis II nondisjunction, sister chromatids fail to separate in one cell — only two of the four gametes are aneuploid, and the other two are normal.
facts.visualDescription: Comparison diagram showing meiosis I nondisjunction producing 4 aneuploid cells vs. meiosis II producing 2 aneuploid and 2 normal cells
distractors: Both produce the same proportion of aneuploid gametes
Meiosis I: 2 aneuploid; meiosis II: 4 aneuploid
Meiosis I: 1 aneuploid; meiosis II: 3 aneuploid
Nondisjunction only occurs in meiosis I
Meiosis II nondisjunction always produces polyploidy
Meiosis I: 0 normal gametes; meiosis II: 0 normal gametes
Meiosis II nondisjunction is always lethal; meiosis I is not

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the condition of having an abnormal chromosome number that is NOT a whole multiple of the haploid number?
facts.explanation: Aneuploidy results from nondisjunction during meiosis. An aneuploid organism has one or more chromosomes too many or too few. Examples include trisomy (one extra chromosome) and monosomy (one missing chromosome). Polyploidy (whole extra sets) is NOT aneuploidy.
facts.visualDescription: Karyotype with one chromosome set having three copies instead of two, indicating trisomy

facts.quizQuestion: Which type of aneuploidy involves having three copies of a particular chromosome (2n+1)?
facts.explanation: Trisomy means three copies of one chromosome instead of the normal two. The most common example is Down syndrome (trisomy 21), where chromosome 21 has three copies. Trisomy results from nondisjunction producing an n+1 gamete that fuses with a normal n gamete.
facts.visualDescription: Chromosome pair shown with three copies of one chromosome highlighted

facts.quizQuestion: Which condition results from trisomy 21 — the presence of three copies of chromosome 21 — caused by nondisjunction during meiosis?
facts.explanation: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) is the most common chromosomal aneuploidy compatible with survival. Three copies of chromosome 21 result from nondisjunction, most often during meiosis I in the egg. Incidence increases with maternal age.
facts.visualDescription: Karyotype showing three copies of chromosome 21 highlighted among 46 total chromosomes

facts.quizQuestion: Which type of aneuploidy involves having only one copy of a particular chromosome (2n-1), which is often lethal?
facts.explanation: Monosomy means only one copy of a chromosome instead of the normal pair. Most autosomal monosomies are lethal in humans. Turner syndrome (45,X) is a notable exception — the only viable sex chromosome monosomy in humans.
facts.visualDescription: Karyotype with one chromosome pair showing only a single chromosome instead of two

facts.quizQuestion: Which condition results from sex chromosome monosomy with a 45,X karyotype — one of the few human monosomies viable to birth?
facts.explanation: Turner syndrome arises from a 45,X karyotype — only one X chromosome, no second sex chromosome. It affects females and causes short stature, infertility, and heart defects. It is one of the few monosomies compatible with human survival.
facts.visualDescription: Karyotype with the sex chromosome pair showing only one X and no Y or second X

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes having more than two complete sets of chromosomes (e.g., 3n or 4n) — common and often beneficial in plants, but usually lethal in animals?
facts.explanation: Polyploidy occurs when an organism has three or more complete chromosome sets. It is extremely common in flowering plants — many crop species (wheat, cotton, bananas) are polyploid. In animals, polyploidy is almost always lethal because it disrupts sex determination and other systems.
facts.visualDescription: Plant cell with four full sets of chromosomes labeled 4n, contrasted with a normal diploid 2n animal cell

facts.quizQuestion: Which scientist, working with pea plants (Pisum sativum) in the 1860s, discovered the fundamental laws of heredity using true-breeding lines and statistical analysis?
facts.explanation: Gregor Mendel, an Austrian monk, performed thousands of crosses with pea plants over eight years. His careful record-keeping and statistical analysis led to the discovery of segregation and independent assortment — the foundation of modern genetics.
facts.visualDescription: Portrait of a monk in garden surrounded by pea plants, with statistical tallies of offspring

facts.quizQuestion: What is the basic unit of heredity — a segment of DNA that encodes a protein or functional RNA molecule?
facts.explanation: A gene is a defined segment of DNA that carries the information to produce a specific protein or functional RNA. Genes are located at specific positions (loci) on chromosomes. Each organism has two copies (alleles) of most genes — one on each homolog.
facts.visualDescription: A DNA double helix with a highlighted segment labeled as a gene

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes an alternative form of a gene — multiple versions of which can exist at a given chromosomal locus in a population?
facts.explanation: Alleles are different versions of the same gene. For example, a gene controlling flower color might have a purple allele and a white allele. An individual inherits one allele from each parent; if both alleles are identical, the individual is homozygous; if different, heterozygous.
facts.visualDescription: Two chromosomes at the same locus showing different colored allele markers — one labeled 'B' and one 'b'

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the genetic makeup of an individual for one or more genes, such as 'Bb' for a heterozygous individual?
facts.explanation: Genotype refers to the actual alleles an organism possesses at one or more loci. It is the underlying genetic constitution, not what is visible. Two individuals with different genotypes (BB and Bb) can have identical phenotypes if dominance is complete.
facts.visualDescription: Punnett square showing genotype labels BB, Bb, and bb with different color coding

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the observable physical or biochemical characteristics of an organism, such as brown eyes or tall stature?
facts.explanation: Phenotype is what you can actually observe or measure — traits like eye color, height, or blood type. Phenotype is determined by genotype interacting with the environment. Organisms with the same genotype can sometimes have different phenotypes due to environmental effects.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side comparison of two plants: genotype labels BB and Bb above, same tall green phenotype shown for both

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes an individual having two identical alleles at a given locus, such as BB or bb?
facts.explanation: A homozygous individual carries two copies of the same allele at a locus — either two dominant alleles (homozygous dominant, BB) or two recessive alleles (homozygous recessive, bb). Homozygous individuals breed true for that trait.
facts.visualDescription: Locus on two homologs showing identical 'B' alleles on both, or identical 'b' alleles on both

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes an individual having two different alleles at a given locus, such as Bb?
facts.explanation: A heterozygous individual (e.g., Bb) carries one copy of each of two different alleles. With complete dominance, the dominant allele's phenotype is expressed and the recessive is hidden. With incomplete dominance or codominance, both alleles contribute to the phenotype.
facts.visualDescription: Locus on two homologs showing 'B' on one chromosome and 'b' on the other

facts.quizQuestion: Which type of allele has its phenotypic effect expressed in a heterozygote, and is conventionally written as an uppercase letter?
facts.explanation: A dominant allele's phenotype is expressed even when paired with a different allele. In a Bb heterozygote, the B allele is expressed if it is dominant. Dominance describes the relationship between alleles — it does NOT mean the allele is more common or 'better.'
facts.visualDescription: Bb genotype with a large 'B' phenotype shown, indicating the dominant allele controls the observable trait

facts.quizQuestion: Which type of allele is masked by the dominant allele in a heterozygote and is only expressed in the homozygous recessive (bb) condition?
facts.explanation: A recessive allele is only expressed phenotypically when an individual carries two copies (bb). In heterozygotes (Bb), the recessive allele is 'hidden' but still present in the genotype and can be passed to offspring. Many genetic disorders are caused by recessive alleles.
facts.visualDescription: bb genotype showing the recessive phenotype, versus Bb showing dominant phenotype — same recessive allele, different expression

facts.quizQuestion: Which of Mendel's laws states that the two alleles for a gene separate during gamete formation, so each gamete receives only one allele?
facts.explanation: Mendel's Law of Segregation states that the two alleles an organism has for a gene separate during meiosis — one goes to each gamete. This is the physical consequence of homologous chromosomes separating at anaphase I of meiosis.
facts.visualDescription: A Bb parent organism with alleles separating — B going to one gamete, b going to another

facts.quizQuestion: Which of Mendel's laws states that genes on different (non-homologous) chromosomes assort independently into gametes during meiosis?
facts.explanation: Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment applies to genes on non-homologous chromosomes. Because homologous pairs orient randomly at metaphase I, the alleles for one gene assort independently of alleles for genes on other chromosome pairs. This law does NOT hold for linked genes.
facts.visualDescription: Two gene pairs on different chromosome pairs shown orienting randomly at metaphase I

facts.quizQuestion: What is the genotypic ratio produced by a Bb × Bb monohybrid cross?
facts.explanation: A Bb × Bb cross produces offspring in a 1:2:1 genotypic ratio — 25% BB (homozygous dominant), 50% Bb (heterozygous), and 25% bb (homozygous recessive). This can be easily worked out with a 2×2 Punnett square.
facts.visualDescription: Punnett square for Bb × Bb showing BB, Bb, Bb, bb in the four boxes

facts.quizQuestion: What phenotypic ratio results from a Bb × Bb monohybrid cross with complete dominance?
facts.explanation: With complete dominance, both BB and Bb individuals show the dominant phenotype, while only bb shows the recessive phenotype. The result is 3 dominant : 1 recessive (75% dominant, 25% recessive) — Mendel's classic 3:1 ratio from his pea plant crosses.
facts.visualDescription: Four offspring icons — three showing dominant trait (tall/purple) and one showing recessive trait (short/white)

facts.quizQuestion: What phenotypic ratio is produced by a BbRr × BbRr dihybrid cross with independent assortment and complete dominance?
facts.explanation: A dihybrid cross (BbRr × BbRr) produces a 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio among offspring: 9 show both dominant traits, 3 show dominant A/recessive B, 3 show recessive A/dominant B, and 1 shows both recessive traits. This is Mendel's signature dihybrid ratio.
facts.visualDescription: 4×4 Punnett square for BbRr × BbRr with offspring sorted into four phenotypic classes in 9:3:3:1 proportions

facts.quizQuestion: What genetic cross involves mating an individual of unknown genotype with a homozygous recessive individual (bb) to determine if the unknown is BB or Bb?
facts.explanation: A test cross reveals whether an individual showing the dominant phenotype is homozygous dominant (BB) or heterozygous (Bb). If ALL offspring show the dominant phenotype, the unknown parent is BB. If HALF show recessive phenotype, the unknown parent is Bb.
facts.visualDescription: Unknown dominant parent crossed with bb showing two possible results: all dominant offspring (BB parent) or 50% recessive offspring (Bb parent)

facts.quizQuestion: What grid diagram is used to predict the genotypic and phenotypic ratios of offspring by systematically combining parental gametes?
facts.explanation: A Punnett square maps all possible combinations of parental gametes. Gametes from one parent are placed along the top; gametes from the other parent are placed along the side. Each inner box represents one possible genotype in the offspring.
facts.visualDescription: 2×2 grid with B and b across the top and B and b down the side, showing BB, Bb, Bb, bb in the boxes

facts.quizQuestion: Which probability rule states that for independent genetic events, P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B)?
facts.explanation: The multiplication rule applies when calculating the probability of two independent events both occurring. In genetics, if two gene pairs assort independently, the probability of a specific two-gene genotype equals the product of the individual probabilities for each gene.
facts.visualDescription: Two independent coin flips shown with P(heads AND heads) = 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4

facts.quizQuestion: Which probability rule states that for mutually exclusive genetic outcomes, P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B)?
facts.explanation: The addition rule is used when calculating the probability of either one event or another occurring — where both cannot happen at the same time. In genetics, this is useful for calculating the probability of multiple alternative genotypes (e.g., P(BB or Bb)).
facts.visualDescription: Two exclusive outcome boxes labeled A and B with arrows pointing to 'P(A) + P(B)'

facts.quizQuestion: Which plant species, with the scientific name Pisum sativum, did Gregor Mendel use in the 1860s to discover the laws of heredity?
facts.explanation: Mendel chose the garden pea (Pisum sativum) because it has distinct traits with clear-cut variation (tall vs. short; purple vs. white flowers), a short generation time, the ability to self-fertilize, and easy manual cross-pollination. These features made statistical analysis of inheritance straightforward.
facts.visualDescription: Green pea pod plant with labeled pairs of contrasting traits: tall/short stems, purple/white flowers, round/wrinkled seeds

facts.quizQuestion: Which inheritance pattern produces a heterozygote with an intermediate phenotype between the two homozygous phenotypes (e.g., red × white = pink)?
facts.explanation: Incomplete dominance occurs when neither allele is completely dominant. The heterozygote shows a blended phenotype intermediate between the two homozygotes. Classic example: red snapdragons (R¹R¹) × white snapdragons (R²R²) → pink (R¹R²). The 1:2:1 genotypic ratio corresponds to a 1:2:1 phenotypic ratio.
facts.visualDescription: Red flower and white flower parents with a pink flower offspring in the center

facts.quizQuestion: Which plant species produces pink-flowered heterozygotes when red-flowered (R¹R¹) and white-flowered (R²R²) varieties are crossed — a classic example of incomplete dominance?
facts.explanation: Snapdragons (Antirrhinum majus) demonstrate incomplete dominance: red (R¹R¹) × white (R²R²) → pink (R¹R²). Neither the red nor white allele is fully dominant over the other, resulting in a blended intermediate pink phenotype in heterozygotes.
facts.visualDescription: Snapdragon flowers in red, pink, and white arranged in a cross diagram showing parent × parent → offspring

facts.quizQuestion: Which inheritance pattern has both alleles fully and simultaneously expressed in the heterozygote, as seen in AB blood type displaying both A and B antigens?
facts.explanation: Codominance means both alleles are expressed equally and completely in the heterozygote — no blending occurs. In AB blood type, the I^A allele produces A antigens AND the I^B allele produces B antigens on the same red blood cells simultaneously.
facts.visualDescription: AB blood type red blood cell with both A and B antigen shapes visible on the surface

facts.correctAnswer: Incomplete dominance blends phenotypes; codominance expresses both simultaneously
acceptableAlternatives: Incomplete dominance: intermediate blended phenotype; codominance: both phenotypes fully expressed
facts.quizQuestion: What is the key difference between incomplete dominance and codominance in heterozygotes?
facts.explanation: In incomplete dominance, the heterozygote shows a blended intermediate phenotype (e.g., red + white = pink — a new color not matching either parent). In codominance, BOTH parental phenotypes are fully expressed simultaneously in the heterozygote without blending (e.g., AB blood type shows both A AND B antigens).
facts.visualDescription: Two diagrams: pink flower (incomplete dominance) vs. red-and-white patches on same organism (codominance)
distractors: Both produce blended phenotypes in heterozygotes
Both produce dominant phenotypes in heterozygotes
Incomplete dominance expresses both phenotypes; codominance blends them
Codominance is only for blood types; incomplete dominance is for all other traits
Both are the same pattern with different names
Incomplete dominance only occurs in plants; codominance only in animals
Codominance follows Mendel's laws; incomplete dominance does not

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the situation where more than two alleles exist in a population for a single gene, as in the ABO blood type system with I^A, I^B, and i?
facts.explanation: Multiple alleles means that while any individual can only carry two alleles at a locus, three or more different alleles exist within a population. ABO blood type is the classic example: the three alleles (I^A, I^B, i) produce four blood type phenotypes (A, B, AB, O).
facts.visualDescription: Three allele symbols I^A, I^B, and i shown as three options at a single gene locus on a chromosome

facts.quizQuestion: Which blood group system has three alleles — I^A (dominant, A antigen), I^B (dominant, B antigen), and i (recessive, no antigen) — where I^A and I^B are codominant with each other?
facts.explanation: The ABO system illustrates both multiple alleles and codominance. I^A and I^B are both dominant over i, but codominant with each other (I^A I^B = blood type AB). The recessive i allele (ii genotype) produces type O blood (no antigens). Four phenotypes arise from the three alleles.
facts.visualDescription: Chart showing three alleles (I^A, I^B, i) and the four resulting blood types (A, B, AB, O) with their genotypes

facts.quizQuestion: Which inheritance pattern, where two or more genes each contribute additively to a single trait, produces continuous phenotypic variation (e.g., skin color, height)?
facts.explanation: Polygenic inheritance involves multiple genes — each adding a small effect — controlling a single trait. The result is a continuous range of phenotypes that form a bell-curve distribution in a population. Skin color and height are classic human examples.
facts.visualDescription: Bell curve showing continuous distribution of human height, contrasted with discrete Mendelian ratios

facts.quizQuestion: Which genetic phenomenon involves one gene influencing or masking the expression of a different gene, as seen in Labrador coat color where 'ee' dogs are yellow regardless of B/b genotype?
facts.explanation: Epistasis occurs when one gene's product controls whether another gene's product is expressed. In Labradors, the E gene controls pigment deposition: ee dogs cannot deposit pigment at all, making them yellow regardless of whether they are BB, Bb, or bb for the B gene.
facts.visualDescription: Labrador coat color cross diagram showing yellow (ee), black (E_B_), and chocolate (E_bb) puppies

facts.quizQuestion: Which dog breed is the classic example of epistasis, where an E gene controls whether the B/b pigment gene is expressed — making ee dogs yellow regardless of their B genotype?
facts.explanation: Labrador retrievers demonstrate epistasis between the B gene (black vs. chocolate pigment) and the E gene (pigment deposition). An ee dog cannot deposit any pigment, producing a yellow coat no matter what B alleles it carries. E_ dogs can be black (B_) or chocolate (bb).
facts.visualDescription: Three Labrador puppies: one black, one chocolate brown, one yellow, with genotype labels showing epistasis

facts.quizQuestion: Which genetic phenomenon occurs when a single gene affects multiple phenotypic traits, as in sickle cell anemia causing both hemolytic anemia and malaria resistance?
facts.explanation: Pleiotropy means one gene has multiple phenotypic effects. The sickle cell allele alters hemoglobin shape, causing red blood cell sickling — which simultaneously causes hemolytic anemia and pain crises AND provides partial protection against malaria. One gene, many consequences.
facts.visualDescription: One gene arrow branching into three different body effects: misshapen red blood cells, anemia, and malaria resistance shield

facts.quizQuestion: Which disease is the classic example of pleiotropy — a single allele causing hemolytic anemia, increased malaria resistance, splenic damage, and multi-organ effects?
facts.explanation: Sickle cell anemia is caused by a single point mutation in the beta-globin gene. This one change causes hemoglobin to polymerize under low oxygen, leading to sickled red blood cells that cause anemia, pain crises, and organ damage — but also provides heterozygous carriers with partial malaria resistance.
facts.visualDescription: Sickled red blood cell shape vs. normal round cells, with an arrow showing multiple downstream effects

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes genes located on the same chromosome that tend to be inherited together and violate Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment?
facts.explanation: Linked genes are physically located on the same chromosome. Because they travel together during meiosis, they do not assort independently. However, crossing over can separate linked genes, creating recombinant offspring — the frequency of recombination indicates how far apart the genes are.
facts.visualDescription: Two genes shown on the same chromosome arm, traveling together into the same gamete

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the percentage of recombinant offspring from linked genes, used to estimate genetic map distance (1% = 1 centimorgan)?
facts.explanation: Recombination frequency measures how often crossing over separates two linked genes. The more often they recombine, the further apart they are on the chromosome. A 1% recombination frequency equals 1 centimorgan (cM) or 1 map unit — allowing geneticists to create linkage maps.
facts.visualDescription: Two genes on a chromosome with a distance scale in centimorgans, showing recombinant offspring

facts.quizQuestion: What are traits encoded by genes on the X chromosome called — which are expressed more frequently in males (XY) than females (XX) when recessive?
facts.explanation: Sex-linked traits are controlled by genes on the sex chromosomes, most commonly the X chromosome. Because males have only one X chromosome (XY), a single recessive allele on that X is sufficient for the trait to be expressed. Females (XX) need two copies of the recessive allele.
facts.visualDescription: X chromosome with a trait gene marked, showing XY male expressing the trait vs. XX female who is a carrier

facts.quizQuestion: Which X-linked recessive trait affecting red-green color discrimination is more common in males than females because males need only one recessive allele to be affected?
facts.explanation: Red-green color blindness is caused by mutations in opsin genes on the X chromosome. Males (XY) need only one copy of the recessive allele (on their single X) to be colorblind, while females (XX) need two copies. About 8% of males but only 0.5% of females are colorblind.
facts.visualDescription: Ishihara color test plate showing numbers visible to normal vision but not to colorblind individuals

facts.quizQuestion: Which X-linked recessive blood-clotting disorder, caused by mutations in clotting factor genes on the X chromosome, almost exclusively affects males?
facts.explanation: Hemophilia results from mutations in clotting factor genes (factor VIII for hemophilia A, factor IX for hemophilia B) on the X chromosome. Males with the allele on their single X cannot clot blood normally. Females with one copy are carriers; only females with two copies of the recessive allele are affected.
facts.visualDescription: Pedigree showing X-linked recessive inheritance pattern with affected males and carrier females

facts.quizQuestion: Which X-linked recessive disorder, caused by mutations in the dystrophin gene on the X chromosome, causes progressive muscle weakness and almost exclusively affects males?
facts.explanation: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) results from loss-of-function mutations in the dystrophin gene on the X chromosome. Without functional dystrophin, muscle cells break down progressively. Because the gene is X-linked, males with one mutant allele are fully affected, while females need two mutant alleles.
facts.visualDescription: X chromosome with dystrophin gene highlighted, showing muscle fiber degeneration in an affected male

facts.quizQuestion: What process randomly and permanently inactivates one X chromosome in each cell of XX females early in development, condensing it into a Barr body?
facts.explanation: X-inactivation (lyonization) was discovered by Mary Lyon in 1961. Early in embryonic development, one X chromosome in each cell is randomly inactivated by methylation and condensed into a Barr body. The inactivated X remains silenced in all that cell's descendants, creating cellular mosaicism in females.
facts.visualDescription: Two XX cells, each with a different X chromosome highlighted as condensed and dark (Barr body)

facts.quizQuestion: What is the condensed, transcriptionally inactive X chromosome visible in the nucleus of XX female cells, resulting from X-inactivation?
facts.explanation: A Barr body is the visible manifestation of X-inactivation: the inactivated X chromosome is tightly coiled and stains darkly in cell nuclei. Females with two X chromosomes have exactly one Barr body; males with one X have none. The number of Barr bodies = number of X chromosomes minus 1.
facts.visualDescription: Cell nucleus with a dark, condensed Barr body visible at the nuclear periphery

facts.quizQuestion: Which animal shows patches of orange and black fur because different cell lineages randomly inactivate different X chromosomes carrying different coat-color alleles?
facts.explanation: Calico cats are almost always female (XX). The orange vs. black coat color gene is on the X chromosome. Random X-inactivation during early development means some cell lineages express the orange allele while others express the black allele — creating the characteristic patches of color.
facts.visualDescription: Calico cat with distinct patches of orange and black fur on white background

facts.quizQuestion: Which epigenetic phenomenon causes gene expression to depend on which parent contributed the allele — silencing one parental copy by methylation — as in Prader-Willi and Angelman syndromes?
facts.explanation: Genomic imprinting is epigenetic: certain genes are permanently silenced with a chemical 'imprint' based on their parental origin. If the active copy is deleted or mutated, the silenced imprinted copy cannot compensate. Prader-Willi vs. Angelman syndromes result from the same chromosome 15 deletion — but which parent contributed the deleted copy determines which syndrome appears.
facts.visualDescription: Two copies of a gene — one with a methylation cap silencing it — and arrows showing only the other copy being transcribed

facts.quizQuestion: Which inheritance pattern involves organelle DNA passed exclusively through the egg cytoplasm from mothers to ALL offspring — bypassing Mendelian segregation?
facts.explanation: Mitochondria have their own circular DNA (mtDNA) inherited maternally. Sperm contribute essentially no cytoplasm at fertilization, so mitochondria come only from the egg. Mitochondrial disorders are passed from an affected mother to all her children, with no paternal transmission.
facts.visualDescription: Egg cell with many mitochondria highlighted, sperm contributing only nuclear DNA; arrows showing all offspring inheriting maternal mitochondria

facts.quizQuestion: Which type of organelle inheritance involves plastid DNA typically passed maternally through the egg in most plant species, not subject to Mendelian segregation?
facts.explanation: Chloroplasts, like mitochondria, contain their own DNA and are generally inherited maternally in most angiosperms — pollen contributes little or no plastid DNA. This means leaf variegation patterns caused by chloroplast mutations follow maternal inheritance, not Mendelian ratios.
facts.visualDescription: Plant egg cell with green chloroplasts highlighted; pollen tube contributing only nucleus; offspring showing maternal chloroplast pattern

acceptableAlternatives: Phenotype results from genotype interacting with environment
facts.quizQuestion: What fundamental principle states that an organism's observable traits are determined by the interaction of its genetic makeup with its environmental conditions?
facts.explanation: No gene acts in isolation from the environment. The same genotype can produce very different phenotypes under different conditions. For example, PKU genotype causes intellectual disability only if dietary phenylalanine is not restricted; Siamese cat pigmentation depends on temperature.
facts.visualDescription: Formula graphic: Genotype (DNA strand) + Environment (sun, food, temperature) = Phenotype (organism traits)

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the range of phenotypes that a single genotype can produce when exposed to different environmental conditions?
facts.explanation: The norm of reaction illustrates phenotypic plasticity: the same genotype can produce different phenotypes across a range of environments. Plotting phenotype vs. environmental variable produces the reaction norm curve — different genotypes may show different norm-of-reaction shapes.
facts.visualDescription: Graph with environmental variable on x-axis and phenotype value on y-axis, showing two different genotype lines with different slopes

facts.quizQuestion: Which animal shows temperature-sensitive coat color because a heat-sensitive tyrosinase enzyme produces dark pigment only at cool extremities (ears, nose, paws, tail) and pale fur at the warm body core?
facts.explanation: Siamese cats have a temperature-sensitive (heat-labile) allele of the tyrosinase gene. The enzyme works at cool temperatures (below ~33°C) to produce dark pigment on extremities, but is inactivated at the warmer body core. This is a vivid example of how environment (temperature) modulates phenotype.
facts.visualDescription: Siamese cat showing dark-colored ears, nose, paws, and tail contrasting with pale cream body

facts.quizQuestion: Which plant produces blue flowers in acidic soil (aluminum available) and pink flowers in alkaline soil (aluminum unavailable), demonstrating that the same genotype can produce different phenotypes based on soil chemistry?
facts.explanation: Hydrangeas show that environmental chemistry (soil pH) alters phenotype independently of genotype. In acidic soil, aluminum ions are soluble and absorbed by the plant, shifting flower color to blue. In alkaline soil, aluminum is insoluble and unavailable, producing pink flowers.
facts.visualDescription: Two hydrangea plants with identical leaves: one in acidic soil with blue flowers, one in alkaline soil with pink flowers

facts.quizQuestion: Which autosomal recessive disorder of phenylalanine metabolism can prevent intellectual disability through strict dietary restriction of phenylalanine from birth — demonstrating gene-environment interaction?
facts.explanation: PKU is caused by mutations in the phenylalanine hydroxylase gene. Without the enzyme, phenylalanine accumulates to toxic levels, causing intellectual disability. However, if phenylalanine intake is restricted through diet from birth, the toxic buildup is prevented and normal development occurs — a powerful example of environmental modification of gene expression.
facts.visualDescription: Metabolic pathway diagram showing blocked phenylalanine conversion, with a dietary restriction intervention arrow preventing accumulation

facts.quizQuestion: Which research method uses identical (monozygotic) twins raised apart to separate the contributions of genetics vs. environment to phenotypic differences?
facts.explanation: Identical twins share 100% of their DNA. When raised in different environments, any phenotypic differences between them must be due to environmental influences rather than genetics. Comparing identical twins raised apart allows researchers to estimate how much of a trait's variation is genetically vs. environmentally determined.
facts.visualDescription: Two identical twins raised in different households showing some shared and some different traits

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the proportion of individuals with a given genotype who actually exhibit the expected phenotype — where less than 100% is called 'incomplete'?
facts.explanation: Penetrance measures how reliably a genotype produces its expected phenotype. If 80% of individuals with a disease genotype show the disease, penetrance is 80% (incomplete). Many genetic disorders show incomplete penetrance, meaning some carriers with the genotype never develop symptoms.
facts.visualDescription: Group of individuals all with the same genotype, but some showing the phenotype and some not — illustrating incomplete penetrance

facts.quizQuestion: Which theory, developed by Sutton and Boveri (~1902), states that genes are located on chromosomes and that chromosome behavior during meiosis explains Mendel's laws?
facts.explanation: The chromosome theory of inheritance connected Mendel's abstract 'factors' to a physical structure — chromosomes. Sutton and Boveri independently noticed that the behavior of chromosomes during meiosis (pairing, segregation, independent assortment) perfectly paralleled the behavior of Mendel's hereditary factors.
facts.visualDescription: Split diagram: Mendel's pea plant ratios on left, chromosome behavior in meiosis on right, with equivalence arrows

facts.quizQuestion: Which two scientists independently proposed the chromosome theory of inheritance (~1902), connecting Mendel's hereditary factors to chromosome behavior during meiosis?
facts.explanation: Walter Sutton (studying grasshopper meiosis) and Theodor Boveri (studying sea urchin development) independently realized that the behavior of chromosomes during meiosis mirrors the behavior of Mendel's hereditary units. Together, their work established the chromosomal basis of inheritance.
facts.visualDescription: Two portraits side by side — Sutton with grasshopper image, Boveri with sea urchin image, both pointing to the same chromosome diagram

facts.quizQuestion: Which American biologist used Drosophila melanogaster to prove genes are physically located on chromosomes and discovered X-linked inheritance through the white-eye trait?
facts.explanation: Thomas Hunt Morgan's white-eye Drosophila experiments in the early 1900s provided direct proof that genes are on chromosomes. The white-eye trait appeared only in males in F2 — proving it was linked to the X chromosome and giving the first concrete chromosomal location for a specific gene.
facts.visualDescription: Morgan's lab bench with fruit fly bottles, showing red-eyed and white-eyed flies, and an X chromosome diagram

facts.quizQuestion: Which experiment crossed white-eyed male Drosophila with red-eyed females, finding that F2 white eyes appeared ONLY in males — proving the eye-color gene is on the X chromosome?
facts.explanation: Morgan crossed white-eyed males (X^w Y) with wild-type red-eyed females (X^+ X^+). All F1 were red-eyed. In F2, white eyes appeared only in males — about half of F2 males had white eyes. This sex-specific pattern could only be explained if the gene was on the X chromosome.
facts.visualDescription: Cross diagram: white-eyed male × red-eyed female → all red F1 → F2 with white-eyed males and red-eyed females only

facts.quizQuestion: Which model organism — the fruit fly — did Thomas Hunt Morgan use to demonstrate X-linked inheritance and the physical location of genes on chromosomes?
facts.explanation: Drosophila melanogaster is ideal for genetics research: short generation time (two weeks), large numbers of offspring, only 4 chromosome pairs, and easily observable phenotypes. Morgan used Drosophila to map the first genes to specific chromosomes and establish the chromosomal theory of heredity.
facts.visualDescription: Close-up of fruit fly (Drosophila) with red compound eyes, showing the small size and 4 chromosome karyotype

facts.quizQuestion: What is the organized display of an individual's full chromosome complement, arranged by size and banding pattern, used to detect aneuploidy and chromosomal mutations?
facts.explanation: A karyotype is produced by staining chromosomes, photographing them during metaphase when they are maximally condensed, and arranging them by size and banding pattern. Karyotypes can detect trisomies (Down syndrome), monosomies (Turner syndrome), and large structural mutations like translocations.
facts.visualDescription: Standard human karyotype with 23 numbered chromosome pairs arranged by size, with sex chromosomes labeled XX or XY

facts.quizQuestion: Which chromosomal mutation involves the loss of a segment of a chromosome, potentially causing haploinsufficiency if essential genes are in the deleted region?
facts.explanation: A chromosomal deletion removes a segment of DNA, taking with it all genes in that region. If the remaining copy of those genes on the homolog cannot fully compensate, haploinsufficiency results. Cri-du-chat syndrome results from a deletion on chromosome 5.
facts.visualDescription: Chromosome diagram showing a segment missing (gap), contrasted with normal chromosome beside it

facts.quizQuestion: Which chromosomal mutation results in an extra copy of a chromosome segment, giving an organism more than two copies of the genes in that region?
facts.explanation: A chromosomal duplication creates an additional copy of a chromosomal segment. The duplicated genes can evolve independently over time — one copy maintaining the original function while the other may mutate to gain a new function. Gene family expansion often originates from ancient duplications.
facts.visualDescription: Chromosome showing a repeated segment, with the duplicated region labeled twice

facts.quizQuestion: Which chromosomal mutation involves a segment being excised and reinserted in the reverse orientation, potentially disrupting gene expression at the breakpoints?
facts.explanation: A chromosomal inversion flips a segment 180°, reversing the order of genes in that region. While no genetic material is gained or lost, gene expression can be disrupted if breakpoints fall within genes or separate genes from their promoters. Inversions also suppress recombination in heterozygous individuals.
facts.visualDescription: Chromosome diagram showing a segment flipped (arrows indicating reversal), contrasted with normal chromosome

facts.quizQuestion: Which chromosomal mutation involves a segment moving from one chromosome to a non-homologous chromosome — as in the Philadelphia chromosome between chromosomes 9 and 22?
facts.explanation: A chromosomal translocation moves genetic material between non-homologous chromosomes. The Philadelphia chromosome results from a reciprocal translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22, fusing the BCR and ABL genes. This BCR-ABL fusion creates a constitutively active kinase that causes chronic myelogenous leukemia (CML).
facts.visualDescription: Two non-homologous chromosomes exchanging segments, resulting in two chimeric chromosomes with swapped ends

facts.quizQuestion: Which cancer results from the Philadelphia chromosome — a reciprocal translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22 that creates the BCR-ABL fusion oncogene?
facts.explanation: The Philadelphia chromosome (Ph) is the hallmark of CML. The BCR-ABL fusion gene encodes a continuously active tyrosine kinase that drives uncontrolled white blood cell proliferation. The drug imatinib (Gleevec) specifically targets BCR-ABL, making CML a landmark success story in targeted cancer therapy.
facts.visualDescription: Chromosome 9 and chromosome 22 undergoing reciprocal translocation, producing the smaller Philadelphia chromosome (22q-) with BCR-ABL fusion

facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for the chi-square test used in genetics to evaluate whether observed offspring ratios differ significantly from expected Mendelian ratios?
facts.explanation: The chi-square formula quantifies how much observed genetic ratios deviate from expected Mendelian ratios. For each phenotypic class, compute (O−E)²/E and sum all classes. A large χ² value indicates the observed data is unlikely to have come from the expected ratio by chance alone.
facts.visualDescription: Chi-square formula displayed with a worked example: observed vs. expected columns for a 3:1 ratio test

facts.quizQuestion: How are degrees of freedom (df) calculated for a chi-square genetics test?
facts.explanation: Degrees of freedom for a genetics chi-square test = number of phenotypic classes − 1. For a monohybrid cross with 2 phenotypes, df = 1. For a dihybrid cross with 4 phenotypes, df = 3. The df value determines which critical value from the chi-square table to use.
facts.visualDescription: Chi-square table excerpt showing df rows 1-4 and critical values at p=0.05

facts.correctAnswer: Reject H₀ if χ² > critical value
acceptableAlternatives: If χ² > critical value at p=0.05, reject null hypothesis
facts.quizQuestion: How do you interpret a chi-square result in a genetics experiment — when do you reject the null hypothesis that observed ratios match expected Mendelian ratios?
facts.explanation: The null hypothesis in a genetics chi-square test states that any deviation from expected ratios is due to chance. If the calculated χ² exceeds the critical value (typically at p=0.05 for the given df), the probability that the difference is due to chance alone is less than 5% — so you reject the null hypothesis and conclude the ratio does not fit.
facts.visualDescription: Chi-square distribution curve showing the critical value at p=0.05, with 'reject null' region shaded in the right tail
distractors: Accept the null hypothesis if χ² exceeds the critical value
Reject the null hypothesis if χ² is less than the critical value
Accept the null if χ² equals the critical value
Use p=0.01 as the standard threshold in AP Biology
Reject the null if all observed values equal expected values
Accept the null hypothesis whenever χ² is positive

facts.quizQuestion: What are the three components of a DNA nucleotide?
facts.explanation: A DNA nucleotide consists of a deoxyribose sugar, a phosphate group, and one of four nitrogenous bases (A, T, G, or C). These units are covalently linked by phosphodiester bonds to form the sugar-phosphate backbone of each strand.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of a DNA nucleotide showing the pentagon deoxyribose sugar connected to a phosphate group and a nitrogenous base
distractors: ribose sugar, phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base
deoxyribose sugar, sulfate group, and a nitrogenous base
ribose sugar, phosphate group, and a lipid tail
deoxyribose sugar, phosphate group, and an amino acid
deoxyribose sugar, hydroxyl group, and a nitrogenous base
ribose sugar, sulfate group, and a nitrogenous base
glucose, phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base
deoxyribose sugar, phosphate group, and a fatty acid

facts.quizQuestion: What are the three components of an RNA nucleotide?
facts.explanation: RNA nucleotides contain a ribose sugar (with a 2'-OH group absent in DNA), a phosphate group, and one of four nitrogenous bases (A, U, G, or C). The extra hydroxyl on ribose makes RNA more chemically reactive and less stable than DNA.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram comparing RNA nucleotide with its ribose sugar to DNA's deoxyribose
distractors: deoxyribose sugar, phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base
ribose sugar, sulfate group, and a nitrogenous base
ribose sugar, phosphate group, and an amino acid
deoxyribose sugar, phosphate group, and a lipid tail
glucose, phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base
ribose sugar, hydroxyl group, and a nitrogenous base
fructose, phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base
ribose sugar, phosphate group, and a fatty acid

acceptableAlternatives: RNA uses uracil instead of thymine
facts.quizQuestion: Which base is found in RNA but NOT in DNA?
facts.explanation: RNA contains uracil (U) in place of thymine (T). Both bases pair with adenine, but uracil lacks the methyl group found on thymine. This substitution makes RNA cheaper to synthesize and is one of several structural differences between RNA and DNA.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side comparison of thymine and uracil molecular structures highlighting the missing methyl group

facts.quizQuestion: Which two nitrogenous bases are classified as purines?
facts.explanation: Purines (adenine and guanine) have a double-ring structure — a pyrimidine ring fused to an imidazole ring. Pyrimidines (cytosine, thymine, uracil) have only a single ring. In the double helix, a purine always pairs with a pyrimidine to maintain uniform width.
facts.visualDescription: Chemical structures of adenine and guanine showing their fused double-ring arrangement

acceptableAlternatives: A equals T and G equals C
facts.quizQuestion: What do Chargaff's rules state about base composition in double-stranded DNA?
facts.explanation: Erwin Chargaff found that in any double-stranded DNA sample, the amount of adenine equals thymine and the amount of guanine equals cytosine. This 1:1 ratio reflects Watson-Crick base pairing — A pairs with T via 2 hydrogen bonds, G pairs with C via 3 hydrogen bonds.
facts.visualDescription: Bar chart showing equal molar quantities of A=T and G=C in a double-stranded DNA molecule
distractors: All four bases are present in equal amounts
Purines equal pyrimidines only in RNA
G=T and A=C in single-stranded DNA
A equals U and G equals C in double-stranded DNA

facts.quizQuestion: Which scientists proposed the double-helix model of DNA structure in 1953?
facts.explanation: James Watson and Francis Crick proposed the double-helix model in 1953, relying critically on Rosalind Franklin's X-ray crystallography data (Photo 51) and Maurice Wilkins's work. Franklin's contribution was not credited in the original paper and she did not share the Nobel Prize awarded in 1962.
facts.visualDescription: Black-and-white portrait of Watson and Crick beside a model of the DNA double helix

facts.quizQuestion: Whose X-ray crystallography data (Photo 51) was critical to Watson and Crick's discovery of the DNA double helix?
facts.explanation: Rosalind Franklin's X-ray diffraction image known as Photo 51 revealed the helical structure and key dimensions of DNA. Watson and Crick used this data — without Franklin's knowledge — as a key piece of evidence for their model. Franklin died in 1958 before the Nobel Prize was awarded.
facts.visualDescription: Grainy X-ray diffraction image (Photo 51) showing the X-shaped pattern of the DNA double helix

facts.quizQuestion: How many hydrogen bonds form between adenine and thymine in the DNA double helix?
facts.explanation: Adenine and thymine form 2 hydrogen bonds, while guanine and cytosine form 3. This difference means G-C base pairs are slightly stronger. DNA with a higher G-C content has a higher melting temperature.
facts.visualDescription: Structural diagram showing A-T base pair with two dashed hydrogen bonds between them

facts.quizQuestion: How many hydrogen bonds form between guanine and cytosine in the DNA double helix?
facts.explanation: Guanine and cytosine form 3 hydrogen bonds, compared to 2 for A-T pairs. The extra hydrogen bond makes G-C pairs slightly more thermally stable. Regions of DNA rich in G-C require more energy (higher temperature) to separate the strands.
facts.visualDescription: Structural diagram showing G-C base pair with three dashed hydrogen bonds

acceptableAlternatives: messenger RNA, transfer RNA, ribosomal RNA
facts.quizQuestion: What are the three major types of RNA involved in gene expression?
facts.explanation: mRNA (messenger RNA) carries genetic instructions from DNA to the ribosome. tRNA (transfer RNA) delivers amino acids to the ribosome during translation. rRNA (ribosomal RNA) is a structural and catalytic component of ribosomes, including catalyzing peptide bond formation.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram showing the three RNA types in their roles during translation at a ribosome

facts.quizQuestion: How many possible codons (triplet combinations of the 4 RNA bases) exist?
facts.explanation: With 4 bases and 3 positions per codon, there are 4^3 = 64 possible codons. Of these, 61 encode amino acids and 3 are stop codons (UAA, UAG, UGA). The extra codons beyond the 20 amino acids explain why the genetic code is degenerate — most amino acids have multiple codons.
facts.visualDescription: 4×4×4 codon table showing all 64 possible triplet combinations

facts.quizQuestion: What model of DNA replication — demonstrated by Meselson and Stahl in 1958 — produces daughter molecules each containing one original strand and one new strand?
facts.explanation: In semiconservative replication, the two parental strands separate and each serves as a template for a new complementary strand. Meselson and Stahl proved this using 15N/14N density gradient centrifugation — after one generation, all DNA appeared at an intermediate density, ruling out conservative and dispersive models.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of the Meselson-Stahl experiment showing 15N heavy band shifting to an intermediate hybrid band after one round of replication

facts.quizQuestion: Which scientists demonstrated semiconservative DNA replication using 15N and 14N isotopes in a density gradient centrifugation experiment?
facts.explanation: In 1958, Matthew Meselson and Franklin Stahl grew E. coli in 15N medium (heavy nitrogen), then switched to 14N medium and sampled DNA after each generation. Cesium chloride density gradient centrifugation showed a hybrid-density band after one generation, proving the semiconservative model.
facts.visualDescription: Timeline diagram showing E. coli generations and shifting CsCl gradient bands from heavy to hybrid to light

facts.quizQuestion: Which enzyme unwinds the DNA double helix at the replication fork by breaking hydrogen bonds between base pairs?
facts.explanation: Helicase uses ATP energy to break the hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs and unwind the double helix, creating the replication fork. Single-strand binding proteins (SSBPs) then stabilize the separated strands to prevent re-annealing.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of helicase moving along the DNA double helix and separating the two strands at the replication fork

facts.quizQuestion: Which enzyme relieves the torsional stress (supercoiling) that builds up ahead of the replication fork as helicase unwinds DNA?
facts.explanation: As helicase unwinds the double helix, positive supercoils accumulate ahead of the fork. Topoisomerase cuts, rotates, and rejoins DNA strands to relieve this tension. Topoisomerase II makes double-strand cuts and is the target of certain antibiotics (fluoroquinolones) and anticancer drugs.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram showing supercoiled DNA ahead of the replication fork and topoisomerase cutting and rejoining strands to relax it

facts.quizQuestion: Which enzyme synthesizes the short RNA primer needed to start DNA replication, providing the 3'-OH group that DNA polymerase requires?
facts.explanation: Primase is a specialized RNA polymerase that synthesizes a short (~10 nucleotide) RNA primer complementary to the template strand. DNA polymerase cannot start a new strand from scratch — it can only extend an existing 3'-OH group, so primase must go first.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of primase laying down an RNA primer on a single-stranded DNA template at the replication fork

facts.quizQuestion: Which strand at the replication fork is synthesized discontinuously as a series of Okazaki fragments?
facts.explanation: The lagging strand runs antiparallel to the direction of fork movement, so DNA polymerase must synthesize it in short, discontinuous segments called Okazaki fragments (each starting with its own RNA primer). These fragments are later joined by DNA polymerase I (primer removal) and DNA ligase.
facts.visualDescription: Replication fork diagram labeling the leading strand (continuous) and lagging strand (Okazaki fragments) with arrows showing 5'→3' synthesis direction

facts.quizQuestion: What are the short, discontinuous DNA segments synthesized on the lagging strand during DNA replication called?
facts.explanation: Okazaki fragments are synthesized in the 5'→3' direction away from the replication fork on the lagging strand. In prokaryotes they are ~1,000-2,000 nucleotides long; in eukaryotes ~100-200 nucleotides. DNA polymerase I removes each RNA primer, fills the gap, and ligase seals the nicks.
facts.visualDescription: Lagging strand showing multiple short labeled Okazaki fragments each beginning with an RNA primer

facts.quizQuestion: Which enzyme seals the phosphodiester bond gaps between adjacent Okazaki fragments after primer removal?
facts.explanation: After DNA polymerase I removes the RNA primers and fills the gaps with DNA, small nicks remain in the sugar-phosphate backbone between adjacent fragments. DNA ligase seals these nicks by forming covalent phosphodiester bonds, creating a continuous lagging strand.
facts.visualDescription: Lagging strand diagram showing DNA ligase sealing the final nick between adjacent Okazaki fragments

facts.quizQuestion: Which DNA polymerase removes RNA primers from Okazaki fragments and replaces them with DNA?
facts.explanation: DNA polymerase I uses its 5'→3' exonuclease activity to remove each RNA primer while simultaneously synthesizing replacement DNA using the adjacent Okazaki fragment as a primer. This leaves only a nick that DNA ligase then seals.
facts.visualDescription: Close-up diagram of DNA polymerase I removing an RNA primer segment while laying down replacement DNA nucleotides

facts.quizQuestion: What are the repetitive non-coding DNA sequences at the ends of linear eukaryotic chromosomes that protect coding DNA from shortening during replication?
facts.explanation: Telomeres consist of hundreds to thousands of repeats of the sequence TTAGGG in humans. Because the lagging strand cannot be fully replicated at the chromosome end, a small amount of telomeric DNA is lost each replication cycle in somatic cells. Telomere shortening is linked to cellular aging.
facts.visualDescription: Chromosome diagram showing the telomere caps at both ends with the repeated TTAGGG sequence highlighted

facts.quizQuestion: Which enzyme extends telomeres using an RNA template, and is active in stem cells and cancer cells but inactive in most differentiated somatic cells?
facts.explanation: Telomerase is a reverse transcriptase that carries its own RNA template (complementary to the telomere repeat). It extends the 3' end of chromosomes, preventing telomere shortening. Cancer cells reactivate telomerase to achieve replicative immortality — making it a cancer therapy target.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of telomerase enzyme using its internal RNA template to add TTAGGG repeats to the chromosome end

facts.correctAnswer: Multiple origins of replication that fire simultaneously
facts.quizQuestion: Why can eukaryotes replicate their very large chromosomes in a reasonable time frame, unlike prokaryotes which have only one origin of replication?
facts.explanation: Eukaryotic chromosomes have thousands of origins of replication that are licensed and fired in a coordinated temporal program. Replication proceeds bidirectionally from each origin simultaneously. The resulting replication bubbles expand and merge until entire chromosomes are replicated.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of a eukaryotic chromosome with multiple replication bubbles expanding simultaneously
distractors: RNA-primed continuous synthesis on both strands
Rolling circle replication from one origin
Bidirectional replication from a single origin
Simultaneous replication of all chromosomes from a central organelle

facts.quizQuestion: What is the process of synthesizing an RNA molecule from a DNA template called?
facts.explanation: Transcription converts the information in a DNA sequence into an RNA sequence. In eukaryotes it occurs in the nucleus; in prokaryotes it occurs in the cytoplasm. RNA polymerase reads the template strand 3'→5' and synthesizes RNA 5'→3'.
facts.visualDescription: Central dogma diagram with an arrow labeled Transcription pointing from DNA to RNA

facts.quizQuestion: Which enzyme reads the DNA template strand and synthesizes the complementary mRNA during transcription?
facts.explanation: RNA polymerase binds the promoter with the help of transcription factors, reads the template strand in the 3'→5' direction, and synthesizes mRNA in the 5'→3' direction. Unlike DNA polymerase, RNA polymerase does not need a primer to initiate synthesis.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of RNA polymerase moving along a DNA template and synthesizing a growing mRNA strand

facts.quizQuestion: What is the specific DNA sequence upstream of a gene where RNA polymerase and transcription factors bind to initiate transcription?
facts.explanation: The promoter is located 5' (upstream) of the gene. In eukaryotes it often contains a TATA box ~25 bp upstream of the transcription start site. RNA polymerase cannot bind the promoter alone in eukaryotes — general transcription factors must assemble first.
facts.visualDescription: Gene map showing the promoter region upstream of the coding sequence with an arrow at the transcription start site

facts.quizQuestion: Which eukaryotic promoter element, with the consensus sequence TATAAA located ~25 bp upstream of the transcription start site, is recognized by TATA-binding protein (TBP)?
facts.explanation: The TATA box is one of the best-characterized core promoter elements in eukaryotes. TATA-binding protein (TBP), a component of the TFIID complex, binds the TATA box and bends the DNA, nucleating the assembly of the full pre-initiation complex including RNA polymerase II.
facts.visualDescription: Promoter region diagram marking the TATA box position 25 bp upstream and TBP protein binding to it

facts.quizQuestion: Which DNA strand is read 3'→5' by RNA polymerase to produce the mRNA transcript?
facts.explanation: The template strand (also called the antisense strand) is read by RNA polymerase in the 3'→5' direction. The resulting mRNA has the same sequence as the non-template (coding) strand, with U in place of T. Knowing which strand is the template is essential for predicting mRNA sequences.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of a double-stranded DNA segment with the template strand labeled below and its complementary mRNA above
distractors: The strand with the promoter sequence

facts.quizQuestion: What modified nucleotide is added to the 5' end of eukaryotic pre-mRNA to protect it from degradation and facilitate ribosome binding?
facts.explanation: The 5' cap is a 7-methylguanosine (m7G) nucleotide added in an unusual 5'-to-5' triphosphate linkage to the pre-mRNA immediately after transcription begins. It protects the mRNA from nuclease attack at its 5' end and is recognized by eukaryotic initiation factors to begin translation.
facts.visualDescription: mRNA diagram showing the m7G cap at the 5' end with the 5'→5' triphosphate linkage highlighted

facts.quizQuestion: What is added to the 3' end of eukaryotic pre-mRNA to protect it from degradation, aid nuclear export, and signal mRNA stability?
facts.explanation: Poly-A polymerase adds approximately 200 adenine nucleotides to the 3' end of pre-mRNA after the cleavage of the primary transcript at the polyadenylation signal (AAUAAA). The poly-A tail protects mRNA from 3' exonucleases, facilitates export from the nucleus, and is recognized by translation factors.
facts.visualDescription: mRNA diagram showing the poly-A tail (string of A's) at the 3' end with poly-A polymerase indicated
distractors: A Shine-Dalgarno sequence at the 3' end
A stop codon-like sequence at the 3' end
A tRNA acceptor stem at the 3' end
~200 thymine nucleotides added to the 3' end

facts.quizQuestion: What is the process called in which introns are removed and exons are joined together to produce mature mRNA?
facts.explanation: RNA splicing occurs in the nucleus of eukaryotes after transcription. The spliceosome — a complex of snRNPs — recognizes splice sites at intron-exon boundaries, excises the intron as a lariat-shaped intermediate, and ligates the flanking exons together.
facts.visualDescription: Pre-mRNA diagram showing introns (non-coding regions) being looped out and cut while exons are spliced together

facts.quizQuestion: What large ribonucleoprotein complex composed of snRNPs catalyzes the removal of introns from eukaryotic pre-mRNA?
facts.explanation: The spliceosome is one of the largest molecular machines in the cell, assembled from five snRNPs (U1, U2, U4, U5, U6) and many associated proteins. It recognizes conserved sequences at the 5' and 3' splice sites and the branch point, catalyzes two transesterification reactions to excise the intron and join exons.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of the spliceosome complex assembled around the pre-mRNA intron, forming a lariat intermediate

facts.quizQuestion: What process allows a single gene to produce multiple different protein variants by joining different combinations of exons from the same pre-mRNA?
facts.explanation: Alternative splicing greatly expands proteome diversity. Humans have ~20,000 protein-coding genes but can produce more than 100,000 distinct protein variants. The spliceosome selects different exon combinations in response to cell-type-specific splicing factors, producing functionally distinct protein isoforms.
facts.visualDescription: Pre-mRNA diagram showing different exon combinations selected in two different cell types to produce two distinct mature mRNAs

facts.correctAnswer: No 5' cap, no poly-A tail, no splicing; translation begins during transcription
acceptableAlternatives: coupled transcription-translation; no cap, tail, or splicing
facts.quizQuestion: How does prokaryotic transcription differ from eukaryotic transcription in terms of RNA processing and coupling with translation?
facts.explanation: Prokaryotes lack a nuclear membrane, so ribosomes can begin translating the 5' end of an mRNA before transcription of the 3' end is complete — a process called coupled transcription-translation. Prokaryotic mRNA is also polycistronic (encodes multiple proteins) and requires no 5' cap, poly-A tail, or splicing.
facts.visualDescription: Split diagram: eukaryote showing nucleus with processing steps vs. prokaryote showing ribosomes attaching to mRNA as it is still being transcribed
distractors: Prokaryotes add a 5' cap but no poly-A tail
Prokaryotes require splicing but no capping
Prokaryotes use RNA polymerase II like eukaryotes
Prokaryotes add a poly-A tail but no 5' cap
Prokaryotic translation occurs only after transcription is complete
Prokaryotes have introns that must be spliced

facts.quizQuestion: What is the process of synthesizing a polypeptide from an mRNA template at the ribosome called?
facts.explanation: Translation is the second step of the central dogma (DNA → RNA → Protein). Ribosomes read the mRNA codon by codon, and tRNAs deliver the corresponding amino acids. The ribosome catalyzes peptide bond formation between successive amino acids to build the polypeptide chain.
facts.visualDescription: Ribosome diagram with mRNA threading through it and a growing polypeptide chain emerging from the exit tunnel

facts.quizQuestion: What is the universal start codon that signals the beginning of translation and sets the reading frame?
facts.explanation: AUG encodes methionine (Met) and is the canonical start codon in nearly all organisms. In bacteria, the initiator tRNA carries formyl-methionine (fMet). AUG establishes the reading frame — every subsequent codon is read as a non-overlapping triplet from this point forward.
facts.visualDescription: mRNA diagram with the AUG start codon highlighted and the initiator Met-tRNA positioned at the P site of the ribosome

facts.quizQuestion: What are the three stop codons that signal termination of translation?
facts.explanation: UAA (ochre), UAG (amber), and UGA (opal/umber) are the three stop codons. No tRNA recognizes them; instead, release factors (RF1/RF2 in prokaryotes, eRF1 in eukaryotes) bind the A site and trigger hydrolysis of the polypeptide from the final tRNA.
facts.visualDescription: Codon table with UAA, UAG, and UGA highlighted in red as stop codons

facts.quizQuestion: Which ribosomal site receives the incoming aminoacyl-tRNA carrying the next amino acid during translation elongation?
facts.explanation: The ribosome has three tRNA-binding sites: A (aminoacyl), P (peptidyl), and E (exit). During elongation, the aminoacyl-tRNA carrying the next amino acid enters the A site, where codon-anticodon matching occurs. Peptide bond formation then transfers the growing chain from the P-site tRNA to the A-site amino acid.
facts.visualDescription: Ribosome cross-section labeling the A, P, and E sites with aminoacyl-tRNA entering the A site

facts.quizQuestion: Which ribosomal site holds the tRNA attached to the growing polypeptide chain during translation?
facts.explanation: The P site holds the peptidyl-tRNA — the tRNA covalently linked to the nascent polypeptide. When a new amino acid arrives at the A site, peptidyl transferase (the catalytic rRNA of the large subunit) transfers the polypeptide from the P-site tRNA to the A-site amino acid, extending the chain by one residue.
facts.visualDescription: Ribosome diagram with the P site highlighted and a tRNA carrying the growing polypeptide chain labeled inside it

facts.quizQuestion: What are the two functionally critical parts of a tRNA molecule: one that reads the mRNA codon and one that carries the amino acid?
facts.explanation: tRNA folds into a cloverleaf secondary structure. The anticodon loop contains 3 bases that are complementary to a specific mRNA codon, enabling codon-anticodon base pairing. The 3' CCA terminus (universal to all tRNAs) is where aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase attaches the cognate amino acid.
facts.visualDescription: Cloverleaf tRNA diagram with the anticodon loop at the bottom and the 3'-CCA amino acid attachment site at the top
distractors: Variable loop and acceptor stem only

facts.quizQuestion: Which enzyme attaches the correct amino acid to its corresponding tRNA, using ATP energy — ensuring accurate translation of the genetic code?
facts.explanation: Each of the 20 aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases recognizes one specific amino acid and all the tRNAs that carry it. The enzyme catalyzes a two-step reaction: first activating the amino acid with ATP (forming an aminoacyl-AMP intermediate), then transferring it to the 3'-OH of the correct tRNA. This 'second genetic code' step is where translational accuracy is primarily set.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram of aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase binding its cognate amino acid and tRNA to form a charged aminoacyl-tRNA

facts.quizQuestion: What catalyzes the formation of peptide bonds between amino acids during translation, and what type of catalyst is it?
facts.explanation: Peptide bond formation is catalyzed by the peptidyl transferase center of the large ribosomal subunit. Crucially, the catalyst is the 23S rRNA (in prokaryotes) or 28S rRNA (in eukaryotes) — not a protein. This makes the ribosome a ribozyme, supporting the RNA World hypothesis.
facts.visualDescription: Close-up of the ribosome peptidyl transferase center showing rRNA directly catalyzing the peptide bond between two amino acids

acceptableAlternatives: release factor triggers termination at a stop codon
facts.quizQuestion: How does translation termination occur when a stop codon enters the ribosomal A site?
facts.explanation: No aminoacyl-tRNA matches a stop codon. Instead, a protein called a release factor (RF1 or RF2 in prokaryotes; eRF1 in eukaryotes) recognizes the stop codon and enters the A site. It stimulates peptidyl transferase to hydrolyze the bond between the polypeptide and the P-site tRNA, releasing the finished protein.
facts.visualDescription: Ribosome diagram with a stop codon at the A site, release factor occupying the A site, and the polypeptide being released
distractors: A tRNA with an anticodon complementary to the stop codon arrives
The ribosome runs off the end of the mRNA
The 5' cap is removed, destabilizing the mRNA
A ribozyme directly cleaves the polypeptide chain
GTPase activity of EF-G releases the chain
The spliceosome signals the ribosome to stop

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name for the structure formed when multiple ribosomes simultaneously translate the same mRNA, increasing protein production efficiency?
facts.explanation: After the first ribosome moves away from the start codon, a second ribosome can attach and begin translating the same mRNA. A single mRNA molecule can be simultaneously translated by dozens of ribosomes, forming a polysome or polyribosome. This dramatically amplifies protein output.
facts.visualDescription: Electron micrograph-style illustration of a string of ribosomes (polysome) threaded along a single mRNA molecule, each producing a growing polypeptide

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the flexible base-pairing at the 3rd codon position that allows some tRNAs to recognize multiple codons encoding the same amino acid?
facts.explanation: The wobble hypothesis (Francis Crick, 1966) explains why the ~45 tRNA species in a cell can decode all 61 sense codons. Non-Watson-Crick base pairs are tolerated at the 3rd codon position (wobble position), so one tRNA anticodon can pair with multiple synonymous codons differing only at that position.
facts.visualDescription: Diagram showing a single tRNA anticodon pairing with two different codons via wobble at the third position

facts.quizQuestion: What property of the genetic code means that nearly all organisms — from bacteria to humans — use the same codon-amino acid assignments?
facts.explanation: The near-universality of the genetic code is strong evidence for common ancestry of all life on Earth. AUG means methionine in E. coli, yeast, plants, and humans. This property allows genes from one organism to be expressed in another — the basis of recombinant DNA technology.
facts.visualDescription: Globe graphic with organisms from different kingdoms (bacterium, plant, animal) all sharing the same codon table
distractors: The genetic code is redundant only in eukaryotes
The genetic code is triplet in bacteria but doublet in eukaryotes
The genetic code is tissue-specific in mammals

facts.quizQuestion: What category of events — including protein folding by chaperones, phosphorylation, glycosylation, and signal peptide cleavage — occurs after translation to produce a functional protein?
facts.explanation: After the ribosome releases a polypeptide, it must fold into its correct 3D structure (often assisted by chaperone proteins). Additional modifications include: phosphorylation (adds –PO4 groups, often regulates activity), glycosylation (adds sugar chains, important for cell recognition), and ubiquitination (tags proteins for proteasomal degradation).
facts.visualDescription: Protein processing diagram showing a newly synthesized polypeptide going through folding, phosphorylation, and glycosylation steps
